## 2020-03-03

**Sean Davis** (22:00:07):
> [https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2020.02.02.930578v2](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2020.02.02.930578v2)

**Aaron Lun** (22:41:25):
> Well, I guess scran doesn't do too badly.

**Aaron Lun** (22:41:49):
> I mean, Figure 11 would be easier to implement if Seurat just used a S(C)E object like the rest of us.

**Aaron Lun** (22:42:14):
> Or at least just an SE object.***sigh***

**Aaron Lun** (23:02:36):
> The final recommendations are interesting because I've been trying to go in the opposite direction w.r.t. the complexity of the analysis; just cutting out any steps that don't provide an clear benefit. I've managed to boil down the essential bits to:
> * QC, using the MAD approach. Could also use hard thresholds but I don't want to have to think.
> * Log-normalize with the library size. Known problems but it won't do anything misleading.
> * HVGs, and just take X genes. Just iterate until you get something you can work with.
> * PCA, and just take X PCs. Again, just iterate.
> * Clustering, with whatever algorithm one might like to use. Again, just iterate.
> I would hack off HVGs if I could, but I need it to run the PCA in reasonable time.

**Aaron Lun** (23:03:42):
> DEFINITELY NO IMPUTATION.


## 2020-03-04

**Aaron Lun** (00:21:02):
> I will say, though, their conflict of interest statement is cute.

**Aaron Lun** (00:22:11):
> If that's the standard, I can't think of a single paper that I've written where I wasn't conflicted.

**Hervé Pagès** (00:28:46):
> So all your papers are benchmarking studies where you have a horse in the race?

**Aaron Lun** (00:29:54):
> Where all the other horses have been drugged or otherwise incapacitated.

**Aaron Lun** (00:31:00):
> The reviewers get what the reviewers ask for!:slightly_smiling_face:

**Aaron Lun** (00:32:48):
> > The authors should show that their method performs better than other methods.
> As a reviewer, I've never gotten a response that says, "Actually, our method doesn't perform as well as other methods, so we decided not to proceed with publishing. Thank you for pointing this out." Which leads to the interesting conclusion that every method is the best method.

**Aaron Lun** (00:33:56):
> Which is also why I don't review papers any more, thus giving me more time to watch Halo 5 killstreak videos. About as productive, but more enjoyable.

**Aaron Lun** (00:49:39) (in thread):
> Certainly, ever since I decided not to review papers, I've been feeling a lot better.

**Hervé Pagès** (00:54:33) (in thread):
> If you change your mind and miss reviewing stuff, let us know and we'll assign you some package submissions:wink:

**Aaron Lun** (00:58:16) (in thread):
> Well, at least there I know that everyone's doing it for free. What's particularly galling about academic publishing is that someone else is making all the money while I'm doing all the work.

**Aaron Lun** (00:59:51) (in thread):
> My aunt is a journalist and she was shocked that we wrote the content AND paid for it to be published. She said "you know, Aaron, usually it's the other way around". Then she made a comment about how a bunch of smart people could come up with such a stupid system. I didn't have much I could say back.

**Aaron Lun** (01:00:25) (in thread):
> Forgot the air quotes she put around "smart".

**Jared Andrews** (10:37:38) (in thread):
> Kill paper publications, force everyone to publish as Rmd/jupyter vignettes showing how all data is analyzed and figures made (outside actual experimental figures), and bam, costs are cut and you just have to pay to host a webpage. Would get some semblance of code review, increase reproducibility, and make formatting less annoying (except references). 
> 
> And no, I foresee no obvious flaws with said approach at all, it is completely foolproof.

**ZedaoLiu** (01:58:53):
> @ZedaoLiu has joined the channel

**Hervé Pagès** (02:51:16):
> Wow Julia is indeed very fast!
> R:
> ```
> > system.time(for (i in 1:150e6) { if (i [| i ](= 5 ) 150e6 - 5) cat(i, "\n") })
> 1 
> 2 
> 3 
> 4 
> 5 
> 149999996 
> 149999997 
> 149999998 
> 149999999 
> 150000000 
>    user  system elapsed 
>   5.254   0.000   5.254
> ```
> Python:
> ```
> >>> def dumbloop(niter):
> ...     start = time.time()
> ...     for i in range(0, niter):
> ...         if i < 5 or i >= niter - 5:
> ...             print(i)
> ...     end = time.time()
> ...     print("ellapsed: ", end - start)
> ... 
> >>> dumbloop(int(150e6))
> 0
> 1
> 2
> 3
> 4
> 149999995
> 149999996
> 149999997
> 149999998
> 149999999
> ellapsed:  10.339980602264404
> ```
> Julia:
> ```
> julia> @time for i=1:150000000 if i [| i ](= 5 ) 150000000 - 5 println(i) end end
> 1
> 2
> 3
> 4
> 5
> 149999996
> 149999997
> 149999998
> 149999999
> 150000000
>   0.180177 seconds (86 allocations: 2.438 KiB)
> ```
> Another big surprise (at least to me) is that this loop is 2x faster in R than in Python!

**Aaron Lun** (03:03:52):
> I blame the printing of zero.

**Aaron Lun** (03:04:27):
> you have to reach all the way to the other end of the keyboard!

> For this Community Call, we’re trying something different. We’ll start with a short talk by Julia Silge, then spend most of the time on Q & A with four panelists - Elin Waring, Erin Grand, Leonardo Collado-Torres, and Scott Chamberlain - moderated by Julia. Our panelists bring a wide range of perspectives so there’s something for everyone. Collectively, they have experience developing and maintaining passion-project packages, very popular packages, too many packages on CRAN, packages on Bioconductor, and taking over maintenance (and changing things!

**Sean Davis** (12:57:52):
> A few of us are collaborating on a#covid19package for accessing, exploring, and analyzing COVID-19 datasets. There are some low-hanging contributions to be made for those interested. For folks tasked with developing educational materials around data science and data exploration, the package offers timely and up-to-date resources.

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (13:06:12):
> I’ve put this together myself:[https://kevinrue.github.io/COVID-19-website/](https://kevinrue.github.io/COVID-19-website/)A bit of educational material here:[https://kevinrue.github.io/post/creating-a-dynamic-rmarkdown-website-for-covid-19-data/](https://kevinrue.github.io/post/creating-a-dynamic-rmarkdown-website-for-covid-19-data/)I update the website daily, automatically pulling the latest data from JHU (`git submodule`), but development of the website itself is extremely irregular and low on my priority list.
  - Attachment (Kevin Rue-Albrecht): [Creating a dynamic Rmarkdown website for COVID-19 data | Kevin Rue-Albrecht](https://kevinrue.github.io/post/creating-a-dynamic-rmarkdown-website-for-covid-19-data/)
> Overview This post refers to code available online as a GitHub repository. Create a new RStudio project Open the RStudio “New Project…” menu -> New Directory -> Simple R Markdown Website -> Type a folder name. Initialize git You may need to install.packages("git2r") first. git2r::init() Also, restart RStudio to apply the changes and benefit of the “Git” tab. Make a new GitHub repository Click on “New repository”.

## 2020-04-13

**Zinian Wang** (15:12:58):
> @Zinian Wang has joined the channel

**Lambda Moses** (20:15:46):
> As I’m working on a review paper on spatial transcriptomics, I realized that MATLAB is still well and alive among biologists, and often the MATLAB repos used for image processing are not well-documented. I’ve only used MATLAB for math and electrical engineering classes, not in research, so I’m not very good at it, but I guess it’s really good for image analysis, which explains why it’s still commonly used for image analysis though there are OpenCV and scikit image. Perhaps it’s the legacy of CellProfiler and friends, which are written in MATLAB. We useRs tend to support open source and reproducible research. What are your opinions about MATLAB? Anyone does not have a MATLAB license provided by their institution or lab? Should MATLAB be replaced by open source alternatives?
> 
> Also, I remember back in rstudio::conf this year, the keynote about RStudio becoming a public benefit corporation mentioned that an advantage of open source is that the availability of the software is not dependent on the existence or permission of a corporation. Now I sort of wonder what if MathWorks goes bankrupt. I mean I think MATLAB is awesome, except for its price tag.

**Aaron Lun** (20:16:13):
> What’s MATLAB?

**Lambda Moses** (20:16:36):
> [https://www.mathworks.com/products/matlab.html](https://www.mathworks.com/products/matlab.html)
  - Attachment (mathworks.com): [MATLAB - MathWorks](https://www.mathworks.com/products/matlab.html)
> The official home of MATLAB software. MATLAB is the easiest and most productive software environment for engineers and scientists. Try, buy, and learn MATLAB.

**Aaron Lun** (20:16:55):
> Yeah, I know, I’m just messing with you.

**Aaron Lun** (20:18:18):
> Only had a brief opportunity to use it once, when fiddling with some spatial transcriptomics data back in 2017. Gave up pretty quickly trying to figure out other people’s scripts - boy, if you think R scripts written by non-computational people are bad, you should have a look at the matlab pile that gets emitted - and also because the CZI’s starfish pipeline development was in full swing, so I just thought I’d use that instead.

**Lambda Moses** (20:18:46):
> Yeah, I’m really bothered by the poor documentation of many such repos for spatial transcriptomics

**Lambda Moses** (20:18:54):
> Or no documentation at all

**Dan Bunis** (20:23:46):
> I don’t have matlab from my school.  Not sure how common that is, but I’d assume highly common for non-engineering biological sciences.  FWIW, R and Matlab are super similar — to the point where I jumped in to R with basically no need for teaching after having learned Matlab 8 or so years prior in undergrad.

**Dan Bunis** (20:27:28):
> Idk if that perspective helps you for your review at all:man-shrugging:. I sense that you may be considering suggesting they switch from Matlab to R, but maybe you are just contemplating leaving a note about the reduced transparency for Matlab methods?

**Lambda Moses** (20:29:30):
> It’s not for the review; I’m just curious what useRs think of MATLAB.

**Aaron Lun** (20:32:11):
> I think of MATLAB in much the same way that I think of the SF Giants. I know that they’re there and I know vaguely what they’re about (there’s a ball in there somewhere, right?) but I don’t really know or care what they do.

**Dan Bunis** (20:34:57):
> Matlab was my first language, because it’s what Cornell taught (might even still) to biological engineers a decade ago.  But R is great and free while Matlab is very expensive, so I don’t see a reason to go back.


## 2020-04-14

**Alan O'C** (06:20:05):
> I've seen Matlab code supplied with a couple of papers but I find it a horrendous experience to write, and yeah it tends to have the same "software written by someone who doesn't understand software" vibes as a lot of R code, for better or worse. I'd not use it unless there was a specific thing I needed to use, and even then I'd re-implement in another language because I don't particularly want to have to fight to get a £1,000 pound licence for my computer(s)

**Alan O'C** (06:20:38):
> Although a colleague uses it so I should probably not smack talk it too much:innocent:

**Lukas Weber** (11:29:04):
> I think there are some good image processing libraries in Matlab, which are better than what is available in other languages. And it is also widely used in engineering disciplines. But yeah apart from that, I would also strongly recommend people switch from Matlab to either R or Python, mainly because Matlab is very expensive, and there is no easy way to distribute code/packages

**Federico Marini** (13:13:33):
> Signal processing is also quite strong in there - they have those toolboxes which are in my understanding like nicely curated collection of extensions/“packages” (in the R sense)

**FelixErnst** (14:29:16):
> Good as in entertaining: I caught up on the reacher series. Good as in something to think about: Roadside Picnic, good as in my happy place: Final watch, new watch and the sixth watch

**Aaron Lun** (14:29:52):
> big fan of iain bank’s culture series.

**FelixErnst** (14:32:49):
> Do you recommend to start at the beginning? Or can I start anywhere?

**Aaron Lun** (14:34:58):
> I started at player of games, and it was fine. Some of them (e.g., inversions) won’t make as much sense without the context.

**Aaron Lun** (14:35:29):
> My favorites are player of games and use of weapons, but the other ones are a good read as well.

**Aaron Lun** (14:35:40):
> Enough of a good read that I almost forgot to do my daily commit.

**FelixErnst** (14:36:48):
> Thx!

**Jared Andrews** (14:45:42):
> Reacher is good, kinda trash, but entertaining. I've heard of Iain Bank, will take a look.

**Jared Andrews** (14:46:03):
> Well, not trash, but easy reads, for sure.

**FelixErnst** (14:49:16):
> Definitely

**FelixErnst** (14:49:40):
> Fair accessment:slightly_smiling_face:

**FelixErnst** (14:49:53):
> The last three books I mentioned are from Sergei Lukyanenkos Night watch series. Thats basically middle ground, it starts as good vs. evil and than by the third book (you have to read the second but thats hard to stay focused on) it descends into grays. Thats when things get really good. and third to six get better by each book. Haven't come across anything similar.

**Jared Andrews** (14:50:57):
> Perfect, will add that to the list.

**Ludwig Geistlinger** (20:20:18):
> Release of R-4.0.0 is scheduled for Friday 2020-04-24, ie it’s not yet released. You can however find pre-release versions here:[https://cran.r-project.org/src/base-prerelease/](https://cran.r-project.org/src/base-prerelease/)

**Stephanie Hicks** (21:03:24):
> Gave a talk at a local R conference in 2018 with an intro to Bioconductor and highlighted plyranges from@Stuart Lee@Di Cook@Michael Lawrence. Found out today there is a video of it! Sharing here in case it’s useful to anyone.[https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=l1MQ7x8cn7Y](https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=l1MQ7x8cn7Y)
  - Attachment (YouTube): [Analyzing Genomics Data in R with Bioconductor](https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=l1MQ7x8cn7Y)

**Charlotte Soneson** (04:31:16):
> Don’t forget to nominate (one or several) people that you think deserve recognition for the Bioconductor Awards![https://community-bioc.slack.com/archives/C34NC134G/p1587145591074800](https://community-bioc.slack.com/archives/C34NC134G/p1587145591074800)
  - Attachment: [Attachment](https://community-bioc.slack.com/archives/C34NC134G/p1587145591074800)
> Bioconductor is excited to announce an opportunity to recognize those making significant outstanding contributions to the Bioconductor community. The Bioconductor project proudly launches the first call for Bioconductor Awards, honoring various forms of contributions to the project. Four awardees will be selected, each having contributed to the project in an outstanding way based on one or more of the criteria found [here](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1ccXPFc_bGZoW3DgfgXf13_4i5TXySvXQCdRR8UJr__I/edit)
> Please fill out the nomination form by June 15 : [Nomination Form](https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSdaI6KHsezSQLWcCAzRCLlOz_fJai59PcyIz03ifutbwmXaVw/viewform)

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (09:14:00):
> Discover new (and old) packages every day:[https://kevinrue.github.io/BiocRoulette/](https://kevinrue.github.io/BiocRoulette/):grin:Accompanied by a brief blog post to  illustrate the use of `BiocPkgTools` and GitHub actions:[https://kevinrue.github.io/post/biocpkgtools/](https://kevinrue.github.io/post/biocpkgtools/)
  - Attachment (Kevin Rue-Albrecht): [BiocPkgTools | Kevin Rue-Albrecht](https://kevinrue.github.io/post/biocpkgtools/)
> Motivation There are currently 5,621, broken down as follows: Repository Packages AnnotationData 2,649 ExperimentData 809 Software 2,163 In an effort to motivate myself to keep an eye out for interesting packages - both new and old - I have used the BiocPkgTools package to develop a website updated daily and feature packages selected randomly from each repository.

> @Fredrik Landfors has joined the channel

> @Hanjie Shen has joined the channel

> Hello everyone, I would like to exploit this platform to recommend the upcoming series of webinars aimed primarily at people who are starting their journey in bioinformatics - for trainees or wet-lab-turned-computational people it might be a really good crash course in some basic computational literacy with a strong biological flavour - I know I would have liked to have something like this when I first switched pipette for keyboard:slightly_smiling_face:[https://twitter.com/NGSchoolEU/status/1265584178869030916](https://twitter.com/NGSchoolEU/status/1265584178869030916)

## 2020-06-12

**Stavros Papadopoulos** (11:40:41):
> @Stavros Papadopoulos has joined the channel

**Nick Borcherding** (12:35:14):
> @Nick Borcherding has joined the channel

**Dan Bunis** (13:07:07):
> cross-posting because I’m not sure how many of us are currently part of the#diversebiocchannel.
  - Attachment: [Attachment](https://community-bioc.slack.com/archives/CL21357E2/p1591981171004500?thread_ts=1591981171004500&cid=CL21357E2)
> Although pride parades are cancelled, and social focus is largely shifted elsewhere* this year, it is still pride month.
> 
> I’m wondering if anyone else in the group has any thoughts on this newer iteration of the general pride flag. Particularly, as a white cis gay male myself, I’d like to hear others’ thoughts on this flag iteration.
> 
> Rather than giving my own words as to why I personally support this flag, I’d like to share its creator’s:
> 
> > Their artist statement:
> > I am so excited that my art is getting some traction. I created this in 2019 as a way to spread the message that we need to center Trans people, People of color, and specifically Trans People of Color in the queer community as a whole. There are no lines separating us as we are one big community that blends together and needs intersectional support. I am very excited about the T-shirts, Hoodies, Tanktops and now Flags that we have to spread this art and this message. As a Brown Queer Disabled Transman. This has helped me feel more supported in the community as a whole. anyways here is my art if you want to spread the word! I am a Portlander and would love the support of my local community with this message.
> (full credit text in link in the thread. Please do not share without it.)
> 
> I’m specifically considering making a square version of the flag into an emoji, and then adding it as my status icon for the rest of the June. I would love to see an open discussion on this, and it would also be wonderful to see how diverse of a community we have here, but DMs (confidentiality assured) are also welcome!
> 
> *rightfully so. #blacklivesmatter, and the LGBT community must proclaim and actively support that because #blacktranslivesmatter, and because of so many other reasons too!

**Aaron Lun** (13:16:39):
> I saw this and thought you were going to make a rainbow Bioconductor sticker

**Aaron Lun** (13:17:55):
> Problem is the dots are too small to easily see the colors, but with some pastel and imagination it can be done.

**Paul Hoffman** (13:30:25):
> @Paul Hoffman has joined the channel

**Dan Bunis** (13:48:23):
> oh something like that would be awesome too!  It would be pretty cool to have a BioC-LGBT community sticker. Perhaps just the standard flag colors (eg:pride:) with the BioC name and logo on top with a white border around each piece. I think that could work on top of most of the standard flags.

**Hervé Pagès** (14:04:20):
> and with a biologically correct double helix:pray:

**Aaron Lun** (15:14:41):

**Aaron Lun** (15:16:05):
> At any rate, I fixed the asymmetry that I couldn't be bothered to deal with earlier, so here is the final version.
  - File (PNG): [rainbow_BioC.png](https://community-bioc.slack.com/files/U34P8RS3B/F015CKMVBHQ/rainbow_bioc.png)

**Martin Morgan** (15:38:16) (in thread):
> @Aaron Lunthe only definitive files available are those on the linked page, so one would have to generate pdf / svg from those.

**Martin Morgan** (15:48:28):
> Any takers for producing the BLM version?

**Dan Bunis** (15:58:34):
> Can we pause on this a bit? I might be the only queer voice here right now. I'd like to thank@Aaron Lunfor making this sticker design. But I'd also like to note that after my initial affirmative response to the pride flag idea, I've become a bit conflicted re: performative actions versus substantive support of diversity.
> 
> I'd like to propose that to go alongside the inclusion of stickers for the LGBT community, and perhaps one specifically for the BIPOC community (if we have members of that community that would support such a sticker), can I ask that we send out some sort of letter of support for BIPOC and LGBT individuals in the sciences?
> 
> Bioconductor itself may not be in a place to create rules that would make it be easier to be different in science, but maybe we can still use our collective voice to support that such measures be implemented elsewhere?

**Dan Bunis** (16:12:13) (in thread):
> My idea for each is to take the colors as they are in the original flag I posted.  Blue pink white pink blue = Trans.  The black -> brown skin tones part is representative of queer POC.  I think such colors could be used for the wider community, but I don't truly know.

## 2020-06-15

**Nicolas Valentin** (09:00:06):
> @Nicolas Valentin has joined the channel

**Aaron Lun** (11:27:52):
> Anyone have any recommendations for apps that call international landlines?

**Robert Castelo** (11:34:34):
> i’ve used[https://internetcalls.com](https://internetcalls.com)but from a desktop computer, i don’t know whether it works from a mobile phone. once you enter your phone number and the phone you want to call, it works by calling your phone first, and once you pick it up, then it calls the other phone. my experience is that the quality of the call was quite good.
  - Attachment (internetcalls.com): [Internetcalls gets you the cheapest international calls of the internet](https://internetcalls.com)
> Free phone calls all around the world: Download the free Internetcalls application and save loads of money on your (inter)national calling charges right away!

**Aaron Lun** (11:36:06):
> interesting. I was going to to buy skype credits but I’ll have a look at this.

**Kasper D. Hansen** (12:04:53):
> I use skype for this

**Aaron Lun** (12:16:44):
> Hm.

**Lukas Weber** (12:21:41):
> I used to use Skype credits for this too, although it's probably been a year or two since I've used it (we all switched to free calls on mobile with whatsapp and facetime)

**Aaron Lun** (12:22:30):
> If only HMRC was so obliging.

**Aaron Lun** (12:22:55):
> Okay, I’ll give skype a go. Hopefully they don’t put me on hold for hours and burn up all my credits.

**Lukas Weber** (12:23:20):
> it's really cheap, like 2c per minute to Aus

**Aaron Lun** (12:23:57):
> Yes, I would have to be on hold for 3 hours to use of $5.

**Robert Castelo** (12:36:51):
> If think internetcalls is 5c per call, irrespective of how long the call is, during the first 3 months after you put credit in your account.

**Aaron Lun** (12:54:05):
> tell me about it.

**Jayaram Kancherla** (13:43:46):
> if you have credit in google voice, you can also make international calls to landlines/mobiles

**Dario Strbenac** (22:00:09):
> Is there a website I could find the coordinates of the hg38 alternate contigs in reference to the main chromosomes? I aim to calculate average read coverage, but there don't seem to be tools for that which support alt-aware mapping, so calculating read coverage across the chromosome segments where there are no alternate contigs beside them seems like the next best thing. Previously a similar question asked at Biostars[https://www.biostars.org/p/442997/](https://www.biostars.org/p/442997/)but unanswered.

**Vivek Das** (14:27:17):
> I have a query#random. How many are using google Colab for EDA? Any thoughts?I have been using it for some curiosity driven gigs so far and finding it very useful. Let’s see how well I can sustain for the free usage untill I receive e-mail for the premium upgrade.

**Vivek Das** (11:45:23):
> #random: I wanted to make sure US faculties/PI/advisors here have seen this. It’s a pretty problematic environment currently for international students as per my understanding. Hope international students get all the needed support in US.[https://twitter.com/siointerrupted/status/1280350792294076421?s=21](https://twitter.com/siointerrupted/status/1280350792294076421?s=21)
  - Attachment (twitter): [Attachment](https://twitter.com/siointerrupted/status/1280350792294076421?s=21)
> CALL TO ACTION: I have created a small document detailing the current situation and what you can do to help. You can find it here. Your international students need you NOW! [https://docs.google.com/document/d/1237MqzCVFgSjB15PKk8hCfF4ZWhrwbcWe0P4rgQkONE/edit?usp=sharing](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1237MqzCVFgSjB15PKk8hCfF4ZWhrwbcWe0P4rgQkONE/edit?usp=sharing)

**Vivek Das** (11:47:22):
> Apologies to post it here but I don’t have any other means to reach out to as many faculties I can other than Twitter and this medium. Thanks and appreciate who all are taking the steps and doing the needful.

**Dario Strbenac** (22:00:16):
> It is fortuitous that BioC Asia 2020 will be online rather than in Beijing. I can only imagine Aaron Lun arriving with a suitcase containing freshly-printed rainbow flag hex stickers. Weeks later, we would wonder what the streak of grey (rounded corners, of course) rectangles on LTLA's GitHub activity summary meant.
  - File (PNG): [Arbitrary Police Arrest](https://community-bioc.slack.com/files/U34P8RS3B/F0172TFFT89/detention.png)

**Luke Zappia** (04:57:49):
> While GitHub is updating their UI something that would actually be useful is file type icons like GitLab seems to have
  - File (PNG): [image.png](https://community-bioc.slack.com/files/U34P8RS3B/F01720K22SY/image.png)

**Dario Strbenac** (08:30:17):
> An algorithm was just published in**Nature Genetics**on Monday that applies a t-test to FPKM values of RNA-seq data to look for outliers! They also multiply a Binomial probability function by a Gaussian function because the distribution of their experimental data didn't follow the null distribution and they wanted it to look more like the null distribution. Plenty more amusement in there for anyone who barely passed undergraduate statistics or did better[https://www.nature.com/articles/s41588-020-0659-5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41588-020-0659-5)Aaron would be even more offended by this Methods section than the new GitHub style. Does anyone have a blog where they critique the Methods sections in Nature journals (apart from Lior)? I saw a tweet by someone a few months ago saying the statistical quality of a journal article is inversely proportional to its impact factor. Sure seems like it.
  - Attachment (Nature Genetics): [Discovery of regulatory noncoding variants in individual cancer genome](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41588-020-0659-5)
> A new computational method integrates whole-genome sequencing and transcriptomic data to identify regulatory noncoding variants in an individual cancer genome.

**Alan O'C** (08:43:14) (in thread):
> That's really something. I like the equation derived for the sigma of the Gaussian: \sigma(N) = 10.8(1-e^{-N/105})

**Alan O'C** (08:43:27) (in thread):
> No magic numbers here no sirree!

**Nitesh Turaga** (08:20:27):
> We have no Bioconductor software packages starting with`J`or`j`. If Any new developers are looking to take that letter!
> ```
> > print(to_install_tbl %>% group_by(start) %>% count() %>% arrange(desc(n)), n = 25)
> # A tibble: 25 x 2
> # Groups:   start [25]
>    start     n
>    <chr> <int>
>  1 s       201
>  2 m       192
>  3 c       188
>  4 r       150
>  5 g       147
>  6 p       121
>  7 b       103
>  8 a        95
>  9 d        95
> 10 t        78
> 11 i        65
> 12 f        63
> 13 e        61
> 14 o        42
> 15 h        41
> 16 n        39
> 17 l        30
> 18 q        19
> 19 v        17
> 20 k        11
> 21 w        10
> 22 u         7
> 23 x         7
> 24 y         4
> 25 z         2
> 
> > letters[!letters %in% to_install_tbl$start]
> [1] "j"
> ```

**Susan Holmes** (10:54:29) (in thread):
> I used Matlab in the early 2000s for doing image analysis but it is expensive for people overseas and the free version (Octave) doesn't have anything like the R community. I switched all of my image  analyses to EBImage and with Wolfgang Huber we have a chapter on image analyses and spatial analyses with Bioc/R and there is a much smoother transition to the stats part with R, so I don't recommend Matlab for any applications nowadays.

## 2020-08-07

**Aaron Lun** (00:32:24):
> Apparently, I'm finally earning enough money to have to start repaying my student loans.

> ... almost 10 years after graduation.

**Aaron Lun** (00:38:58):

## 2020-08-21

**Dario Strbenac** (20:00:12):
> Anyone know where to find the proportion of A, C, G, T in a reference genome (e.g. hg38)?

**Hervé Pagès** (21:43:42):
> Don't know where to find this but it's easy to compute if a BSgenome package is available:
> ```
> library(BSgenome)
> genome <- getBSgenome("hg38")
> afq <- sapply(seqnames(genome),
>     function(seqname) alphabetFrequency(genome[[seqname]],
>                                         baseOnly=TRUE))
> rowSums(afq)
> #         A         C         G         T     other 
> # 911537909 633799113 636431323 914210243 161368694
> ```
> BSgenome packages include scaffolds (unlocalized & unplaced) in addition to the "assembled molecules" (a.k.a chromosomes). Replace`seqnames(genome)`with`getChromInfoFromUCSC("hg38", assembled.molecules.only=TRUE)$chrom`in the call to`sapply()`above to include only the chromosomes.


## 2020-08-23

**Sean Davis** (12:50:09):
> Please join us for a data science seminar on Friday, 09/04/2020, at 12 p.m. ET.
> Title: Galaxy for Immunological and Infectious Disease Research***Speakers:***Dave Clements, Galaxy Community Manager, Johns Hopkins University
> 
> Steven Weaver, Senior Programmer Analyst, Temple University***Abstract:***Galaxy is an open web-based platform for data integration and analysis in the life sciences. Galaxy makes sophisticated bioinformatics analysis accessible to bench researchers without requiring them to learn Linux system administration or command line interfaces. Every tool and tool setting is automatically recorded by Galaxy, making analyses reproducible by default. Analyses can also be shared with colleagues and with the public, enabling others to re-use and reproduce analyses pipelines.
> In the first part of this webinar, we will introduce Galaxy and its supporting ecosystem and community. This will include the many ways Galaxy is available to researchers, and a brief overview of the Galaxy user interface.
> In the second part, we will walk through an application of Galaxy to SARS CoV-2 research. We developed and published public reproducible Galaxy workflows for processing raw deep sequencing read data and calling intra-host genomic variants, as well as processing GISAID full-genome data in a comparative evolutionary framework ([covid19.datamonkey.org](http://covid19.datamonkey.org)). The goal of our analysis is to make use of all readily available sources of information to create a frequently updated list of sites in the SARS-CoV-2 genome that may be subject to positive or negative selection. High ranking sites on the list, especially those that are consistently detected over time or accumulate additional evidence in their favor with more data, could be taken as a set of candidates for functional impact or other downstream analyses. We search for evidence of selection at three different evolutionary levels: intra-host (next generation sequencing (NGS) data), between SARS-CoV-2 isolates (assembled genome data), and among beta-coronavirus isolates that are closely related to SARS-CoV-2 (assembled genome data).   In this webinar, we will review the comparative analysis dashboard that can be used to which sites may have a functional impact or could be used for further downstream analysis, as well as how Galaxy can be used to implement the pipeline on researchers’ datasets.
> Participants will learn how Galaxy is available, the basics of using Galaxy for data analysis, and how it can be applied in immunology in an example domain.
> Join ZoomGov Meeting[https://nih.zoomgov.com/j/1617561452?pwd=bE5YOVgrL2tHbFZidUJQOWZzdGlpZz09](https://nih.zoomgov.com/j/1617561452?pwd=bE5YOVgrL2tHbFZidUJQOWZzdGlpZz09)Meeting ID:[161 756 1452](tel:1617561452)Passcode: 586729

>  
> Dial by your location
>        +1 669 254 5252 US (San Jose)
>        +1 646 828 7666 US (New York)

> Find your local number:[https://nih.zoomgov.com/u/ayFfvRtd4](https://nih.zoomgov.com/u/ayFfvRtd4)
  - Attachment (Zoom Video): [Join our Cloud HD Video Meeting](https://nih.zoomgov.com/j/1617561452?pwd=bE5YOVgrL2tHbFZidUJQOWZzdGlpZz09)
> Zoom is the leader in modern enterprise video communications, with an easy, reliable cloud platform for video and audio conferencing, chat, and webinars across mobile, desktop, and room systems. Zoom Rooms is the original software-based conference room solution used around the world in board, conference, huddle, and training rooms, as well as executive offices and classrooms. Founded in 2011, Zoom helps businesses and organizations bring their teams together in a frictionless environment to get more done. Zoom is a publicly traded company headquartered in San Jose, CA.
  - Attachment (Zoom Video): [Video Conferencing, Web Conferencing, Webinars, Screen Sharing](https://nih.zoomgov.com/u/ayFfvRtd4)
> Zoom is the leader in modern enterprise video communications, with an easy, reliable cloud platform for video and audio conferencing, chat, and webinars across mobile, desktop, and room systems. Zoom Rooms is the original software-based conference room solution used around the world in board, conference, huddle, and training rooms, as well as executive offices and classrooms. **Sean Davis** (09:27:27):
> ***Special CCR Grand Rounds******2020 AAAS Martin and Rose Wachtel Cancer Research Award and Lecture******* ********“Mapping Cancer Genetics at Single Cell Resolution”******** ******** *******Luke Gilbert, Ph.D.***Assistant Professor, Department of Urology
> University of California, San Francisco**** ******** *******Friday, August 28, 2020******12:00–1:00 p.m.******* *******WebEx Event Information***When it’s time,[join the meeting](https://cbiit.webex.com/cbiit/onstage/g.php?MTID=e7627d7ef649c1c2ee7d38bfbdda44a51)**** *******Join by phone***[1-650-479-3207](tel:16504793207)Call-in toll number (US/Canada)[Can't join the meeting?](https://collaborationhelp.cisco.com/article/WBX000029055)**** *******To register for the lecture, please visit ***[https://american-association-for-the-advancement-of-science.ticketbud.com/aaas-wachtel-award-lecture](https://american-association-for-the-advancement-of-science.ticketbud.com/aaas-wachtel-award-lecture)Luke Gilbert is an Assistant Professor in the Department of Urology, the Helen Diller Family Comprehensive Cancer Center and the Innovative Genomics Institute at the University of California, San Francisco.
>  
> Dr. Gilbert was an early pioneer in repurposed CRISPR systems that are used to turn genes on (CRISPRa) and off (CRISPRi) by editing the epigenome. The Gilbert lab continues to develop new epigenetic editing approaches as well as new CRISPR functional genomics platforms. Recently, the Gilbert lab developed two next-generation CRISPR functional genomics platforms to systematically and quantitatively map genetic interactions.
>  
> The Gilbert lab is focusing on utilizing our expertise to tackle big problems in cancer such as metastasis and drug resistance in cancer. They use genome-scale screens, genetic interaction mapping and genome engineering to identify genetic and epigenetic causes underlying why some patients are cured and others are not by cancer therapy.
  - Attachment (Cisco Webex Site): [Meet virtually with Cisco Webex. Anytime, anywhere, on any device.](https://cbiit.webex.com/cbiit/onstage/g.php?MTID=e7627d7ef649c1c2ee7d38bfbdda44a51)
> Simple, modern video meetings for everyone on the world's most popular and trusted collaboration platform.
  - Attachment (help.webex.com): [Video Conferencing - Troubleshooting Meetings](https://collaborationhelp.cisco.com/article/WBX000029055)
> Troubleshooting Meetings
  - Attachment (american-association-for-the-advancement-of-science.ticketbud.com): [AAAS Martin and Rose Wachtel Award Lecture 2020 | Buy Tickets | Ticketbud](https://american-association-for-the-advancement-of-science.ticketbud.com/aaas-wachtel-award-lecture)
> AAAS Martin and Rose Wachtel Award Lecture 2020 - August 28, 2020 at Webex link will be emailed to registered attendees the week of the event. Find event and ticket information on Ticketbud.

**Sean Davis** (11:04:41):
> ***Explore the intersection of data visualization and cancer***Data are only as useful as the insights we can glean from them. How might recent advances in interactive media and data visualization help illuminate and solve pertinent challenges in cancer research?[Join us in a series of virtual events](https://lnks.gd/l/eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9.eyJidWxsZXRpbl9saW5rX2lkIjoxMDEsInVyaSI6ImJwMjpjbGljayIsImJ1bGxldGluX2lkIjoiMjAyMDA4MTQuMjU3MDEwNTEiLCJ1cmwiOiJodHRwczovL2FwcGx5Lmh1Yi5raS9jYW5jZXJwbHVzdml6Lz9jaWQ9ZWJfZ292ZGVsIn0.yi6eahZc-tWp4SpDMDtuGs1n4pKcnvf0NT20-IBPTgo/s/1189596639/br/82403408070-l)to explore these questions and spark new collaborations between experts in cancer research, data visualization, user-experience, virtual/augmented reality, and game design/development. 
>  
> 
>  
> There will be a series of four Micro Labs followed by a virtual poster session and 2-day Expo. Each Micro Lab starts with an inspiring conversation between thought leaders from the Cancer Moonshot and creative visualization experts followed by an opportunity to engage new colleagues from other fields in a fascinating discussion on the frontiers of cancer data visualization[MICROLAB 1: August 31, 1:30PM ET](https://lnks.gd/l/eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9.eyJidWxsZXRpbl9saW5rX2lkIjoxMDMsInVyaSI6ImJwMjpjbGljayIsImJ1bGxldGluX2lkIjoiMjAyMDA4MTQuMjU3MDEwNTEiLCJ1cmwiOiJodHRwczovL2FwcGx5Lmh1Yi5raS9jYW5jZXJwbHVzdml6Lz9jaWQ9ZWJfZ292ZGVsI3Byb2dyYW0tZGV0YWlscyJ9.FQL5ROh6blxUaAdCG__B1OOfjJmEZFYxHTYJ8KfgFJA/s/1189596639/br/82403408070-l)***Tracy Fullerton***Director of USC Game Innovation Lab, pursuing experimental design of games in cultural realms including art, science, politics, and learning.***Corrie Painter***Patient advocate and research scientist at the Broad Institute; directs Count Me In, partnering researchers with patients to spread cancer discoveries.[MICROLAB 2: September 1, 2:30PM ET](https://lnks.gd/l/eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9.eyJidWxsZXRpbl9saW5rX2lkIjoxMDQsInVyaSI6ImJwMjpjbGljayIsImJ1bGxldGluX2lkIjoiMjAyMDA4MTQuMjU3MDEwNTEiLCJ1cmwiOiJodHRwczovL2FwcGx5Lmh1Yi5raS9jYW5jZXJwbHVzdml6Lz9jaWQ9ZWJfZ292ZGVsI3Byb2dyYW0tZGV0YWlscyJ9.7CPiTwX3Rtl0Q-rpSqvlFDz3FCnM0Z5_MKlnwAA71vM/s/1189596639/br/82403408070-l)***Joe Gray***Director, OHSU Spatial Systems Biomedicine, creating a multi-scale tumor atlas and using systems analysis of extrinsic and intrinsic factors affecting cancer.***Sabrina Culyba***An independent designer with experience spanning animatronics to theme park rides, virtual/augmented reality, and transformational games.[MICROLAB 3: September 2, 1:00PM ET](https://lnks.gd/l/eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9.eyJidWxsZXRpbl9saW5rX2lkIjoxMDYsInVyaSI6ImJwMjpjbGljayIsImJ1bGxldGluX2lkIjoiMjAyMDA4MTQuMjU3MDEwNTEiLCJ1cmwiOiJodHRwczovL2FwcGx5Lmh1Yi5raS9jYW5jZXJwbHVzdml6Lz9jaWQ9ZWJfZ292ZGVsI3Byb2dyYW0tZGV0YWlscyJ9.JMXzTa5IXh88R06vt7U3_ZYui20KA5t-P428PtwHEjg/s/1189596639/br/82403408070-l)***Nils Gehlenborg***Harvard professor integrating visual and computational approaches to support sense-making of biology and reproducible collaborative research across epigenomics and genomics.***Kunle Odunsi***Physician scientist and co-leader of Roswell Park Cancer Center’s Tumor Immunology and Immunotherapy Program, advancing diagnostics and treatments for gynecological cancer patients.[MICROLAB 4: September 4, 11:30AM ET](https://lnks.gd/l/eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9.eyJidWxsZXRpbl9saW5rX2lkIjoxMDcsInVyaSI6ImJwMjpjbGljayIsImJ1bGxldGluX2lkIjoiMjAyMDA4MTQuMjU3MDEwNTEiLCJ1cmwiOiJodHRwczovL2FwcGx5Lmh1Yi5raS9jYW5jZXJwbHVzdml6Lz9jaWQ9ZWJfZ292ZGVsI3Byb2dyYW0tZGV0YWlscyJ9.hjfvRBuTtxK3-RmOoyrDp0qhb_Ye4KbTXTfxMEySeCY/s/1189596639/br/82403408070-l)***Lindsay Grace***Knight Chair of interactive media at the University of Miami School of Communication, exploring social impact through design.***Karen Emmons***National Academy member and Director of Harvard’s Community Engagement Program employing interdisciplinary approaches to reducing cancer risk and health disparities.***POSTER SESSION: September 21-23 (Asynchronous)***What are exciting visualization techniques? What are the data challenges in cancer? Submit your poster with a companion video and enjoy submissions from your peers across data visualization & cancer.***2-DAY EXPO: September 24 & 25, 10AM - 6PM ET***Collaborate with your peers on how to apply data viz to cancer challenges. Join us for an interactive symposium where we’ll explore the insights and get a chance to meet and connect with experts from both industries.
> 
> 
>

**Hervé Pagès** (20:37:15):
> I've seen systematic use of the right arrow in a package I was looking at recently. They were using the tidyverse pipe for almost every expression. So with the data flowing from left to right it seemed kind of natural to place the assignment to the right of the expression. I said "no way", of course.

**Federico Marini** (10:30:46):
> ```
> # T and F are wrong 5% of the time
>   makeActiveBinding( "T", function() runif(1) < .95, as.environment("evil_shims") )
>   makeActiveBinding( "F", function() runif(1) < .05, as.environment("evil_shims") )
> ```

**Monika Krzak** (07:08:57):
> Hi all , I have question about methods / ideas which would help to detect important covariates (biological or technical) that should be potentially included in the model formula when doing sc de analysis. At the moment I am using variance decomposition (cc here@Davis McCarthyand@Aaron Lun) to identify most contributing factors. As in MAST paper (cc here@Greg Finak) most contribution also in my data comes from CDR. Ideally one would need to perform analysis of deviance on nested models to identify other important covariates, but I have too many combinations to be tested. Are there any methods out there with similar spirit to variance decomposition or more focused on testing various models to help selecting the most optimal one ? To follow the question I created<#C01A0UWNVNW>slack channel, any ideas are very welcome here or there:slightly_smiling_face:

## 2020-09-04

**Dario Strbenac** (03:15:30):
> But aren't some genes only expressed in normal tissue and other in both normal and cancer cells? This would only apply to those genes which are cancer-specific.
  - File (PNG): [RNA-seq Purity Adjustment](https://community-bioc.slack.com/files/U34P8RS3B/F01AT4KCFL0/image.png)

**Alan O'C** (06:09:26):
> Tumour purity can influence genes expressed only in normal cells for example through compositional effects (ie, given a fixed library size, if more "cancer genes" are sequenced. then there's less reads available for "non-cancer genes"). Even if not, adjusting for a non-informative covariate in limma/DESeq2 etc shouldn't be a problem right?

**Dario Strbenac** (06:30:18):
> I think it will be a problem. Say you have gene A which is only expressed in normal cells and only 20% of the cells are cancer cells. It wouldn't be right to multiply the read count of gene A by 1/0.2 = 5 to rescale it based on the tumour purity. Gene A's expression in tumour is zero and all of its reads come from normal cells. I think this highlights the difficulty of accounting for different cell types and is probably a key reason why CIBERSORTx, which estimates cell type specific count matrices using scRNA-seq for deconvolution, made it into**Nature Biotechnology**.[https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-019-0114-2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-019-0114-2)In bulk RNA-seq, you can't tell which genes are specific to which kinds of cells, so a one-size-fits-all scaling factor seems optimistic.
  - File (PNG): [Composition](https://community-bioc.slack.com/files/U34P8RS3B/F01A3RSLX7D/image.png)

**Dario Strbenac** (08:10:04):
> Indeed. Even with the simple situation of one biopsy at one site and simultaneous DNA and RNA library preparation on it, the adjustment of different genes for the purity differences between biopsies of different patients is tricky.

**Alan O'C** (08:20:40) (in thread):
> A similar work was published on biorxiv in 2018/nature comms 2019 (not cited); I'd suggest perhaps CIBERSORTx getting into Nat Biotech has more to do with the popularity of CIBERSORT:wink:

**Alan O'C** (08:21:24) (in thread):
> With the caveat that I will always remain bitter about how they choose to distribute their program. Anyway good point

**Dario Strbenac** (08:30:10) (in thread):
> Oh, certainly. It seems like one of those saturated single-cell research topics. It would be nice to have the source code and be able to understand better what calculations it's doing behind the scenes.

**Aaron Lun** (11:17:48):
> Don’t see the point of reconstructing cell-type-specific count matrices after deconvolution. Seems like you’re effectively just doing DE analyses on your reference profiles.

**Dario Strbenac** (21:00:06) (in thread):
> Couldn't you then take the Cancer matrix and then split the patients into groups such as responder and treatment-resitant and then do differential expression between those groups to ask the question "what cancer cell genes are associated with treatment resistance, ignoring those immune cells everyone fawns over for a moment?" I think the scenario is you might have done scRNA-seq on half a dozen patients but you already historically have a couple of hundred bulk RNA-seq samples and you want to do a more detailed analysis of them. I like how they gave their method the nickname Digital Cytometry. That's good marketing.

**Aaron Lun** (21:02:02) (in thread):
> Sounds hairy to make inferences on “stuff that didn’t match any of our references so we’ll just call it the cancer profile”.

**Aaron Lun** (21:02:50) (in thread):
> god knows how much quantitative discrepancies there are between bulk and single-cell data

## 2020-09-09

**rohitsatyam102** (07:43:50):
> can we use Pkgdown to make websites for teaching purpose? If not, are there other alternatives to it apart from blogdown

**Federico Marini** (07:46:10):
> If you see how the workshops at Bioc2020 were done, I think the answer is yes

**Federico Marini** (07:46:52):
> Workshop pacakges had vignettes in them -> you can use that to get them rendered via rmarkdown and then have the landing page

**Federico Marini** (07:47:41):
> if you set up cleverly the GH actions, it can even autodeploy upon push- for this I’d leave you to the GHA-master@Kevin Rue-Albrecht:wink:

**Lluís Revilla** (08:29:57):
> There is always the option to use Distill to make websites

**Constantin Ahlmann-Eltze** (09:01:26):
> There is also a webinar by Alison Hill and Desirée De Leon on how to share rmarkdown documents with netlify for teaching materials[https://rstudio.com/resources/webinars/sharing-on-short-notice-how-to-get-your-materials-online-with-r-markdown/](https://rstudio.com/resources/webinars/sharing-on-short-notice-how-to-get-your-materials-online-with-r-markdown/)
  - Attachment (rstudio.com): [Sharing on Short Notice: How to Get Your Materials Online With R Markdown](https://rstudio.com/resources/webinars/sharing-on-short-notice-how-to-get-your-materials-online-with-r-markdown/)
> Educators create a lot of files for teaching- slides, exercises, solutions, assignments, data, figures - that all ultimately need to be shared with other people.

**Dirk Eddelbuettel** (09:10:19):
> > can we use Pkgdown to make websites for teaching purpose? If not, are there other alternatives to it apart from blogdown
> Of course there are!  Don't just fall for the loudest pied piper but find what suits you.  You can (for the simplest case of static pages)
> * simply edit in either Markdown or if you wish in RMarkdown
> * use any applicable converter function to turn (R)md into html, just as one does for pdf creation; at the end of the day they pretty much all call`pandoc`so you could also do that yourself.
> * post them on any website and to me the simplest remain GitHub pages
> None of this**requires**any package that may change and break subtly in the future (as apparently happened to blogdown users), or disappear, or add additonal complexity (distill is really nice, but do you**really**need it all?).  One can also look into other frameworks.  What the Python folks have in`mkdocs`isn't bad either (and calls one of their markdown parsers rathern than pandoc).   For what it is worth I keep updating the website for a course I teach for the 3rd time now (at[https://stat430.com](https://stat430.com)) and I use "just" the`hugo`binary and tooling there (and host via GitHub Pages) though I did look into two of the`*down`packages at the beginning.  My (very personal) preference is to have**some**understanding of the different processing steps so that I can disentangle and adapt them as needed.  Hugo is already pretty complicated so I prefer not to layer too many other steps on top.  That method seems to have found other fans as`hugodown`is also an apparent simplification (NB have not used it or looked in any detail at it).
> 
> But I don't mean to discourage any efforts here: if`pkgdown`or`blogdown`or`bookdown`or`hugodown`or ... work for you by all means enjoy them.  There is ample documentation and there are some helpers.  Some of us may have some concerns about long-term viability of those approaches but on the marging those are our concerns, not yours so make your choice and enjoy.  At the end of the day the most important part is always**your content**.

**Sean Davis** (10:55:21) (in thread):
> Our requirements for Bioc2020 were:
> 1. Structure each “workshop” as a self-contained, installable, testable package.
> 2. Provide workshop authors with a simple (from lines-of-code perspective) approach to serve documentation and course materials.
> 3. Build a reproducible, completely self-contained environment for running workshops in a portable way.
> Number 1 was pretty easy to accomplish. Number 2 has many possible solutions, but we promoted pkgdown because it is relatively easy to use and produces a very standard set of resources that authors and many users have come to know. Number 3 is a bit specific to how Bioc runs workshops, but containers are really one of the few ways to memorialize a workshop for the ages. If you don’t need number 3, then Docker images aren’t important. If you don’t need number 1, then pkgdown doesn’t really apply.
> I tend to agree with@Dirk Eddelbuettelbelow that pkgdown, blogdown, and friends are cool tools, but with some technical skill, it is easy to be creative with static website generators and documentation frameworks without a real need to go beyond rmarkdown or even simple markdown.
> Finally, consider using jupyter notebooks as well. For some, they provide a nice alternative to Rstudio/Rmarkdown.

**Sean Davis** (10:56:48) (in thread):
> If you want to reproduce the Bioc2020 model, just use[https://github.com/seandavi/BuildABiocWorkshop2020](https://github.com/seandavi/BuildABiocWorkshop2020)repo as a template. Documentation there describes the process to modify with your own content.

**rohitsatyam102** (10:59:03) (in thread):
> Thanks@Sean Davisfor this detailed thread.

**rohitsatyam102** (11:01:45):
> Thanks @Drik@Constantin Ahlmann-Eltze@Lluís Revilla@Federico Marini@Sean Davis. Will surely try all these suggestions. I got a lot of time this month. It will be fun learning website development this way

**Dirk Eddelbuettel** (11:10:51) (in thread):
> Glad to have been of help.  These explorations can be fun. I am currently (slowly) noodling on a possible alternative to`pkgdown`which has some design decisions that do not match my package structure (I use a GNU-style ChangeLog I want to render, my NEWS is in Rd format as for base R and I want that too, my vignettes tend to be pdf and on and on. )  Can be fun to explore these things, and it is**really nice**when you have something that "remains functional".  My personal blog is a non-R static html compiler I used for 17+ years now, CRANberries uses the same etc....

**Selvi G** (10:16:34):
> Dear All,
> 
> Good Morning!
> 
> I am a bioinformatics scientist at DFCI - DS. 
> 
> We download a lot of data to maintain our centralized databases. We are in the process of improving our network bandwidth and are trying to gauge the biotech industry standard. 
> 
> Would anyone be willing to share what their network bandwidths and inbound/outbound speeds are at their organization?
> 
> Thanks,
> SelviG

**Lluís Revilla** (10:36:14) (in thread):
> At work (not on industry) with[speedtest.net](http://speedtest.net); ping: 2 Download Mbps: 734.71, Upload Mbps: 469.83

**Sean Davis** (13:44:34) (in thread):
> NIH has two independent 100Gb connections to the outside and a 200Gb (100 x 2) in a “ring” configuration  that extends between three “hub buildings”. Other buildings have 10 or 40 Gb connections from the ring. Buildings themselves typically have one or more 10Gb trunks that serve 1Gb connections to individual machines except in special cases where high bandwidth is necessary (10Gb can be employed for special equipment or use cases). So, in practice, everyone has at least a 1Gb connection to the desktop. One special case is our HPC cluster which has 8 independent data transfer nodes, each with 10Gb connection, so we can theoretically sustain 80Gb from the HPC to the outside world (typically requiring something like Globus/UDP to even approximately reach). In practice, a border firewall limits transfer speeds somewhat.

**Sean Davis** (13:48:26) (in thread):
> A highly simplified overview of NIH network features is here:[https://www.cit.nih.gov/service-area/network-services-ns](https://www.cit.nih.gov/service-area/network-services-ns)

**Selvi G** (14:20:33) (in thread):
> @Lluís RevillaThanks!

**Selvi G** (14:22:18) (in thread):
> @Sean DavisThanks for the detailed info, Sean! This is very helpful. I will pass it on to our IT Director.

**FelixErnst** (09:45:31):
> Has anybody every tried plotting a heatmap of a 70k*2k matrix? Any tips on aggregating the data?

**Alan O'C** (09:48:29):
> Zuguang Gu had some posts on big heatmaps recently, more about rasterisation though[https://jokergoo.github.io/2020/06/30/rasterization-in-complexheatmap/](https://jokergoo.github.io/2020/06/30/rasterization-in-complexheatmap/)

**Jared Andrews** (09:55:52):
> Yeah, rasterization is also the way to go if you want any shot of opening heatmaps saved to PDF in an editing program (Illustrator) afterwards.

**Spencer Nystrom** (13:10:10):
> Depending on the data type you can take a random sample. For genomic signal heatmaps, for example there are many instances where the full matrix or a subset matrix look the same as long as the subset is large enough. But not always the ideal solution.

**Aaron Lun** (13:12:03):
> Back in the day I had to make a large t-SNE that was too large for PDF, but the journal wouldn’t accept bitmaps. So I saved the t-SNE as a bitmap and then**loaded it back into R**as a raster image in a plot to save the axes and title as a PDF.

**Jared Andrews** (13:21:31):
> Yes, we just added rasterization to dittoSeq to help take care of that for scatter/dim plot figures with tons of cells.

**Alan O'C** (13:27:55):
> ggrastr provides raster geoms too

**Jared Andrews** (13:32:52):
> That's what we use internally for dittoSeq.

**Jared Andrews** (13:33:44):
> Really need to swap pheatmap out for complexHeatmap in dittoSeq so that we can use rasterization for that as well.

**Nathan Dyjack** (23:36:25):
> @Nathan Dyjack has joined the channel

**José Sánchez** (23:48:02):
> @José Sánchez has joined the channel


## 2020-09-29

**Aaron Lun** (15:09:18):
> just changed my slack notification sound to a lady saying “hummus”

**Aaron Lun** (15:09:22):
> it’s pretty terrifying

**Jared Andrews** (15:19:02):
> Hesitate to ask, but why?

**Jared Andrews** (15:20:00):
> You can also have someone saying "complete" when your R code finishes running if you use beepr:[https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/beepr/index.html](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/beepr/index.html)

**Jared Andrews** (15:20:31):
> Or the Wilhelm scream.

**Dirk Eddelbuettel** (15:21:22) (in thread):
> That one gets all the fame, but RPushbullet was earlier and can do more.  Karl Broman featured it in a short comment years ago too...

**Dirk Eddelbuettel** (15:21:54) (in thread):
> [https://cran.r-project.org/package=RPushbullet](https://cran.r-project.org/package=RPushbullet)
  - Attachment (cran.r-project.org): [RPushbullet: R Interface to the Pushbullet Messaging Service](https://cran.r-project.org/package=RPushbullet)
> An R interface to the Pushbullet messaging service which provides fast and efficient notifications (and file transfer) between computers, phones and tablets. An account has to be registered at the site [http://www.pushbullet.com]([http://www.pushbullet.com)](http://www.pushbullet.com)) site to obtain a (free) API key.

**Dirk Eddelbuettel** (15:22:48) (in thread):
> Blimey. He even published a`done()`function. I missed that.[https://rdrr.io/cran/broman/man/done.html](https://rdrr.io/cran/broman/man/done.html)
  - Attachment (rdrr.io): [done: Send a short message via RPushbullet. in broman: Karl Broman's R Code](https://rdrr.io/cran/broman/man/done.html)
> Send a short message via RPushbullet, to be used to indicate that some R job is complete.

**Jared Andrews** (15:23:04) (in thread):
> That's nifty, though it requires a bit more effort.

**Dirk Eddelbuettel** (15:23:18) (in thread):
> Here is the post:[https://kbroman.wordpress.com/2014/09/04/error-notifications-from-r/](https://kbroman.wordpress.com/2014/09/04/error-notifications-from-r/)
  - Attachment (The stupidest thing...): [Error notifications from R](https://kbroman.wordpress.com/2014/09/04/error-notifications-from-r/)
> I’m enthusiastic about having R notify me when my script is done.
> But among my early uses of this, my script threw an error, and I never got a text or pushbullet about that. And really, I’m even more interested in being notified about such errors than anything else.
> It’s relatively easy to get notified of errors. At the top of your script, include code like options(error = function() { } )
> Fill in the function with your notification code. If there’s an error, the error message will be printed and then that function will be called. (And then the script will halt.)
> You can use geterrmessage() to grab the error message to include in your notification.
> For example, if you want to use RPushbullet for the notification, you could put, at the top of your script, something like this:
> 
> options(error = function() { 
>                     library(RPushbullet)
>                     pbPost("note", "Error", geterrmessage())
>                 })
> 
> Then if the script gives an error, you’ll get a note with title “Error” and with the error message as the body of the note.
> Update: I knew I’d heard about this sort of thing somewhere, but I couldn’t remember where. Duh; Rasmus mentioned it on twitter just a couple of days ago! Fortunately, he reminded me of that in the comments below.
> Another update: Ian Kyle pointed out in the comments that the above function, if used in a script run with R CMD BATCH, won’t actually halt the script. The simplest solution is to add stop(geterrmessage()), like this:
> 
> options(error = function() { 
>                     library(RPushbullet)
>                     pbPost("note", "Error", geterrmessage())
>                     if(!interactive()) stop(geterrmessage())
>                 })

**Jared Andrews** (15:23:30) (in thread):
> Phone notifications are quiiiiite nice though.

**Dirk Eddelbuettel** (15:23:58) (in thread):
> Yes, but then your phone puzzes miles or meters away where you don't here`beep`scream.  Or get notified from a cloud service.  And on and on...

**Jared Andrews** (15:24:17) (in thread):
> True enough.

**Dirk Eddelbuettel** (15:27:18) (in thread):
> Setup is easy but one has to sit down once and create the (still free) token.

**Hervé Pagès** (17:00:16):
> if "hummus" is terrifying then try "baba ghanoush"

**Friederike Dündar** (16:25:22) (in thread):

**Federico Marini** (16:29:26) (in thread):

> Still***is***:disappointed:

**Friederike Dündar** (16:30:00) (in thread):

**Federico Marini** (16:31:24) (in thread):
> Oh, can imagine. Germany’s a paradise for that. And parental leave for fathers is not even that crazy idea

**Friederike Dündar** (16:32:11) (in thread):
> yeah, who would have thought

**Federico Marini** (16:34:51) (in thread):
> Patriarchal-structured societies are still quite widespread, thinking it is 2020:disappointed:

**Friederike Dündar** (16:36:32) (in thread):

**Friederike Dündar** (16:36:45) (in thread):
> it's telling that they usually call it "Urlaub"

**Federico Marini** (16:37:26) (in thread):
> :nauseated_face:

**Friederike Dündar** (16:37:41) (in thread):
> on the other hand it is almost impossible to find full-day child care in Germany as in so many hours that parents can actually work 8hrs/day

**Federico Marini** (16:39:14) (in thread):
> Especially in Rhineland-Palatinate, that I can confirm on my skin in triplicates (well, one round of *technical* replicates:slightly_smiling_face:)

**Friederike Dündar** (16:39:50) (in thread):
> not just there, I believe:slightly_smiling_face:

**Friederike Dündar** (16:41:04) (in thread):

**Federico Marini** (16:49:20) (in thread):

**Friederike Dündar** (16:50:49) (in thread):

**Federico Marini** (16:55:39) (in thread):

**Federico Marini** (16:56:04) (in thread):
> patience, lots thereof.

**Friederike Dündar** (16:56:07) (in thread):
> and you worked on those fields and bikes?

**Federico Marini** (16:56:26) (in thread):
> no, but that was the steam-off valve

**Federico Marini** (16:57:08) (in thread):

**Friederike Dündar** (16:57:11) (in thread):

**Federico Marini** (16:57:51) (in thread):

**Federico Marini** (17:01:21) (in thread):

**Friederike Dündar** (17:02:13) (in thread):

**Dario Strbenac** (04:00:20):
> Is there an R function that applies a function to every combination (e.g. a pair) of elements of a vector? I am thinking about the shortest R command to calculate fold changes between all pairs of gene abundances in a numeric vector.

**Aaron Lun** (04:10:41):
> ```
> vec <- log2(runif(10))
> combs <- combn(length(vec), 2)
> lfcs <- vec[combs[1,]] - vec[combs[2,]]
> df <- data.frame(t(combs), LogFC=lfcs)
> ```

**Dario Strbenac** (04:20:18):
> Thanks. I was thinking there might be something even shorter using a functional programming function, akin to`Reduce`.

**Sudarshan** (13:42:56):
> @Sudarshan has joined the channel

> @Adele Barugahare has joined the channel


## 2020-10-19

**Eijy Nagai** (04:23:00):
> @Eijy Nagai has joined the channel

**Suzy Downing** (09:24:20):
> @Suzy Downing has joined the channel

**Elana Fertig** (09:40:42):
> All@Melanie Lothand I are noticing a boost error in the`CoGAPS`package that occurs only on******riesling1 ******Windows s**erver
> ```
> include/boost/mpl/assert.hpp:188:21: warning: unnecessary parentheses in declaration of 'assert_arg' [-Wparentheses]
>  failed ************************ (Pred::************************
>                      ^
> include/boost/mpl/assert.hpp:193:21: warning: unnecessary parentheses in declaration of 'assert_not_arg' [-Wparentheses]
>  failed ************************ (boost::mpl::not_<Pred>::************************
> ```
> Has anyone seen this bbefore?

**Elana Fertig** (09:43:46):
> seems to be a compiler error with boost[https://stackoverflow.com/questions/57457659/gccs-warning-in-boost-source](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/57457659/gccs-warning-in-boost-source)
  - Attachment (Stack Overflow): [GCC's Warning in Boost Source](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/57457659/gccs-warning-in-boost-source)
> I am using boost 1.63 and when I compile my application that includes #include <boost/algorithm/string.hpp>. GCC complains: In file included from /opt/boost/boost/mpl/aux_/na_assert.hpp:23,...

**Dirk Eddelbuettel** (09:43:47):
> Yes. BH maintainer here (**i.e.**the package whose headers call this, if you use`BH`rather than system Boost).  In general you would set options in the client package. But CRAN doesn't let us.

**Elana Fertig** (09:44:07):
> Thankns@Dirk Eddelbuettel

**Elana Fertig** (09:44:11):
> any idea how we should fix?

**Elana Fertig** (09:44:25):
> is there a widows specific flag to the complier we should include?

**Dirk Eddelbuettel** (09:45:15):
> **Locally**just for you in`~/.R/Makevars`add all the flags listed with a 'no' (**i.e.**from above:`-Wno-parentheses`to suppress that warning) to`CXXFLAGS`(or`CXX11FLAGS`).

**Dirk Eddelbuettel** (09:45:26):
> BTW not a good topic for#random.

**Elana Fertig** (09:45:38):
> sorry — wasnt sure which channel we shoudl use

**Elana Fertig** (09:45:41):
> happy to move it

**Elana Fertig** (09:45:56):
> the local solution doesn’t seem to help the Bioconductor isnstall errors for submission though

**Dirk Eddelbuettel** (09:46:03):
> So I use something like this:

**Dirk Eddelbuettel** (09:47:24):
  - File (Shell): [Untitled](https://community-bioc.slack.com/files/U34P8RS3B/F01CNSB6PT8/untitled)

**Elana Fertig** (09:47:51):
> do you submit that with the package?

**Dirk Eddelbuettel** (09:47:57):
> None of these are**errors**though.  Just extra extra tedious**warnings**.  If you have errors we need a new question.

**Dirk Eddelbuettel** (09:48:22):
> No you do NOT at least not on CRAN which forbids "non-portable flags":man-facepalming:

**Elana Fertig** (09:48:26):
> it’s onnky on the reisling1 Windows server

**Elana Fertig** (09:48:44):
> so for running on the linux / mac we use in the lab it’s not a problem

**Elana Fertig** (09:48:49):
> but erroring in the Bioconductor builds

**Dirk Eddelbuettel** (09:48:55):
> Probably Riesling (ie no ei) if named after the wine.  Same with Wiener of his processes.  Not "Weiner".

**Elana Fertig** (09:49:02):
> [http://bioconductor.org/checkResults/devel/bioc-LATEST/CoGAPS/riesling1-install.html](http://bioconductor.org/checkResults/devel/bioc-LATEST/CoGAPS/riesling1-install.html)

**Elana Fertig** (09:49:17):
> sorry, typing quickly

**Elana Fertig** (09:49:41):
> with a newborn so my typos may be frequent:laughing:

**Dirk Eddelbuettel** (09:50:22):
> Mine are now at college in 3rd and 5th year and I still type like crap.  But slack lets me edit so I can sometimes look respectable:wink:

**Elana Fertig** (09:52:11):
> so I guess the question is since it’s just this one compiler in this one system and working fine otherwise, and seems to be a dependency issue

**Elana Fertig** (09:52:17):
> how do we address w/ the Bioc submission?

**Elana Fertig** (09:52:20):
> to make error free?

**Elana Fertig** (09:53:07):
> happy to move channels to discuss if that’s more appropraite

**Dirk Eddelbuettel** (09:55:51):
> I just glanced at your HTML log.  I see two**unrelated**things happening
> * you a ton of warnings; all are harmless (apart from confusing you, see next point) but irritating; per the discussion above you can try to suppress some; I would consider add openmp**if**Windows has that (I just don't know)
> * a hard error ... but that one, experience taught us, is often a C++ compilation resource issue --> not enough memory

**Dirk Eddelbuettel** (09:56:51):
> If that analysis is correct then there is very little**you**can do other than maybe trying to dial down optimisation levels for the compiler.  We see these errors "all the time"**e.g.**on cloud instances with 512mb or 1gb ram.  For serious C++, you need more.  I think that is your issue here.

**Elana Fertig** (10:05:18):
> So nothing we can do?

**Elana Fertig** (10:05:25):
> Thanks for checking.

**Alan O'C** (10:05:53):
> Worth asking in#bioc-buildswhat amount of memory is available on the riesling builder

**Elana Fertig** (10:06:04):
> Thanks. Will do.

**Elana Fertig** (10:10:47):
> Really appreciate both of your quick responses@Dirk Eddelbuettel@Alan O'C!

**Alan O'C** (10:11:29):
> No worries, can be messy getting things to build on windows at the best of times

**Kasper D. Hansen** (10:20:34):
> Seems like ti would be worthwhile to figure out a configure script that identifies the compiler and then conditional on the compiler, sets the flags. This comes up once in a while, and there must be some autoconf way of doing this (although this problem is on Windows, so we don't have autoconf)

**Dirk Eddelbuettel** (10:22:53):
> I don't think you can.  Flags seen are standard`-O2 -Wall`which come from R.  The box is simply too small or doing too many other things.**Sometimes**the error is also spurious, so hitting it on the side (kidding) and re-submitted has also worked...

**Elana Fertig** (10:23:59):
> it woudl be nice if this were a Bioc solution vs left to the individual package builder

**Dirk Eddelbuettel** (10:24:50):
> The build admins generally work very hard at these things.  And "nobody leaves a $10 bill on the floor".  Most easy and cheap fixes have been made.

**Kasper D. Hansen** (10:38:34):
> Yeah, so we need a configure script which detects the compiler and then modifies the FLAGS but only if the compiler understands them

**Kasper D. Hansen** (10:40:26):
> something like this:[https://github.com/sifive/verilator/blob/master/configure.ac#L175](https://github.com/sifive/verilator/blob/master/configure.ac#L175)

**Kasper D. Hansen** (10:40:36):
> (I think, just did a quick google)

> @Chris Chiu has joined the channel

## 2020-10-21

**Britta Velten** (07:02:32):
> @Britta Velten has joined the channel

**Mikhael Manurung** (16:18:20):
> Hi, I have a question regarding managing multiple package environments in various devices. In a project that I am working on, I use both Python and R. Sometimes I work on my personal workstation and then there are times when I submit my jobs to a cluster. However, I realised that the package versions in my workstation and cluster are not similar -- I do not update the packages in my cluster as often as in my workstation. How should I solve this?
> 
> I am thinking of using singularity in both my personal workstation and cluster. Is this a good solution or an overkill?

**Nitesh Turaga** (16:21:23):
> A good way to go about it is, use a container (docker or singularity) and then upon start of that container it pulls from your Github version control.  That way you always have the updated package running.
> 
> Another more “localized” way is to use virtualenvs in python and R. Basilisk package in R helps with this.

**Aaron Lun** (16:22:34):
> At least for R, I used to have a cron job on the cluster that would just do`BiocManger::install(ask=FALSE)`every night.

**Nitesh Turaga** (16:30:23):
> But maybe don’t complicate things. Just use version control (Git / Github), virtualenv for python and virtualenv for R (renv or packrat)

**Dirk Eddelbuettel** (16:30:49):
> I never found it to be a real problem for R either and did what Aaron suggests:  current packages.  No issues.

**Mikhael Manurung** (16:39:24):
> Thanks for the ideas especially re: the Github. Maybe I can just put my conda env`.yml`there and use it as the reference for all of my devices. Is`renv`also capable of generating a`.yml`file?

**Mikhael Manurung** (16:41:13) (in thread):
> This is:fire:. Could you point me to a tutorial that can get me started with this? Thanks in advance!

**Nitesh Turaga** (16:41:58) (in thread):
> [https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/BiocManager/vignettes/BiocManager.html](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/BiocManager/vignettes/BiocManager.html)

**Nitesh Turaga** (16:42:19) (in thread):
> No idea about the`.yml`file for renv.

**Mikhael Manurung** (16:43:49) (in thread):
> I could not find an explanation on how to make a cron job in the link...

**Aaron Lun** (16:45:47) (in thread):
> this is a fairly standard unix operation, google provides reasonably good tutorials.

**Mikhael Manurung** (16:48:34) (in thread):
> Okay, I'll try it out! Thanks again:smile:

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (17:03:36) (in thread):
> `renv`generates a`renv.lock`file, which is the equivalent of the conda`.yml`file

**Alan O'C** (17:55:46) (in thread):
> I don't think renv works nicely with bioc-devel at all fwiw

**Burak Kutlu** (18:30:08):
> @Burak Kutlu has joined the channel

> The support site update will be happening today in roughtly 30 min.  The support site will be temporarily unavailable until the update is complete.  We will make an announcement on the support site and on the bioc-devel mailing list when it is okay to start posting.  Thank you for your patience and understanding.

**Iara Souza** (10:02:35) (in thread):
> When I have to work in multiple platforms, I use renv to control library versions. Also, I use DVC ([https://dvc.org/](https://dvc.org/)) and git to sincronize my data in those multiple platforms. This way I can ensure I have the same data in different machines.
  - Attachment (dvc.org): [Data Version Control · DVC](https://dvc.org/)
> Open-source version control system for Data Science and Machine Learning projects. Git-like experience to organize your data, models, and experiments.

## 2020-10-28

**Sean Davis** (08:29:33):
> Is anyone here using GitHub Enterprise? If so, how is your usage different from a standard GitHub experience (that is, how are you using Github Enterprise features)?

**Michael Lawrence** (11:11:13):
> For us it’s not so much about the features but the support that comes with the product, particularly on the development side, where validation is so emphasized.

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (11:16:45):
> I'm curious now,@Michael Lawrencehow much time would you estimate (roughly) is wasted figuring out things without the extra "support that comes with the product", at least in the academic context?

**Aaron Lun** (11:17:33):
> Unless you have super-secret stuff that needs to stay on-prem, I would say that you would have better value-for-money using the public instance.

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (11:20:21):
> Ok. Considering I've got my habits and I'm using GH basically every day, I thought as much. Though there are times when it'd be nice to poke support to clarify things, but I'd say that's rare enough

**Michael Lawrence** (11:37:03):
> Since for obvious reasons we have an on-prem deployment, “support” is mostly about integrating it with our internal infrastructure and keeping it working. It’s not about getting help with using it.

**Sean Davis** (13:54:05):
> Thx,@Michael Lawrence,@Aaron Lun, and@Kevin Rue-Albrechtfor the discussion.

**Dirk Eddelbuettel** (14:03:24):
> (For completeness, there is even "middle ground". U of I had a GHE pilot in the CS dept but is now testing a standard GH hosted "outside" yet accessed with a the same campus-wide single sign-on.  So same user account, but properties (orgs, repos, ...) you can only get to via SSO.)

**Lori Shepherd** (15:04:14):
> Bioconductor 3.12 is Released! Thank you to all developers and community members for contributing to the project! Please see the full release announcement:[Bioc 3.12](https://bioconductor.org/news/bioc_3_12_release/)

## 2020-10-30

**Mikhael Manurung** (05:49:53):
> Does anyone have ideas for fun activities during virtual casual group meeting? What have you tried so far? What worked and what didn't? And does any of you successfully recreate a virtual version of small talk at the coffee machine?

**Steve** (07:34:02):
> Our team has a weekly chat session where we show and tell a photo (or screenshot) from the week. No pressure to produce one if your week was boring. It started as being a photo from our lives, but quickly became a way to talk about anything of interest, including some obscure (but fascinating) hobbies some fo the team have. It’s extremely casual and relaxed.

**Mike Smith** (07:35:57):
> I'm guessing from 'casual' this isn't supposed to be work related.  One thing I've found works well is to have a fairly open ended question that each person in the group can take turns answering.  For example, recently someone asked "Where's the first place you'd travel too if COVID cleared up tomorrow?"  That's broad enough that people can answer in many ways (you can cover hobbies, family, adaption to remote working, previous trips, etc) so you learn a bit about each other, and everyone is likely to have something to say.

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (08:13:49):
> Hello community!
> 
> The#eurobioc2020submission deadline is extended to***Monday, November 16th***!
> 
> The organising committee welcomes submissions for:
> * talks
> * workshops
> * posters
> * birds-of-a-feather session topics
> The scope for submissions is far from limited to Bioconductor packages; we welcome contributions as workflows, live demonstrations, interactive Shiny applications, data visualisations, new concepts, packages in development, calls for feedback and collaborations, ... and all the many more hidden gems produced by the community; surprise us!
> 
> Submit your contribution here![https://openreview.net/group?id=bioconductor.org/EuroBioC/2020/Conference](https://openreview.net/group?id=bioconductor.org/EuroBioC/2020/Conference)We look forward to seeing you all online for this stimulating and community-driven conference!
  - Attachment (OpenReview): [EuroBioC 2020 Conference](https://openreview.net/group?id=bioconductor.org/EuroBioC/2020/Conference)
> Welcome to the OpenReview homepage for EuroBioC 2020 Conference

**Mikhael Manurung** (09:20:52) (in thread):
> Yes! This is exactly what I'm looking for. Thanks:smile:
