## 2020-12-10

**Steve** (02:52:09):
> Hi all. I’m an avid R coder, but not a biologist at all. I was curious to know if it’s possible to get someone’s genome sequence (i.e. via one of those tests available for a few hundred dollars), and use R to somehow understand the individual’s predisposition to long term consequences from concussion injuries?
> 
> The reason for my interest: I observe sports people receive similar injuries (head knocks), yet some develop Alzheimer’s at age 40, yet others experience no long term problems. I wonder if the genome can tell us who may suffer the worst so that they can avoid contact sports and minimise their chances of long term implications. Any suggestions / advice greatly appreciated

**Lambda Moses** (02:59:35) (in thread):
> You might not need the whole genome sequence. You can get select SNPs used for genome wide association studies (GWAS) and see which SNPs are associated with the predisposition. There might already be a GWAS for something related.

**Steve** (03:00:49) (in thread):
> Thanks@Lambda Moses! This is all new to me, so I’ll start googling the terms you mention, thanks very much for the ideas

**Lambda Moses** (03:04:30) (in thread):
> SNP means single nucleotide polymorphism, like single bits in the genome where people have different nucleotides. They can be associated with disease risk. Those tests most likely only checked some SNPs of interest instead of the whole genome, since the former is a lot cheaper than the latter.

**Steve** (03:08:03) (in thread):
> Thanks a lot, I’ve already found some GWAS’s that look useful. I just realised the human genome is 100GB (at least according to google)!

**Steve** (03:08:30) (in thread):
> I wonder if the $300 consumer tests provide a 100GB data file. I’m guessing they probably don’t. I’ll have to investigate

> @Ricardo Ramirez has joined the channel

**Abhijeet Sonawane** (13:41:40):
> @Abhijeet Sonawane has joined the channel

## 2020-12-25

**Hervé Pagès** (15:43:21):
> Let's not forget the "Writing R Extensions" manual:[https://cran.r-project.org/doc/manuals/r-release/R-exts.html](https://cran.r-project.org/doc/manuals/r-release/R-exts.html)It doesn't read as a book, doesn't have exercises, doesn't have much on the S4 class system, doesn't have an opinion on good software practices, but it's the reference and the authority, so a must to have in your bookmarks.
  - Attachment (cran.r-project.org): [Writing R Extensions](https://cran.r-project.org/doc/manuals/r-release/R-exts.html)
> Writing R Extensions

I use this very often as well:[https://kbroman.org/pkg_primer/](https://kbroman.org/pkg_primer/)
  - Attachment (kbroman.org): [R package primer](https://kbroman.org/pkg_primer/)

## 2021-01-04

**Lluís Revilla** (03:03:58):
> Why does Bioconductor exists as separate repository than CRAN?[https://twitter.com/ozjimbob/status/1345947982210818052](https://twitter.com/ozjimbob/status/1345947982210818052)
  - Attachment (twitter): [Attachment](https://twitter.com/ozjimbob/status/1345947982210818052)
> Hey #rstats folks, why does BioConductor exist?  It's super helpful that there is just the occasional random package with one useful function that isn't on CRAN but is just...somewhere else instead.

**Jared Andrews** (08:44:32):
> Different standards, different review process, different goals.

**Hervé Pagès** (12:58:36):
> also: 6-month release cycles + rapid push/build/propagate mechanism for the devel branch + an overall size of 140GB (mostly due to some really big data packages) that wouldn't be compatible with CRAN policy

**Aaron Lun** (13:00:13):
> some developer-level sentiments at[https://github.com/Bioconductor/MatrixGenerics/issues/21](https://github.com/Bioconductor/MatrixGenerics/issues/21)

**Michael Lawrence** (15:09:04):
> It’s also worth pointing out that Bioc is a lot more than a repository, contrary to what that tweet implies.


## 2021-01-05

**Ralf Weber** (06:10:55):
> @Ralf Weber has joined the channel

**Malte Thodberg** (06:14:28):
> ***Bioconductor also encourages reuse of common S4-classses that are maintained and optimized by the core team. CRAN is more of a wild west of S3 (perhaps with the exception of tidyverse packages)***

**Alan O'C** (06:39:17):
> Even tidyverse is kind of a wild west. I think Hadley's written about how they've got a mad mix of class systems (ggproto and reference classes for example)

**Federico Marini** (09:28:57):
> I don’t know about you, but I don’t think CRAN has much of an interest in having interoperable packages - and therefore*improve *your package, if you want to see it like that.
> The review you get there is only technical - and I guess pretty much based on the R CMD check output, with the extra “as-cran” flags on.
> Every package submitted to Bioc gets a human-made (and***for humans***) review, which normally elevates the package one or two steps more. And this is potentially priceless in the academic software devel panorama

**Lori Shepherd** (09:30:59):
> And as mentioned above... much more than a repository . you get this; a whole community to tap into and ask for advice and help.

**Kasper D. Hansen** (10:11:47):
> I would say a critical factor is the dependency tree which was apparant from the get-go would be much bigger than for CRAN. A bigger dependency tree has clear immediate implications for testing and release

**Kasper D. Hansen** (10:13:25):
> You can see the same with the tidyverse now, where I would argue they are starting to suffer from their approach of just updating each package as necessary. But because there are so many packages, tidyverse is essentially updated all the time. Not great for reproducibility. You can then argue that it is the users responsibility of making it reproducible, but that’s really just punting on a complicated problem.

**Aparna Krishnan** (13:56:31):
> @Aparna Krishnan has joined the channel

**Casey Greene** (15:05:45):
> I know@Sean Daviswon't toot his own horn but he has a new gig:slightly_smiling_face:[https://twitter.com/GreeneScientist/status/1346548082549391365?s=20](https://twitter.com/GreeneScientist/status/1346548082549391365?s=20)
  - Attachment (twitter): [Attachment](https://twitter.com/GreeneScientist/status/1346548082549391365?s=20)
> I am thrilled to be joined by [@seandavis12](https://twitter.com/seandavis12) at [@CUAnschutz](https://twitter.com/CUAnschutz) [@CUMedicalSchool](https://twitter.com/CUMedicalSchool) [@CUCancerCenter](https://twitter.com/CUCancerCenter) to work together at the intersection of AI and health!
> 
> [https://news.cuanschutz.edu/cancer-center/sean-davis-data-science-ai](https://news.cuanschutz.edu/cancer-center/sean-davis-data-science-ai) [https://pbs.twimg.com/media/Eq_mF_wXEAATmoE.jpg](https://pbs.twimg.com/media/Eq_mF_wXEAATmoE.jpg)

**Aaron Lun** (15:06:46):
> damn, I didn't know sean was a MD.

**Aaron Lun** (15:07:25):
> I'll remember that the next time we're at a conference together and I sprain my ankle or something.

**Lluís Revilla** (15:18:53):
> Thanks all for the input, but I was thinking (and I think OP too) more on the historical events that lead to the creation of Bioconductor.

**Sean Davis** (16:08:05) (in thread):
> Thanks,@Casey Greene. I could not be more excited! (except if, perhaps, I could go to an office sometime soon, but….)

**Vince Carey** (17:36:11):
> @Lluís RevillaI can only speak for my view of the history, but just about every one of the points that has been raised was a concern at the inception of the project with a modest number of packages, back around 2001.  We needed packages dealing with annotation that were large and "strange" relative to CRAN packages.  We needed stronger standards for documentation than were obligatory at CRAN (vignettes).  We needed two maintenance streams (release for stability and devel for experimentation).  We needed a fixed schedule for synchronization with changes to R and for interoperation of packages in the ecosystem.  We needed that schedule to be rapid enough to accommodate rapid change in biotech and slow enough to permit stabilization of user code.  I credit Rob Gentleman with seeing all of those issues, and Jeff Gentry and Jianhua Zhang with starting it off.  I am surely forgetting others who set the groundwork -- Tony Rossini was our first release manager and his rigorous approach to dealing with stability is memorable.  It may be illuminating to read this[https://genomebiology.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/gb-2004-5-10-r80](https://genomebiology.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/gb-2004-5-10-r80)... very readable, and, IMHO, not that much has changed.
  - Attachment (Genome Biology): [Bioconductor: open software development for computational biology and bioinformatics](https://genomebiology.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/gb-2004-5-10-r80)
> The Bioconductor project is an initiative for the collaborative creation of extensible software for computational biology and bioinformatics. The goals of the project include: fostering collaborative development and widespread use of innovative software, reducing barriers to entry into interdisciplinary scientific research, and promoting the achievement of remote reproducibility of research results. We describe details of our aims and methods, identify current challenges, compare Bioconductor to other open bioinformatics projects, and provide working examples.

## 2021-01-08

**rohitsatyam102** (07:03:30):
> I was wondering if there is any package wherein you input the NC ID of variants and it gives output about which ACMG attribute does the Variant qualifies for?

**Vince Carey** (08:38:06):
> what is an NC ID?

**rohitsatyam102** (16:14:33) (in thread):
> I was referring to variant annotation using HGVS nomenclature. Here by NC ID i mean the chromosomal position of variant. More from here:[http://varnomen.hgvs.org/recommendations/general/](http://varnomen.hgvs.org/recommendations/general/)
  - Attachment (varnomen.hgvs.org): [Sequence Variant Nomenclature](http://varnomen.hgvs.org/recommendations/general/)
> General

**rohitsatyam102** (16:20:57) (in thread):
> For example with NC ID: NC_000011.9:g.111959625C>T I can tell if the variant is pathogenic or benign, but I wish to ask if there is a package that assign it if the variant corresponds to which of the 28 ACMG attributes. For examples attributes discussed in this figure

**rohitsatyam102** (16:21:26) (in thread):
  - File (JPEG): [Screenshot_2021-01-08-22-21-09-79.jpg](https://community-bioc.slack.com/files/U34P8RS3B/F01HWA1L1QX/screenshot_2021-01-08-22-21-09-79.jpg)

**rohitsatyam102** (16:21:51) (in thread):
> Literature based attributes


## 2021-01-10

**Vince Carey** (08:19:27) (in thread):
> Could you have a look at[https://vjcitn.github.io/BiocOpenCRAVAT/articles/BiocOpenCRAVAT.html](https://vjcitn.github.io/BiocOpenCRAVAT/articles/BiocOpenCRAVAT.html)... I would like to do more to update Bioc variant annotation capabilities, and OpenCRAVAT ([https://opencravat.org/](https://opencravat.org/)) is extremely relevant.  The case study 3 in[https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/794297v1.full.pdf](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/794297v1.full.pdf)mentions the ACMG guidelines but I am not sure whether there is an annotator in OpenCRAVAT that uses those attributes.
  - Attachment (vjcitn.github.io): [Annotating variants with OpenCRAVAT, using Bioconductor](https://vjcitn.github.io/BiocOpenCRAVAT/articles/BiocOpenCRAVAT.html)
> BiocOpenCRAVAT

**Robert Castelo** (02:55:53):
> speaking of macOS catalina, i use docker to work with bioc-devel and, without any image running, the docker-related process`com.docker.backend`is working at 100% CPU, even without laundry to dry. i’ve tried the suggestions i found on google about restricting “Resources File sharing” to`/Users`,`/private`and`/tmp`and CPUs to 1, but still doesn’t go away when docker should be idle. any suggestions besides shutting down docker when i don’t use it? (which is what i’m currently doing)
  - File (PNG): [Screenshot 2021-01-12 at 08.49.12.png](https://community-bioc.slack.com/files/U34P8RS3B/F01J26R4FFH/screenshot_2021-01-12_at_08.49.12.png)

**Dario Strbenac** (18:00:05):
> Someone has proposed that[Genome Wide Association Study (GWAS) statistics should be stored in VCF format](https://genomebiology.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13059-020-02248-0).
> > To address these issues, we adapted the variant call format to store GWAS summary statistics (GWAS-VCF) and developed open-source tools to use this format in downstream analyses.


## 2021-01-15

**Malte Thodberg** (04:35:46):
> That paper had me wondering: Is there a Bioconductor package that has developed infrastructure for storing and manipulating GWAS summary stats?

**Dario Strbenac** (06:00:04):
> [GWASTools](https://bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/html/GWASTools.html)seems like the one you're wondering about. The journal article about GWAS-VCF didn't even mention its existence.

**Malte Thodberg** (06:23:42):
> GWASTools seems to be storing actual genetic data for GWAS, I was thinking in terms of storing post-GWAS summary stats, like positions, rsID, effect/other allele, INFO, MAF, N, P-Value, Z-score, standard error, etc. Basically the type of data you would use for SNP heritability, finemapping and colocalization

**Vince Carey** (20:20:44) (in thread):
> @Malte Thodberg@Kevin BligheI have done a bit of work on "data warehousing" for multiple sets of GWAS summary statistics.  The fields noted by Malte define records in SQLite databases.  Should we identify some example summary statistics -- for example, from the GIANT consortium -- and start a package to tackle this problem?  The colocalization task is of especial interest.


## 2021-01-18

**Malte Thodberg** (02:57:00):
> Interesting: What are the advantages of using SQL? My immediate idea would be to use a RangedSummarizedExperiment, with a GPos as ranges and Z-scores/SEs/P-values stored as assays in some form of DelayedArray

**Andrea Gobbini** (03:33:14):
> @Andrea Gobbini has joined the channel

**Kasper D. Hansen** (04:24:43):
> There are some long term advantages to storing data in SQLite because it is OS independent and the file format is unlikely to change. In contrast, when we serialize R objects, we need to update them every time we change the class definition. This has started to become more of a burden. Now,@Malte Thodbergsuggestion makes total sense for the R representation of the database.

**Kasper D. Hansen** (04:27:03):
> Having said that, Im not sure@Vince Carey’s suggestion was based on a careful analysis. Finally, there is the alternative to build of GWAS-VCF and use that to store stuff. Personally, I would probably start with writing code for ingesting GWAS-VCF because no matter what you would probably want to be able to access that data and once you have it, you have some example data to play with. But using GWAS-VCF as the defacto file format for this, might not be a great idea.

**Malte Thodberg** (04:35:32):
> I wouldn’t mind something standardized like GWAS-VCF catching on: Most people use their own csv/tsv files. That quickly becomes tedious to work with with GWAS tracks with tens of millions of variants

**Malte Thodberg** (04:37:25):
> Given so many usefull R-packages for post-GWAS analysis, like COLOC/MOLOC, hypRcoloc, susieR and gChromVar, it’s a shame they don’t use the same data representations

**Kasper D. Hansen** (05:07:13):
> The lack of standardization in the GWAS world is surprising to me. However, it is not clear to me that the answer is GWAS-VCF which - only based on the abstract - reads as someone taking a fileformat they already work with and say “hey, let’s expand this”. But perhaps its good? At least I hope the data they provide is great, even if the file format is not.

**Kasper D. Hansen** (05:08:09):
> In general I am a bit skeptic about these file formats. For example, IMO SAM/BAM was not great and I don’t have a great feeling about VCF but honestly I have only used it very little so I have no insight.

**Vince Carey** (06:11:29):
> [https://github.com/mrcieu/gwasglue/](https://github.com/mrcieu/gwasglue/)

**Vince Carey** (06:11:39):
> That looks pretty amazing?

**Malte Thodberg** (06:11:55):
> Also here:[https://www.biorxiv.org/content/biorxiv/early/2020/10/23/2020.10.22.351221.full.pdf](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/biorxiv/early/2020/10/23/2020.10.22.351221.full.pdf)

**Malte Thodberg** (06:12:50):
> Both these projects seem to tackle the issue of assembling GWAS summary stats + LD information into a single pipeline

**Vince Carey** (06:16:04):
> Let's deploy on AnVIL.

**Malte Thodberg** (06:20:11):
> Would be super cool if Bioconductor had a parallel to these projects though, with standardized classes for holding GWAS summary stats and LD-information from various populations. Then one could simply plug-and-play whatever colocalization or finemapping tool was best in a given situation

**Vince Carey** (06:28:11):
> Have you used any of these mrcieu resources?

**Malte Thodberg** (06:38:54):
> Looked briefly at echolocator, but since I was already running all the R-packages, using the their wrappers didn’t seem worth the while

**Vince Carey** (07:30:45):
> @Kasper D. Hansenthe gwasvcf doc includes: Querying by rsid is slow. If a large number of queries by rsid are to be performed then it could be worth generating an index which would speed up the querying. This approach uses SQLite to create a local database, linking rsid to chromosome and position. It strips out the ‘rs’ from the rs identifiers to make fast searchers by integer. The concept is based on that developed here: bioforensics/rsidx. ... so it looks like this is a hybrid of tabix/bcftools/sqlite....  perhaps a strong standard has not emerged because such a standard would really be the purview of GA4GH (imho) and the sights seem set very high ([https://vrs.ga4gh.org/en/stable/releases.html#id1](https://vrs.ga4gh.org/en/stable/releases.html#id1))

**Kasper D. Hansen** (07:33:08):
> Im just going to say that from where Im sitting, the GWAS community has failed to deliver on this for many years, so I would not hold my breath.

**Kasper D. Hansen** (07:33:44):
> A good long term solution might also not be easy, for example with different reference genomes and different populations and structural variants.

**Kasper D. Hansen** (07:34:30):
> But more immediate challenges, for example how to do a co-localization analysis well in R using standardized objects, seems doable.

**Kasper D. Hansen** (07:35:24):
> This may be a situation where you should start with a few reachable and identified goals and focus on that. And not get distracted by a perfect solution until you have some experience with 1 iteration.

**Vince Carey** (07:50:29) (in thread):
> We have to make good choices on representation and storage for the analyses to be performant.  But it seems like you@Kevin Bligheare thinking more about individual-level contributions to GWAS.  Even the summary statistics are proving to be unwieldy and need benchmarking and standardization.  I'll have a look at RegParallel....

**Vince Carey** (08:02:32):
> The gwasglue seems to depend on[https://github.com/explodecomputer/genetics.binaRies](https://github.com/explodecomputer/genetics.binaRies)... which provides plink, bcftools, and gcta in binary format.... unwinding this could be laborious.

**Malte Thodberg** (08:14:09):
> There’s quite a few very useful R packages for analyzing GWAS summary stats:
> * COLOC:[https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/coloc/vignettes/vignette.html](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/coloc/vignettes/vignette.html)
> * MOLOC:[https://github.com/clagiamba/moloc](https://github.com/clagiamba/moloc)
> * HyPrColoc:[https://github.com/jrs95/hyprcoloc](https://github.com/jrs95/hyprcoloc)
> * susieR:[https://github.com/jrs95/hyprcoloc](https://github.com/jrs95/hyprcoloc)
> * mmbr:[https://github.com/stephenslab/mmbr](https://github.com/stephenslab/mmbr)
> * chromVar:[http://www.bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/html/chromVAR.html](http://www.bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/html/chromVAR.html)
> * gChromVar:[https://caleblareau.github.io/gchromVAR/articles/gchromVAR_vignette.html](https://caleblareau.github.io/gchromVAR/articles/gchromVAR_vignette.html)

**Malte Thodberg** (08:15:42):
> It seems obvious to use them together with GRanges. Main thing would be a class to store the summary stats, and then sum functions for easier merging.

**Malte Thodberg** (08:16:18):
> For example, merging two GPos based on seqnames/pos/strand and effect/other allele.

**Kasper D. Hansen** (08:29:06):
> There is also the locusZoom script which is widely used but is just one enourmous R script as far as I know

**Malte Thodberg** (08:31:53):
> Never really liked LocusZoom, simply doesn’t produce very nice looking plot. We are currently working on a ggplot-based replacement:[https://github.com/MalteThodberg/tidyGenomeBrowser](https://github.com/MalteThodberg/tidyGenomeBrowser)

**Vince Carey** (09:08:46):
> Maybe we should start a new channel for GWAS-related software in Bioc.  But I will just dump out this concept:  We are looking for a GRanges-oriented interface to an external store with columns of diverse types, IMHO.  So really a DelayedDataFrame as the mcols of a GRanges... I don't think we have that.  Maybe recent work in TileDb is relevant; bigrquery also comes to mind.  The restfulSE project is somewhat relevant -- we have have a RangedSummarizedExperiment with a BigQuery back end ... for both the assay and the colData IIRC.  But handling the colData is complex.  End of dump.  bigdata-rep channel very relevant.

**Martin Morgan** (09:11:27):
> @Qian Liuhas worked on DelayedDataFrame and also with gdsfmt ; perhaps she will provide some commentary

**Martin Morgan** (09:12:07):
> [http://bioconductor.org/packages/devel/bioc/html/DelayedDataFrame.html](http://bioconductor.org/packages/devel/bioc/html/DelayedDataFrame.html)
  - Attachment (Bioconductor): [DelayedDataFrame (development version)](http://bioconductor.org/packages/devel/bioc/html/DelayedDataFrame.html)
> Based on the standard DataFrame metaphor, we are trying to implement the feature of delayed operation on the DelayedDataFrame, with a slot of lazyIndex, which saves the mapping indexes for each column of DelayedDataFrame. Methods like show, validity check, [/[[ subsetting, rbind/cbind are implemented for DelayedDataFrame to be operated around lazyIndex. The listData slot stays untouched until a realization call e.g., DataFrame constructor OR as.list() is invoked.

**Vince Carey** (09:12:25):
> right ... i googled on that but did not see it ... it is low in page rank for now

**Qian Liu** (09:20:14):
> Thanks@Martin Morgangdsarray uses gdsfmt as the back end, which was originally designed by xiuwen Zheng for gwas data lazy representation. Delayeddatadrame is compatible with all delayedarray concepts including gdsarray.

**Qian Liu** (09:26:27):
> Xiuwen has also developed several very useful bioc packages for analyzing big popular genomic data using`gdsfmt`(core array). For example,`SNPRelate`for analyzing GWAS data, and`SeqArray`for analyzing sequencing data (as an alternative to VCF). A new package called`SCArray`also suggests that he is working to use gds format to lazily represent the SCRNA-seq data.  He is also a co-author of`GWASTools`. The series of packages for representing and analyzing GWAS/DNA-seq/scRNA-seq data using GDS format is well worth to explore.

**Qian Liu** (23:18:45):
> Above message updated!

**Qian Liu** (23:20:36):
> The`GDSArray`package is currently a wrapper of GDS formats that corresponds to the`SNPRelate`and`SEQArray`only. There are more general gds formats that I will need to update the`GDSArray`to accommodate.

**Qian Liu** (23:23:38):
> For@Vince Careyconcept, the`DelayedDataFrame`is now extending the`DataFrame`and represents`DelayedArray`columns (e.g.,`GDSArray`,`VCFArray`) It should fit as mcols of GRanges.


## 2021-01-19

**Aaron Lun** (16:07:05):
> Julia's "all functions are methods" philosophy is pretty interesting. Basically every function is (or has the capability of being) equivalent to an S4 generic in R.

**Alma Hernandez de Rojas** (16:18:57):
> @Alma Hernandez de Rojas has joined the channel

**Spencer Nystrom** (16:29:05) (in thread):
> I spent a lot of time with pre-1.0 Julia and really loved this about it. Alas, the Biojulia project is not keeping pace over time as I'd like.`Bioc`wins again.

**Aaron Lun** (16:40:49) (in thread):
> As a learning experience/proof of concept, I'm going to see if I can replicate some of BioC's core structures and generics in Julia. Probably will start with the SE and see how far I can go.

**Spencer Nystrom** (16:48:45) (in thread):
> That would be really neat. I'll be curious to hear your thoughts after you've spent some time with it, so do report back. I very much want to jump on the Julia hype train, but I got a little tired of having to implement everything from scratch just to get an analysis done. I'm sure if you get something working well the Biojulia folks would be interested.
> 
> A major annoyance for me was their`GenomicFeatures.jl`which is their attempt at`GRanges`, but it uses B-trees and the ergonomics of it aren't quite the same, but maybe I just never grokked it correctly. Regardless, something more data.frame like in that space could really open up the genomics space.

**Aaron Lun** (16:54:28) (in thread):
> I can already see one advantage of the Julia CLI over python's; I can indent as haphazardly as I like and it'll take it.

**Spencer Nystrom** (16:58:10) (in thread):
> Performance of C, flexibility of python, indentation like a madman (if you want).

**Hervé Pagès** (16:59:28) (in thread):
> This will be an interesting experiment@Aaron Lun. I had that itch for a long time now but never got the time to dedicate to it.  Let us know how that goes.

**Aaron Lun** (17:18:37) (in thread):
> Trickiest part is how to handle copies, and the fact that the class objects are not mutable by default. A philosophy generally closer to R than Python, I would say.

**Michael Lawrence** (19:05:22) (in thread):
> `GenomicFeatures.jl`was developed by Pete Haverty, formerly of our group here at GNE. It was directly inspired by Bioconductor, and made some interesting internal optimizations that we could adopt (not sure about back compat).

**Michael Lawrence** (19:09:03) (in thread):
> Adding more Bioc equivalents is a good idea. Julia generics though are less constrained than S4 generics (the formals do not need to be consistent), which makes them less useful as an abstraction. Of course, a project could voluntarily put that constraint on itself.

**Spencer Nystrom** (19:09:08) (in thread):
> Was that not`GenomicVectors.jl`?

**Spencer Nystrom** (19:09:45) (in thread):
> I remember pete pointing me towards that one in the early dev phase.

**Spencer Nystrom** (19:10:11) (in thread):
> I recall that one acting more similarly to Granges.

**Michael Lawrence** (19:10:44) (in thread):
> Yea, that’s right, sorry.

**Michael Lawrence** (19:14:34) (in thread):
> Julia would complement R in much the same way that C++ does, as a means of increasing performance, with the added benefit (over C++) of being a simpler high-level language. R would remain the primary user interface. From that perspective, it may be acceptable and even desirable for the Julia equivalent of Bioc to implement mutable data structures.

**Aaron Lun** (19:15:20) (in thread):
> for that, we would need no-memory copies of data structures into Julia.

**Aaron Lun** (19:15:33) (in thread):
> I don't know whether the RJulia interface really supports that, or whether we have to do it ourselves.

**Michael Lawrence** (19:15:42) (in thread):
> definitely doable; would be fun

**Aaron Lun** (19:15:49) (in thread):
> juliacall

**Michael Lawrence** (19:21:05) (in thread):
> Would still need COW at the interface layer, but mutable after initial (lazy) copy.

**Aaron Lun** (19:30:50) (in thread):
> well, that's the thing; do we? We could just`unsafe_wrap`the input to create a Julia object that is understood by developers to be immutable. Provisioning the output is a bit trickier but could be done by calling into R to allocate the necessary memory.


## 2021-01-20

**Aaron Lun** (03:59:27) (in thread):
> My god. I finally got this damn thing working. Uses Julia as an embedded language inside the C++  code of an R package.

**Aaron Lun** (04:05:45) (in thread):
> Major hurdles with actual deployment as the embedding relies on dynamic linking. Also I keep on getting stack smashing errors when I close the R session, though that's probably because I had to do some voodoo`dlopen`without a`dlclose`somewhere.

**Kasper D. Hansen** (04:13:35) (in thread):
> How are Julia doing now with packages? Last time I looked I felt that the package concept (installing from say Github) was much more developer friendly, but I think that is a huge mistake. One of the most powerful thing about R is actually the CRAN/Bioc gatekeeping insisting on minimal (and sometimes not so minimal) requirements.

**Spencer Nystrom** (07:16:05) (in thread):
> My understanding is that what happens a bit is folks will form groups which host packages around a task, like BioJulia and the JuliaImage groups. These groups have their own standards. Of course, as you say, most things are hosted on GitHub, and there is a version of package registration, but nowhere like CRAN or bioc. The package manager, underwent a major rewrite years back to support many backends that are not GitHub.


## 2021-01-21

**Aaron Lun** (03:19:29) (in thread):
> Still waiting for my SQLDataFrame that's actually a DataFrame, by the way.

**Robert Castelo** (07:03:18):
> Not sure this is the right channel (tell me otherwise), but is there any documentation out there on best practices in using BiocParallel and basilisk? i want to call a python program from an R/Bioc pkg. the python program takes one BAM file at a time, i want to`bplapply()`over BAM files recorded in a BamFileList object. i guess the two main options are:
> 1. open parallelism before starting the basilisk run
> 
> ```
> bplapply(bfl, function(bf) { cl <- basiliskStart(env) ; out <- basiliskRun(cl, function() { ## call python }) ; basiliskStop(cl) }
> ```
> 2.  open parallelism within the basilisk run
> ```
> cl <- basiliskStart(env) ; out <- basiliskRun(cl, function() { bplapply(bfl, function() { ## call python }) }) ; basiliskStop(cl)
> ```

**Aaron Lun** (11:18:14):
> the former is safer with respect to arbitrary BPPARAMs. The latter only works in shared memory contexts, and might not even achieve parallelization in some cases.

**Martin Morgan** (11:25:28):
> It seems like`SnowParam()`is going to be a better bet for these complicated types of situations, which is method 1.

**Robert Castelo** (11:47:36):
> Thanks for the advice, i’ll follow method 1. One further question for@Aaron Lun, i looked up the code of[velociraptor](https://github.com/kevinrue/velociraptor), expecting to maybe find an example of using BiocParallel and[basilisk](https://github.com/LTLA/basilisk)together, which i didn’t, but i noticed that the call to[basiliskRun()](https://github.com/kevinrue/velociraptor/blob/8ec54c1731a1acda51e7040215afaafcd2547815/R/scvelo.R#L172)is not enclosed by the`basiliskStart()`and`basiliskStop()`calls. How velociraptor creates the Python environment without`basiliskStart()`? What is the recommended use of`basiliskStart()`and`basiliskStop()`?

**Aaron Lun** (11:48:17):
> oh,`basiliskRun`calls start/stop automatically if you specify`env`and don't pass it a`cl`object.

**Aaron Lun** (11:48:38):
> The explicit start/stop is useful if you have multiple`basiliskRun`calls in between them.

**Robert Castelo** (11:55:28):
> i see, thanks for the clarification!!:blush:

**Román Pérez** (15:54:14):
> @Román Pérez has joined the channel

**Amarinder Singh Thind** (20:01:37):
> any recommendation of R packages for nanostring analyses

**Alan O'C** (21:42:32) (in thread):
> Been a while but I believe this is well regarded for QC. Once you've got normalised expression values it depends what you want to do downstream but should mostly be able to treat it as any other expression matrix[https://www.bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/html/NanoStringQCPro.html](https://www.bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/html/NanoStringQCPro.html)
  - Attachment (Bioconductor): [NanoStringQCPro](https://www.bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/html/NanoStringQCPro.html)
> NanoStringQCPro provides a set of quality metrics that can be used to assess the quality of NanoString mRNA gene expression data -- i.e. to identify outlier probes and outlier samples. It also provides different background subtraction and normalization approaches for this data. It outputs suggestions for flagging samples/probes and an easily sharable html quality control output.

**Amarinder Singh Thind** (21:52:12) (in thread):
> Thanks for sharing, but I can't find a relevant article of this method. Recently, the normalization problem was reported in nanostring commercial pipeline.[https://academic.oup.com/nar/article/47/12/6073/5494770](https://academic.oup.com/nar/article/47/12/6073/5494770)and I am wondering if anyone has used[NanoNormIter](https://github.com/bhattacharya-a-bt/NanoNormIter)[https://academic.oup.com/bib/advance-article/doi/10.1093/bib/bbaa163/5891144](https://academic.oup.com/bib/advance-article/doi/10.1093/bib/bbaa163/5891144)

**Lori Shepherd** (08:01:02):
> and what version of vep do you have installed on your system? and you sessionInfo()  -- maybe open an issue on the ensemblVEP github page for Bioconductor so we don't use this channel   -- Please open the issue and post your version of installed vep,   sessionInfo(),  and you said that 1.32 couldn't install?  could you please print the error you receive when trying to install the latest version --  please post all of this  at:[https://github.com/Bioconductor/ensemblVEP/issues](https://github.com/Bioconductor/ensemblVEP/issues)

**Vince Carey** (15:11:35):
> The key here is to have a valid current version of Ensembl VEP.  I can tell you that installing it will likely require nontrivial activities in your perl infrastructure.  But once that is done, the ensemblVEP package passes check nicely.

**Martin Morgan** (15:17:47) (in thread):
> Are the instructions at the top of the vignette helpful for diagnosing this?
> ```
> To use this package the Ensembl VEP perl script must be installed in your path. See the package README for details.
> NOTE: As of Ensembl version 88 the VEP script has been renamed from variant effect predictor.pl to vep. The
> ensemblVEP package code and documentation have been updated to reflect this change.
> Downloads:[http://uswest.ensembl.org/info/docs/tools/vep/index.html](http://uswest.ensembl.org/info/docs/tools/vep/index.html)Complete documentation for runtime options:[http://uswest.ensembl.org/info/docs/tools/vep/script/vep_options](http://uswest.ensembl.org/info/docs/tools/vep/script/vep_options).
> html
> To test that Ensembl VEP is properly installed, enter the name of the script from the command line:
> vep
> ```
> This is from[http://bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/vignettes/ensemblVEP/inst/doc/ensemblVEP.pdf](http://bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/vignettes/ensemblVEP/inst/doc/ensemblVEP.pdf); the README is at[https://bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/readmes/ensemblVEP/README](https://bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/readmes/ensemblVEP/README). Both are linked from the package landing page[https://bioconductor.org/packages/ensemblVEP](https://bioconductor.org/packages/ensemblVEP).
> 
> Maybe a helper function`ensemblVEP::valid()`could provide diagnostics?
  - Attachment (Bioconductor): [ensemblVEP](https://bioconductor.org/packages/ensemblVEP)
> Query the Ensembl Variant Effect Predictor via the perl API.


## 2021-01-26

**rohitsatyam102** (03:43:20) (in thread):
> Yes I saw those line. However, I am doing this on windows. So how do I give the path of the perl script and where should I add this path? Sorry for sounding naive (in Linux maybe adding the path to bashrc would work)

**rohitsatyam102** (03:43:37) (in thread):
> But where to add the path in windows?

**Dario Strbenac** (22:00:08) (in thread):
> Control Panel -> System and Securiry -> System -> Advanced system settings -> Click on Environment Variables ... button.

**Mahmoud Ahmed** (20:16:42):
> What is a good way to get feedback on a preprint before submitting it to a journal?

**Jared Andrews** (21:55:54):
> bioRxiv + twitter. Getting colleagues to read/critique it.

**Aaron Lun** (22:02:25):
> most labs have "that one guy" who is supercritical and pedantic. Useful stand-in for the reviewer experience.

**Mahmoud Ahmed** (22:53:00) (in thread):

**Mahmoud Ahmed** (22:54:41) (in thread):

## 2021-02-17

**Dario Strbenac** (00:00:06):
> Does anyone know what happened to**Bioinformatics**journal? Its latest issue was four months ago.

**Jared Andrews** (01:05:31):
> My guess is they're just behind on getting the "proper" issues together since they all get listed as "Advance articles" anyway. Pretty sure their printing/shipping was impacted by COVID. The latest issue was just shipped on January 11th. Pre-COVID, they were shipping every ~2 weeks - that's fallen to about once a month since mid summer.

**Archana Panche** (01:18:57):
> @Archana Panche has joined the channel

> their rss feed is also stuck somehow

> @David Fischer has joined the channel

**abdullah hanta** (15:46:49):
> @abdullah hanta has joined the channel

**Aaron Lun** (16:28:56):
> I thought they were still hungover from the new year

**Aaron Lun** (16:29:06):
> you know the OUP motto: "publish hard, party harder"

**Dan Bunis** (16:49:32):
> I’ve been meaning to reach out to them actually… all correspondence I’d gotten on the dittoSeq paper said Advance version would be days & main release would be ~2weeks after signing of licenses.  Instead, Advance Access took somewhere around 1-2 weeks and was published Dec 12, but the full release version is still nowhere to be seen.

**Dan Bunis** (16:50:15):
> I can report back if I learn anything useful.

## 2021-03-06

**Faride Lamadrid Feris** (14:05:10):
> @Faride Lamadrid Feris has joined the channel

**Dario Strbenac** (22:00:14):
> Does anyone have a computationally-efficient R script that groups variables into clusters, such that each cluster contains a group of variables where each variable has correlation of at least some number (e.g. 0.85) with all other variables within the same cluster? If so, may you share it with me?

**Aaron Lun** (22:01:54):
> You could probably do the cosine normalization trick, then`BiocNeighbors::findNeighbors`at a distance threshold corresponding to a correlation of 0.85, followed by clique discovery with ****igraph****.

**Aaron Lun** (22:02:17):
> Should work decently well for a few thousand variables, depending on the dimensionality.

## 2021-03-11

**Michael Lawrence** (11:51:48):
> I wonder if there could be a way for Slack (like via some sort of bot?) to recognize when people mention a Bioc package name and automatically highlight it, and make a link to the package page. That might also allow integration of Slack conversations with package metadata somewhere. Like, “what are people saying about this package?“.

**Alan O'C** (12:20:28) (in thread):
> You probably would want some way to tag packages (eg, what R people do on twitter, {pkg}), to avoid the bot searching every word of every message on bioc. I've not written slackbots but I imagine it'd be pretty easy for someone (other than me) to do

**Sridhar N** (12:21:35):
> There are quite a few bots that are floating around

**Sridhar N** (12:21:54):
> but integration is PTA IMO

**Sridhar N** (12:22:11):
> unless some one has down time and willing to incorporate it in here

**Hervé Pagès** (12:46:03):
> PTA?

**Alan O'C** (12:47:20):
> PITA?

**Hervé Pagès** (12:49:11) (in thread):
> I agree that without specific tagging, we'd probably get too many false positives.

**Michael Lawrence** (12:56:15) (in thread):
> Specific tagging sounds like a pragmatic solution; is it supported?

**Sridhar N** (13:07:23):
> Yes

**Sridhar N** (13:07:24):
> lol

**Hervé Pagès** (13:14:21) (in thread):
> Don't know, not a Slack expert. Now how many Slack users are actually going to use the specific tagging is another question.

**Alan O'C** (13:15:31) (in thread):
> A bunch of existing bots filter/process the text of messages so I think it must be. Also agreed, it might be useful for the 5 people who use it (I would probably not bother lol)

**Michael Lawrence** (14:58:09) (in thread):
> Well I’ve noticed people at least using simple markdown on GitHub issues, e.g., for code, so I’m optimistic. If we had special functionality like that, there could be a bot that informs people about it upon first joining the Slack.

**koki** (00:16:19):
> Many Bioconductor packages depending on Seurat 4.0.0 was unable to build or install.[https://github.com/satijalab/seurat/issues/4226](https://github.com/satijalab/seurat/issues/4226)
  - Attachment: [#4226 Error: package or namespace load failed for ‘Seurat’:  object ‘markvario’ is not exported by 'namespace:spatstat'](https://github.com/satijalab/seurat/issues/4226)
> I recently installed Seurat 4.0 along with R 4.0.4 on my Macbook Pro running OSX Big Sur v11.1.
> 
> I was able to load the Library(Seurat) and run multiple analyses.  
> It worked well until I installed the "limma" package to speed up the find clusters, and I selected "Yes" when it asked, Do you want to install from sources the package which needs compilation? (Yes/no/cancel)
> 
> Now I get the following error message when I try to load the Library(Seurat)
> 
> Error: package or namespace load failed for ‘Seurat’:  
> object ‘markvario’ is not exported by 'namespace:spatstat'
> 
> I uninstalled R and re-installed and I re-installed R studio and re-installed Seurat and re-installed limma, but I'm still getting the error.
> 
> How can I fix this?

## 2021-04-12

**Naohisa Goto** (00:24:24):
> @Naohisa Goto has joined the channel

**Dario Strbenac** (02:00:06):
> I don't get it.
> ```
> > 1+!+1
> [1] 1
> ```

**Martin Morgan** (02:03:16):
> Nice!
> ```
> > !1
> [1] FALSE
> > 1 + FALSE
> [1] 1
> ```
> and also
> ```
> > 1 + ! + 1 + 1
> [1] 1
> > !1 + 1
> [1] FALSE
> > ! + 1 + 1
> [1] FALSE
> > 1 + ! + ! + 1
> [1] 2
> ```

**Chengyang Ji** (02:45:53):
> What does "+" do here
> ```
> > !+TRUE
> [1] FALSE
> ```

**Kasper D. Hansen** (03:27:47):
> probably the same as
> ```
> > +1
> [1] 1
> ```

**Chengyang Ji** (03:29:18):
> Right.
> And I think what might be happening here is that~~~~`+`~~~~has the power to convert`boolean`type to`numeric`, and`!`can do the opposite.
> ```
> > !+1
> [1] FALSE
> > +!1
> [1] 0
> ```
> This might be more obvious if parenthesis are used to force operator precedence
> ```
> > (1+!)+1
> Error: unexpected ')' in "(1+!)"
> > 1+(!+1)
> [1] 1
> ```
> And also in javascript, they allow`+`to convert`string`to`number`

**Chengyang Ji** (03:35:43):
> Like this
> ```
> Welcome to Node.js v14.15.1.
> Type ".help" for more information.
> > +"123"
> 123
> ```

**Alan O'C** (05:50:16) (in thread):
> usually
  - File (PNG): [whydoesR.png](https://community-bioc.slack.com/files/U34P8RS3B/F01UC47CS8Z/whydoesr.png)

**Marcel Ramos Pérez** (10:13:23) (in thread):
> converts to numeric
> ```
> > `+`(TRUE)
> [1] 1
> ```

## 2021-04-16

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (10:17:28):
> I'm close to solving an issue for the`TVTB`package, but I'm stuck on a 'basic' R code issue:
> In the example below, the user wants an expression that returns`TRUE`for elements of the list that contain**any combination**of the target values.
> At the moment, it is missing the first element of the list`c("a", "b")`, which contains both values, and should return`TRUE`
> ```
> targets <- c("a", "b")
> x <- list(
>   c("a", "b"),
>   "c",
>   "d",
>   "e",
>   "a",
>   "b"
> )
> x %in% targets
> # [1] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE  TRUE  TRUE
> ```
> I thought I'd try this friendly crowd before I head to SO on a Friday:wink:

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (10:18:21) (in thread):
> Original issue:[https://github.com/kevinrue/TVTB/issues/8](https://github.com/kevinrue/TVTB/issues/8)
  - Attachment: [#8 Rules of accepting multiple filters](https://github.com/kevinrue/TVTB/issues/8)
> Hi there, I am new here  
> I would like to know if I would like to keep entries in VCF to a specific set of filters.  
> For example, I would accept variants with any combinations of the following filters: `c("ASSEMBLY_BIAS", "ASSEMBLY_ONLY", "PASS", "PON")`  
> How should I specify the rules?  
> I have tried several kinds of syntax such as
> 
> ```
> all_filters <- c("ASSEMBLY_BIAS", "ASSEMBLY_ONLY", "PASS", "PON")
> customFilterRules <- VcfFixedRules(
>   exprs = list(
>     all = expression(FILTER %in% all_filters)
> ))
> 
> ```
> 
> However, it doesn't work.  
> Is it required to have another customized function? Thank you.

**Charlotte Soneson** (10:22:03) (in thread):
> `vapply(x, function(y) any(y %in% targets), FALSE)`?

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (10:22:52) (in thread):
> riiiight, thanks! let's see if that works as a`FilterRules`expression as well

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (10:29:10) (in thread):
> Thanks both. My understanding of their issue is that@Charlotte Soneson’s`any`is the right solution, not@Kevin Blighe’s`all`. We'll see when they answer.

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (10:30:03) (in thread):
> That said I still haven't fully converted that into a an`S4Vectors::FilterRules`that behaves well in`evalSeparately`. I'm just a bit rusty I guess:smile:

**Martin Morgan** (10:30:52) (in thread):
> also`vapply(relist(unlist(x) %in% targets, x), any, logical(1))`which I think would be more efficient (maybe not!); in XVector-land you get `any(CharacterList(x) %in% targets)` which is fast and comprehensible

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (10:33:34) (in thread):
> actually,@Kevin Blighe, you're making reconsider that they were likely after your version, now that I read their post again

**Michael Lawrence** (11:09:07) (in thread):
> ```
> any(CharacterList(x) %in% targets)
> ```

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (11:10:43) (in thread):
> testing now, thanks! (in FilterRules expression)

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (11:11:27) (in thread):
> yay!

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (11:13:49) (in thread):
> Thanks all for your help, here's my reply to their issue:[https://github.com/kevinrue/TVTB/issues/8#issuecomment-821247079](https://github.com/kevinrue/TVTB/issues/8#issuecomment-821247079)
  - Attachment: [Comment on #8 Rules of accepting multiple filters](https://github.com/kevinrue/TVTB/issues/8#issuecomment-821247079)
> Dear [@hsiaoyi0504](https://github.com/hsiaoyi0504)
> 
> I will split my answer in a few points. I hope they are all clear.
> 
> **Origin and solution to the error**
> 
> Having cross-posted your issue on the package that TVTB relies on to actually do the work:  
> <https://github.com/Bioconductor/S4Vectors/issues/80#issuecomment-820623709|Bioconductor/S4Vectors#80 (comment)>
> 
> I was made aware that the calling environment is not automatically passed to the filtering step.
> 
> The solution to your issue is to pass the global environment (where the object `all_filters` is defined, to the `evalSparately()` function call, so that the object `all_filters` is visible in the environment where the expression is evaluated.
> 
> ```
> all_filters <- c("ASSEMBLY_BIAS", "ASSEMBLY_ONLY", "PASS", "PON")
> customFilterRules <- VcfFixedRules(
>   exprs = list(
>     all = expression(FILTER %in% all_filters)
> ))
> summary(evalSeparately(customFilterRules, vcf)) # fails
> summary(evalSeparately(customFilterRules, vcf, enclos = .GlobalEnv)) # succeeds
> 
> ```
> 
> To illustrate the point, I will note that another solution could be to define the vector of values in the expression itself, so that it is defined on the fly when the rule is evaluated. However, that is probably not convenient for a programmatic and dynamic approach.
> 
> ```
> customFilterRules [https://www.internationalgenome.org/wiki/Analysis/vcf4.0/](- VcfFixedRules(
>   exprs = list(
>     all = expression(FILTER %in% c("ASSEMBLY_BIAS", "ASSEMBLY_ONLY", "PASS", "PON"))
> ))
> summary(evalSeparately(customFilterRules, vcf))
> 
> ```
> 
> **Clarification about "combinations of a set of filters"**
> 
> I understand what you mean now. It is possible for the FILTER column to contain multiple values separated by semi-colon, e.g. "q10;s50" ([https://www.internationalgenome.org/wiki/Analysis/vcf4.0/)).
> 
> I'm just not sure whether you want:
> 
> • records where the FILTER value contains any *one* of the target values
> • records where the FILTER value contains *all* of the target values
> 
> As the VariantAnnotation package does not split the FILTER value (it is read as a single string `"q10;s50"`, it is not converted as a vector `c("q10", "s50")`, you need to do the splitting yourself.
> 
> I have created an example file here: [https://gist.github.com/kevinrue/120d584b07834fc457ec1442fd72c5df](https://gist.github.com/kevinrue/120d584b07834fc457ec1442fd72c5df)  
> My example code below refers to this file, if you wish to reproduce my code and outputs.
> 
> First, load the file as a VCF object:
> 
> ```
> library(SummarizedExperiment)
> library(TVTB)
> 
> vcf <- VariantAnnotation::readVcf(file = "moderate.vcf")
> 
> all_filters <- c("q10", "q20")
> 
> ```
> 
> Assuming this is what you want:
> 
> ](https://www.internationalgenome.org/wiki/Analysis/vcf4.0/)).
> 
> I'm just not sure whether you want:
> 
> • records where the FILTER value contains any *one* of the target values
> • records where the FILTER value contains *all* of the target values
> 
> As the VariantAnnotation package does not split the FILTER value (it is read as a single string `"q10;s50"`, it is not converted as a vector `c("q10", "s50")`, you need to do the splitting yourself.
> 
> I have created an example file here: [https://gist.github.com/kevinrue/120d584b07834fc457ec1442fd72c5df](https://gist.github.com/kevinrue/120d584b07834fc457ec1442fd72c5df)  
> My example code below refers to this file, if you wish to reproduce my code and outputs.
> 
> First, load the file as a VCF object:
> 
> ```
> library(SummarizedExperiment)
> library(TVTB)
> 
> vcf <- VariantAnnotation::readVcf(file = "moderate.vcf")
> 
> all_filters <- c("q10", "q20")
> 
> ```
> 
> Assuming this is what you want:
> 
> ) • records where the FILTER value contains *all* of the target values
> 
> Then, the code would be:
> 
> ```
> customFilterRules <- VcfFixedRules(
>   exprs = list(
>     all2 = expression(unlist(lapply(strsplit(FILTER, ";"), function(x) all(all_filters %in% x))))
> ))
> summary(evalSeparately(customFilterRules, vcf, enclos = .GlobalEnv))
> evalSeparately(customFilterRules, vcf, enclos = .GlobalEnv)
> rowRanges(vcf)[eval(customFilterRules, vcf, enclos = .GlobalEnv)]
> rowRanges(vcf) # compare with
> 
> ```
> 
> Assuming this is what you want:
> 
> • records where the FILTER value contains any *one* of the target values
> 
> then, the code would be
> 
> ```
> customFilterRules <- VcfFixedRules(
>   exprs = list(
>     all2 = expression(any(CharacterList(strsplit(FILTER, ";")) %in% all_filters))
> ))
> 
> ```
> 
> Let me know how it goes!

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (11:15:29) (in thread):

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (11:17:25) (in thread):

>   CharacterList(x),
>   function(y) all(targets %in% y)
> ))

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (11:18:02) (in thread):
> damn, that's worth a Friday beer:stuck_out_tongue_winking_eye:

**Michael Lawrence** (11:28:37) (in thread):
> `sum(unique(CharacterList(x)) %in% targets) == length(targets)`

**Michael Lawrence** (11:31:59) (in thread):
> `all(CharacterList(targets) %in% CharacterList(x))`

**Michael Lawrence** (11:33:30) (in thread):
> That last one is a bit unintuitive because of the behavior of`CharacterList()`on a list vs. vector, and then the recycling.

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (11:35:03) (in thread):
> :exploding_head:

## 2021-04-22

**Davide Risso** (16:08:56):
> This R behavior was surprising to me...
> ```
> > x <- seq(10)
> > x[c(TRUE, FALSE)]
> [1] 1 3 5 7 9
> ```
> the surprise was that there is no warning that the two vectors are of different lengths... any idea what is the reason for the lack of warning? Am I missing something obvious?

**Davide Risso** (16:09:55):
> I encounter this while reviewing this code:
> ```
> keepgenes <- !isOutlier(sce_cordblood$sum, type="lower", log=TRUE)
> sce_cordblood <- sce_cordblood[keepgenes, ]
> ```
> Obviously the intent was to filter out cells, but R didn't complain and the student didn't realize they filtered out genes instead

**Alan O'C** (16:11:07):
> Vector recycling, R never warns for that

**Alan O'C** (16:11:47):
> Doesn't even warn for`x[c(T, F, T)]`

**Davide Risso** (16:12:41):
> yeah exactly, I used a wrong minimal example since 2 is a multiple of 10, but I assumed that when not a multiple of the vector length it would throw a warning, while it doesn't

**Alan O'C** (16:12:50):
> Well strictly speaking it will~~~warn~~~error if you try to add a column to a dataframe and the length of the vector isn't a factor of the number of rows, and similar cases.

**Alan O'C** (16:14:20):
  - File (PNG): [whydoesR.png](https://community-bioc.slack.com/files/U34P8RS3B/F01URQG5QS3/whydoesr.png)

**Davide Risso** (16:15:46):
> yeah I guess that I knew that R recycles vectors, I just never thought that it could lead to that type of easy-to-make mistake in matrix subsetting

**Alan O'C** (16:17:08):
> That,`match`, and factors have each caused me immeasurable pain and embarrassment over the years

**Hervé Pagès** (16:24:07):
> Yeah, weird that R doesn't issue a warning for`x[c(T, F, T)]`, especially since things like`rep(TRUE, 10) & c(TRUE, FALSE, FALSE)`or`1:10 + c(TRUE, FALSE, FALSE)`do issue one. You wish that vector recycling would behave consistently across a wide range of operations. And that would just be the case if the recycling code was implemented in one single place and shared but I'm not aware of anything like that. So it seems that every function needs to re-implement their own little recycling mechanism:disappointed:

**Hervé Pagès** (16:32:17):
> Another famous exception to the rule:
> ```
> > paste0(letters, LETTERS[1:3])
>  [1] "aA" "bB" "cC" "dA" "eB" "fC" "gA" "hB" "iC" "jA" "kB" "lC" "mA" "nB" "oC"
> [16] "pA" "qB" "rC" "sA" "tB" "uC" "vA" "wB" "xC" "yA" "zB"
> ```
> No warning!
> Also:
> ```
> > paste0(letters, LETTERS[0])
>  [1] "a" "b" "c" "d" "e" "f" "g" "h" "i" "j" "k" "l" "m" "n" "o" "p" "q" "r" "s"
> [20] "t" "u" "v" "w" "x" "y" "z"
> ```
> Inconsistent with how binary arithmetic operations treat the zero-length case.


## 2021-04-23

**Robert Castelo** (02:11:32):
> The only place where**Recycling rules**are referred to in the Rlang manual ([here](https://cran.r-project.org/doc/manuals/R-lang.html#Recycling-rules)), where it is described that a warning is given when the longer vector is not a multiple of the shorter one, is the section on**Elementary arithmetic operations**, so probably we cannot expect any specific behaviour for non-arithmetic operations. The[R inferno](https://www.burns-stat.com/pages/Tutor/R_inferno.pdf)from Patrick Burns contains another funny edge case of the recyling rule even in arithmetics, where**something plus nothing is … nothing!**
> ```
> > 1 + NULL
> numeric(0)
> ```

**FelixErnst** (03:37:52) (in thread):
> I always think of`NULL`as a blackhole. It sucks in everything and even though you cannot see it, it is usually somewhere

**Robert Castelo** (04:15:53) (in thread):
> Albert Einstein would have been an enthusiastic R user:zany_face:

**FelixErnst** (05:19:15) (in thread):
> You are onto something:slightly_smiling_face:`NA`is the empty space: If you add something to that is still vast empty space. That should have added AE's enthusiasm

**Robert Castelo** (05:53:21) (in thread):
> If physicists would learn about this, they  would all quit MATLAB, Maple and Mathematica and move into R:grin:… or maybe that’s the reason why so many physicists move into bioinformatics with R.

**Alan O'C** (11:36:21):
> I'm part of a group in bonnie Scotland developing a short (2-day) carpentries-style course on high-dimensional statistics for biological and health data. If you'd be interested in taking that type of course it'd be helpful if you could fill out this form to give us ideas on what topics to cover[https://forms.gle/yvA3fKyHkkJhYUTr7](https://forms.gle/yvA3fKyHkkJhYUTr7)
  - Attachment (Google Docs): [Learners for high dimensional statistics](https://forms.gle/yvA3fKyHkkJhYUTr7)
> High dimensional stats in this context refers to methods you might apply (for example) to large sequencing data like RNAseq, scRNAseq, or genetic sequence data particularly of large cohorts. We might cover topics like dimensionality reduction, clustering, multiple testing, and some broader statistical concepts that can be useful for data like these. These are some questions to guide our development of a course on statistics for high-dimensional datasets, to choose what topics to cover and how to cover them. The course will be delivered in the latter half of 2021.

**Alan O'C** (12:24:36) (in thread):
> Should mention: the course materials will be open and we'll be happily encouraging others to use the materials. We may also be looking for external groups who would like to test out the course in the latter stages of development:slightly_smiling_face:

**Henrik Bengtsson** (13:59:10) (in thread):
> > `x[c(T, F, T)]`
> I'm pretty sure Section 'Indexing' in 'R Language Definition' ([https://cran.r-project.org/doc/manuals/R-lang.html#Indexing](https://cran.r-project.org/doc/manuals/R-lang.html#Indexing)) covers that case.  We relied on that long section when implementing support for row and column subsetting in***matrixStats***and I remembering it as comprehensive and covering lots of corner cases.

> Hi  guys, just to double confirm that pushing bug fix to RELEASE_3_12 is not accepted anymore right?

**Haichao Wang** (08:52:19):
> my pushing to master branch is successful, but RELEASE_3_12 was rejected

**Haichao Wang** (08:53:42):
> I saw the announced schedule here:[http://bioconductor.org/developers/release-schedule/](http://bioconductor.org/developers/release-schedule/)

**Haichao Wang** (08:54:10):
> but not sure whether I understand it correctly, as this is my first package ( as a maintainer!)

**Lori Shepherd** (08:58:18):
> yes. We freezed the RELEASE_3_12 branch to no longer accept changes.  You may continue to push to master (Devel branch).  When the release happens in another two weeks the core team will create the new RELEASE_3_13 branch from the current version of the master branch.

**Haichao Wang** (09:14:05):
> will the RELEASE_3_12 be kept in the git repo as a branch? or it will be wiped completely after 3_13?

**Lori Shepherd** (09:22:26):
> it will be kept for legacy and we keep the versions of the package to be able to download the appropriate version of the package for past versions of R

**Lori Shepherd** (09:22:45):
> but you will not be able to push to our[git.bioconductor.org](http://git.bioconductor.org)version of the release_3_12 branch

**Mike Smith** (09:24:29):
> If you look at a package that's been in Bioconductor for a long time, you can see the available RELEASE branches go back a quite a way e.g.[https://code.bioconductor.org/browse/biomaRt/](https://code.bioconductor.org/browse/biomaRt/)

**Pratheepa Jeganathan** (10:37:28):
> @Pratheepa Jeganathan has joined the channel

**Haichao Wang** (10:51:48):
> thank you both Lori and Mike. still got another Q: will I be allowed delete the old RELEASE branches? (due to some special reasons …)

**Lori Shepherd** (10:55:27):
> normally we would keep all branches on[git.bioconductor.org](http://git.bioconductor.org)for legacy purposes and not delete anything -- it would probably be better to have a private discussion over email if there were special circumstances for this request.  I suggest maybe the maintainer at[bioconductor.org](http://bioconductor.org)email

**Haichao Wang** (14:17:58):
> thanks Lori, appreciate your suggestion. I will email[maintainer@bioconductor.org](mailto:maintainer@bioconductor.org)about this

**Briha Ansari** (17:41:53):
> @Briha Ansari has joined the channel

**Alan O'C** (10:43:34):
> Any ideas what licence GEO data is released under?

**Casey Greene** (10:46:47):
> the "good luck" license

**Casey Greene** (10:46:50):
> > Databases of molecular data on the NCBI Web site include such examples as nucleotide sequences (GenBank), protein sequences, macromolecular structures, molecular variation, gene expression, and mapping data. They are designed to provide and encourage access within the scientific community to sources of current and comprehensive information. Therefore, NCBI itself places no restrictions on the use or distribution of the data contained therein. Nor do we accept data when the submitter has requested restrictions on reuse or redistribution. However, some submitters of the original data (or the country of origin of such data) may claim patent, copyright, or other intellectual property rights in all or a portion of the data (that has been submitted). NCBI is not in a position to assess the validity of such claims and since there is no transfer of rights from submitters to NCBI, NCBI has no rights to transfer to a third party. Therefore, NCBI cannot provide comment or unrestricted permission concerning the use, copying, or distribution of the information contained in the molecular databases.

**Casey Greene** (10:47:10):
> key is:`Nor do we accept data when the submitter has requested restrictions on reuse or redistribution.`

**Alan O'C** (10:49:29):
> Clear as mud

**Alan O'C** (10:49:44):
> Thanks though, where'd you find it?

**Casey Greene** (10:50:45):
> [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/home/about/policies/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/home/about/policies/)

**Sean Davis** (11:54:45):
> Also, this:[https://grants.nih.gov/grants/guide/notice-files/not-od-14-124.html](https://grants.nih.gov/grants/guide/notice-files/not-od-14-124.html)
  - Attachment (grants.nih.gov): [NOT-OD-14-124: NIH Genomic Data Sharing Policy](https://grants.nih.gov/grants/guide/notice-files/not-od-14-124.html)
> NIH Funding Opportunities and Notices in the NIH Guide for Grants and Contracts: NIH Genomic Data Sharing Policy NOT-OD-14-124. NIH

**Alan O'C** (12:47:33):
> Thanks!


## 2021-05-15

**Belinda Phipson** (04:56:49):
> Hi! I'm not sure where to ask this question or who to ask, but I was wondering whether it is possible to obtain the number of monthly visitors to a particular package's page? I'm trying to collate some "impact" statistics about the minfi and missMethyl packages.

**Belinda Phipson** (07:35:09) (in thread):
> Thanks Kevin, I’ve already downloaded all those statistics (avg # distinct IPs per month, rank etc). I guess I was thinking more of whether traffic to package landing pages is tracked.

**Martin Morgan** (09:35:50) (in thread):
> We don’t publish landing page views, sorry. The unique IP download traffic is probably useful in a relative way (my package 1 is downloaded more frequently than my package 2…) but still quite problematic for a measure of ‘use’ for a number of reasons. Web page traffic would I think be even more problematic.


## 2021-05-16

**Belinda Phipson** (01:49:43) (in thread):
> Thanks for replying Martin. It feels like a tricky task to assess/estimate the user base of software packages.

**Jovana Maksimovic** (01:13:15) (in thread):

**Dario Strbenac** (02:00:14):
> Does anyone have a script for spiltting a FASTQ file by lane and flowcell into separate FASTQ files? The service provider delivered 100x whole genome sequencing samples concatenated into one file per read side, per sample, but I want to have meaningful`@RG`IDs that we had from the previous sequencing service provider.

**Sean Davis** (09:37:15) (in thread):
> I don’t think there is a standard for how read names are formatted, so you may have to create a quick script yourself. In Bioconductor, take a look at`ShortRead::FastqStreamer`. Inside the yield, you can segregate reads and concatenate to separate files.

**Alexander Toenges** (09:57:14) (in thread):
> Feel free to ask at[biostars.org](http://biostars.org)incl an example how data look like right now. My gutfeeling says that there is a command from BBtools that can do that efficiently.

**Hervé Pagès** (14:23:56) (in thread):
> Also if you look at the commit history of a long time Bioconductor package like***Biobase***, you'll see that all the commits from the SVN era have a`git-svn-id`line at the bottom:
> ```
> git-svn-id: file:///home/git/hedgehog.fhcrc.org/bioconductor/trunk/madman/Rpacks/Biobase@129350 bc3139a8-67e5-0310-9ffc-ced21a209358
> ```
> The last commit to have this line for***Biobase***is commit 536f1371 from May 2, 2017. The next commit (from Oct 30, 2017) no longer has a`git-svn-id`line.

**Henry Miller** (15:05:07):
> @Henry Miller has joined the channel

**Krithika Bhuvanesh** (17:22:23) (in thread):
> Which machine is this ? I think Illumina may have a tool for this ? They have a tool to convert he BCL files into FASTQ files . You could also reach out to your service provider to provide the FASTQ files in the format you prefer.

**Dario Strbenac** (19:00:10) (in thread):
> Thanks for good suggestions.`FastqStreamer`might be a convenient high-level function to use in Rscript. I have seen[a similar question with a similar answer](https://www.biostars.org/p/9470487/)on Biostars but I was not sure how to adapt Frédéric Mahé's awk code to a more general case since I have only used awk a couple of times before and am very much a beginner. I think Illumina FASTQ files contain fairly standard read names. I checked with the service provider and they didn't keep the separate FASTQ files, so they would need to convert BCL to FASTQ again and it's for 100x coverage whole genome sequencing. I think I could split the files quicker than a BCL to FASTQ conversion would take.

**Mike Smith** (04:12:54):

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (05:30:14):

## 2021-06-06

**Dario Strbenac** (22:00:20):
> A student showed me an example of R consistently crashing. I don't work with single-cell data, so I don't have experience with this, but what's the solution to this?
> ```
> > mat <- counts(sceATAC)
> > class(mat)
> [1] "dgTMatrix"
> > keep <- Matrix::rowSums(mat == 0) > 1000
> 
>  ***** caught segfault *****
> address 0x7f123daf63c0, cause 'memory not mapped'
> 
> Traceback:
>  1: .Call(dgeMatrix_colsums, x, na.rm, FALSE, FALSE)
>  2: .local(x, na.rm, dims, ...)
>  3: rowSums(x = x)
>  4: rowSums(x = x)
> ```

**Dario Strbenac** (22:05:36):
> I think that addresses a different question. I think she's looking for rows with at least 1000 zeros.
> ```
> > dim(mat)
> [1] 190937  34774
> ```

**Dario Strbenac** (22:21:08):
> `temp <- mat == 0`only uses 30 GB on a 768 GB RAM server and the error isn't triggered there. Applying`rowSums`triggers the error and only 103 GB is used with about 400 GB free. I think I can now make a reproducible example.

**Chengyang Ji** (23:56:09):
> It says here[https://stackoverflow.com/a/50163562](https://stackoverflow.com/a/50163562)the compiler incompabilities can cause segfault. Since`Matrix`is a built-in package, maybe an upgrade could help?
  - Attachment (Stack Overflow): [caught segfault - 'memory not mapped' error in R](https://stackoverflow.com/a/50163562)
> I have a problem running some R scripts on our cluster. The problems appeared suddenly (all the scripts were working just fine but one day they started giving a caught segfault error). I cannot pro...


## 2021-06-07

**Chengyang Ji** (00:00:06):
> The souce code for the culprit function`dgeMatrix_colsums`looks really innocent and couldn't possibly go wrong (I think)[https://github.com/cran/Matrix/blob/65c37738919156f6bbb682a1d8198d715a82a19f/src/dgeMatrix.c#L839](https://github.com/cran/Matrix/blob/65c37738919156f6bbb682a1d8198d715a82a19f/src/dgeMatrix.c#L839)

**Stuart Lee** (00:11:59) (in thread):
> `sparseMatrixStats::rowCounts`might be useful here

**Alan O'C** (05:26:13):
> OOM has never segfaulted for me. I would tend towards updating packages as well, that usually fixes those memory not mapped errors when I'm working on the HPC

**Kasper D. Hansen** (07:35:29):
> Running our of memoery is different from a segfault

**Mike Smith** (11:17:37):
> I agree.  If this was an out of memory error I'd expect to see`Error: cannot allocate vector of size XX Gb`

**USLACKBOT** (11:20:58):
> This message was deleted.

**Michael Lawrence** (12:06:01):
> Probably just integer overflow

**Mike Smith** (12:35:10) (in thread):
> One option might be to do`temp <- as(mat, "lsparseMatrix")`which I think creates a logical sparse matrix is the equivalent of`mat == 0 `and then doing`sparseMatrixStats::rowSums2( temp ) > 1000`.  That way everything should stay sparse.

**Hervé Pagès** (13:14:18) (in thread):
> A program cannot try to allocate memory that is not available. Memory allocation is done via system calls like`malloc()`,`calloc()`,`realloc()`, and AFAIK these system calls won't segfault if there's not enough memory available. What they will do in this case is return a NULL pointer instead of the address of the allocated memory and it's the responsibility of the program to check that. However poorly written C code doesn't always check that and then tries to write to the NULL address which causes a segfault. So yeah, using a system with more memory will avoid running into that problem but the program is buggy nonetheless and should be fixed.

**Henrik Bengtsson** (14:31:11) (in thread):
> Please report this to the maintainer of***Matrix***(Martin Mächler), because this is a (serious) bug in***Matrix***- anything that causes R to segfault is a bug.  Don't forget to report`sessionInfo()`.

**Mike Smith** (06:52:29):
> Does anyone know when/where the {packname} convention for mentioning R packages started?  I swear I hadn't seen it before about 2 weeks ago,  and now my twitter feed is full of {knitr} and {ggplot2} etc. Is it just a twitter thing?

**Lluís Revilla** (06:57:30):
> I think it was started by Colin Fay he explained in a tweet he took it from the help pages, on the top left corner the package name is with curly parenthesis:`usethis-package {usethis}`,`select {dplyr}`,`org.Hs.eg.db {org.Hs.eg.db}`, ...

**Mike Smith** (08:30:21):
> Well, he tweets a lot!  But here were are:[https://twitter.com/_ColinFay/status/934740425956569088?s=20](https://twitter.com/_ColinFay/status/934740425956569088?s=20)Definitely not just the last two weeks.
  - Attachment (twitter): [Attachment](https://twitter.com/_ColinFay/status/934740425956569088?s=20)
> [@dataandme](https://twitter.com/dataandme) [@juliasilge](https://twitter.com/juliasilge) [@drob](https://twitter.com/drob) This is the how it is written on the online R help :) 
> I find it helpful to make it clear I'm referring to a package :package: [https://pbs.twimg.com/media/DPjddcpWsAE0NFh.jpg](https://pbs.twimg.com/media/DPjddcpWsAE0NFh.jpg)

**Mike Smith** (08:30:58):
> Took me a while to realise that was the HTML help.  It looks different in a terminal
  - File (PNG): [image.png](https://community-bioc.slack.com/files/U34P8RS3B/F024W9JSX1C/image.png)

**Alan O'C** (08:31:53):
> Yeah I did not recognise it for the same reason

> @mahmod suliman has joined the channel

**GuandongShang** (23:40:09):
> Hi, I am writing @examples in my packages, and loading TxDb.Athaliana.BioMart.plantsmart28 in examples in every function. But I think each library(TxDb.Athaliana.BioMart.plantsmart28) will take some times, so I am wondering whether some one can give me some advice about it.
> 
> my examples code like it
> function 1
> ```
> #' @examples
> #' data("RNA_normCount")
> #' data("ATAC_normCount")
> #'
> #' library(TxDb.Athaliana.BioMart.plantsmart28)
> #' Txdb <- TxDb.Athaliana.BioMart.plantsmart28
> #' seqlevels(Txdb) <- c(paste0("Chr", 1:5), "M", "C")
> ```
> function 2
> ```
> #' @examples
> #' data("ATAC_normCount")
> #' data("input_genes")
> #' library(TxDb.Athaliana.BioMart.plantsmart28)
> #' Txdb <- TxDb.Athaliana.BioMart.plantsmart28
> #' seqlevels(Txdb) <- c(paste0("Chr", 1:5), "M", "C")
> #'
> #' peak_path <- system.file("extdata", "ATAC.bed.gz", package = "FindIT2")
> #' peak_GR <- loadPeakFile(peak_path)
> #'
> #' ChIP_peak_path <- system.file("extdata", "ChIP.bed.gz", package = "FindIT2")
> #' ChIP_peak_GR <- loadPeakFile(ChIP_peak_path)
> #' ChIP_peak_GR$TF_id <- "AT1G28300"
> ```


## 2021-06-16

**Dario Strbenac** (02:00:09):
> You shouldn't immediately load packages in examples because data packages should be in`Suggests:`part of DESCRIPTION file and might not be installed on a user's computer unless they did a comprehensive install. Examples should test if the package is available before doing some analysis.
> ```
> if(require(dataPackage))
> {
>   # Do analysis.
> }
> ```
> See Section 2.8 of[Package Development Guidelines](http://bioconductor.org/developers/package-guidelines/)for information.

**Mike Smith** (04:25:53):
> I agree with Dario that`require()`is more appropriate, and that you want the package load statement in each example to make it clear to the user what's required.
> 
> I think it's also worth noting that when the examples are tested in`R CMD check`they're actually all concatenated together into a single file before being run.  So even if you have lots of repeated`require()`or`library()`calls across many examples, you don't suffer from excessive load times during the package check process.
> 
> You can verify this by taking a look in the`<PKG>.Rcheck`folder produced by`R CMD check`, where there should be a file called`<PKG>-Ex.R`that contains all your examples.

**GuandongShang** (04:26:39) (in thread):
> Thanks for your suggestion ! This is helpful. I am correcting it

**GuandongShang** (04:27:27) (in thread):
> got it. Thanks for your reply, I will see the Rcheck.

**Kasper D. Hansen** (04:27:46):
> You could consider the concatenation to be a bug however

**GuandongShang** (04:29:26):
> I am sorry, what's the meaning of`concatenation to be a bug`?

**Kasper D. Hansen** (04:33:30):
> The fact that R - at the moment - concatenates all examples into a single R which is then executed, is something I claim you could consider a bug or at least not a great design. You can argue that having a fresh environment for each example is much better, but this will also be much slower

**GuandongShang** (04:35:51):
> Thanks, I get it : ).

**Kasper D. Hansen** (04:36:13):
> This is more of a meta comment. What concerns you is that repeated calls does not slow down checking - at the moment.

**Kasper D. Hansen** (04:36:32):
> And you should follow the`require`advice

**Mike Smith** (05:27:29):
> I think what I said previously holds true, but R does run the function`cleanEx()`between each example.  That function doesn't seem to be documented anywhere (I think it's this[https://github.com/wch/r-source/blob/79298c499218846d14500255efd622b5021c10ec/share/R/examples-header.R#L37-L75](https://github.com/wch/r-source/blob/79298c499218846d14500255efd622b5021c10ec/share/R/examples-header.R#L37-L75)), so it's a bit mysterious.  At first read it looks to me like it will remove created variables, and detach any loaded packages, but not unload them.

**Kasper D. Hansen** (05:38:28):
> interesting

**Heather Turner** (08:18:12):
> :wave:Hi Folks! We are looking for volunteers to serve as a Zoom Host at useR! 2021 (5-9 July).
> 
> What does a Zoom Host do? Manage the running of talk or tutorial sessions, to take the technical burden off the session chair or tutor. You will work in pairs and be responsible for a certain time block between you (~8 hours).
> 
> You will be given training by experienced community organizers in the week of June 21st. You need to be available to run conference rehearsals one week before the conference, at exactly the same time as during the conference.
> 
> Since this is quite a commitment, Zoom Hosts are offered***a stipend of USD 100***for their help.  We're also happy to offer**** conference fee waivers**for volunteers if required  ([https://user2021.r-project.org/participation/faq/#waiver](https://user2021.r-project.org/participation/faq/#waiver)).
> 
> Interested? Please fill in this doodle asap![https://doodle.com/poll/mn56u8qne5uwqerr](https://doodle.com/poll/mn56u8qne5uwqerr). It would be helpful if you fill it out with "your name - Bioconductor" to know which community you're coming from:bioc:

**Heather Turner** (08:23:32):
> We're looking for ~50 hosts to cover the whole schedule, so please still respond even though several have already volunteered!

**Hervé Pagès** (12:55:48) (in thread):
> I didn't know about the`cleanEx()`feature and I wonder how long it's been around. I remember seeing some packages with examples failing in the context of`R CMD check`not so long ago where  trying to run the problematic examples interactively would not trigger the error. After some digging around, this situation turned out to be a side-effect of`R CMD check`concatenating all the examples in one single file. These events are rare and`cleanEx()`probably helps making them even more rare but it's not perfect either. More generally speaking I don't think there's an easy way to reset the state of an R session to a clean slate except by restarting R.
