## 2021-06-20

**Vince Carey** (18:39:41) (in thread):
> @Marcel Ramos Pérezit looks like you've done the last non-trivial changes to interactiveDisplayBase.  To get this to work with[shinyapps.io](http://shinyapps.io), as far as I can tell, we need to have a function that hands back the list(ui, server) that interactiveDisplayBase produces.  The relevant function seems to be .dataFrame3, which is not exported.  So we'll need to discuss what should be exported from the package so that@Kozo Nishidacan achieve his aim.


## 2021-06-21

**Steve** (05:55:13):
> I built a site where to make it easy for clients or students to book you for consulting or R tuition by the hour. It’s called Hangswith. I’m cultivating a modern and intellectual culture (as opposed to other sites which can be ‘fluffy’ in nature). Pease check it out if you have a moment, feel free to create a profile if you feel so inclined! (it’s completely free to sign up / create a profile).[https://www.hangswith.com/](https://www.hangswith.com/)All feedback very welcome. I’m learning daily!

**Sebastian Worms** (06:02:43):
> That's sounds nice! I was considering signing up on upwork to do similar things. What do you see as your platform's edge? Being more people focued and less task focused?

**Steve** (06:13:51):
> That’s a great question, I’m figuring that out myself:slightly_smiling_face:I’d say the two strongest bits of feedback I’ve had so far is
> 1. More honest/transparent pricing than similar sites, which hit both sides of the transaction with hidden fees.
> 2. It’s been great in cases where someone receives a lot of inquiries, and ends up doing a lot of unpaid work (via email etc) - hangswith lets you take a booking straight over the web, and have it paid for by the client/student up front. In other words, instead of 10 inquiries leading to 1 hour of actual work, you only get notified**after**someone’s already booked and paid. That puts more responsibility on the consultant/tutor, but relives them of all the rigmarole arranging it. Although they do have to send a zoom link, but that’s not a huge burden:slightly_smiling_face:

**Marcel Ramos Pérez** (09:58:43) (in thread):
> Yes, I can look into this. One thing about[shinyapps.io](http://shinyapps.io)is that the monthly compute hours are limited so if the app gets quite some use, we may have to think about alternative solutions for hosting.

**Marcel Ramos Pérez** (12:22:42) (in thread):
> Here is a rough draft of the[shinyapps.io](http://shinyapps.io)hosted app.[https://mramos.shinyapps.io/AnnotationHubShiny/](https://mramos.shinyapps.io/AnnotationHubShiny/)The send button doesn't work because it is hosted on a server which we don't have access to other than for running the app. I could look into adding a panel for the data.frame return after clicking`Send`.

**Marcel Ramos Pérez** (12:28:36) (in thread):
> see the source repository here[https://github.com/LiNk-NY/AnnotationHubShiny](https://github.com/LiNk-NY/AnnotationHubShiny)

**Vince Carey** (19:12:06) (in thread):
> We could use a download control instead of the send-to-session action.  I will have a look and try to produce a PR

**Stephanie Hicks** (07:36:14):
> This seems useful[https://twitter.com/natfriedman/status/1420122675813441540](https://twitter.com/natfriedman/status/1420122675813441540)
  - Attachment (twitter): [Attachment](https://twitter.com/natfriedman/status/1420122675813441540)
> We’ve just added built-in citation support to GitHub so researchers and scientists can more easily receive acknowledgments for their contributions to software.
> 
> Just push a CITATION.cff file and we’ll add a handy widget to the repo sidebar for you.
> 
> Enjoy! :tada: [https://pbs.twimg.com/media/E7VJ5FbWUAYithw.jpg](https://pbs.twimg.com/media/E7VJ5FbWUAYithw.jpg)

**Alan O'C** (08:42:42) (in thread):
> Sadly it's just to cite the actual repo so far, can't have a paper in there. Which for R packages means you may get people citing the R package (before CITATION is added), people citing the paper, and people citing the repo

**Stephanie Hicks** (10:13:04) (in thread):
> oh wow, seriously??

**Stephanie Hicks** (10:13:28) (in thread):
> that’s not helpful

**Alan O'C** (10:14:19) (in thread):
> Unless I misunderstand, yeah. I went from 100 to 0 pretty fast. I think the idea is you archive a version (which version???) in Zenodo and then people cite that...[https://docs.github.com/en/github/creating-cloning-and-archiving-repositories/creating-a-repository-on-github/about-citation-files#citation-formats](https://docs.github.com/en/github/creating-cloning-and-archiving-repositories/creating-a-repository-on-github/about-citation-files#citation-formats)

**Alan O'C** (10:16:35) (in thread):

  - Attachment (twitter): [Attachment](https://twitter.com/stdruskat/status/1420289082211672064?s=20)
> [@arfon](https://twitter.com/arfon) [@OriolAbril](https://twitter.com/OriolAbril) [@diehlpk](https://twitter.com/diehlpk) [@JOSS_TheOJ](https://twitter.com/JOSS_TheOJ) Yes, the shortly forthcoming version 1.2.0 of CFF will introduce a `preferred-citation` field which can hold just that, a preferred citation (such as a JOSS paper). This way you won't have to hack the `message` field to provide this information, although it will also be slightly

> I am having P.faliciparum genomes sequenced using sanger and i would like to do variant calling. How do I go about it.

**Darin Takemoto** (15:52:57) (in thread):
> With the disclaimer that I haven't worked with Sanger sequencing data in over a decade, it would depend on what the issues you are having with variant calling and why. If it is detection of low-frequency variants that don't fit the normal diploid model, there are variant callers made for cancer genomics and viral sequencing that can detect lower-frequency variants (this white paper suggests down to 5%):[https://assets.thermofisher.com/TFS-Assets/LSG/Application-Notes/variant-detection-tumor-ffpe-samples-sanger-sequencing.pdf](https://assets.thermofisher.com/TFS-Assets/LSG/Application-Notes/variant-detection-tumor-ffpe-samples-sanger-sequencing.pdf)

**Darin Takemoto** (15:54:44) (in thread):
> If the issue is more heterogeneity, one would have to deconvolute the trace, which is something I personally have never done. Here is a paper describing software to do this for ABI Sanger sequencing data:[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3557274/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3557274/)
  - Attachment (PubMed Central (PMC)): [Base-Calling Algorithm with Vocabulary (BCV) Method for Analyzing Population Sequencing Chromatograms](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3557274/)
> Sanger sequencing is a common method of reading DNA sequences. It is less expensive than high-throughput methods, and it is appropriate for numerous applications including molecular diagnostics. However, sequencing mixtures of similar DNA of pathogens ...

**Darin Takemoto** (15:55:44) (in thread):
> The BCV software package itself is here:[http://bioinf.fbb.msu.ru:8080/bcviss/index/index.gsp](http://bioinf.fbb.msu.ru:8080/bcviss/index/index.gsp)

## 2021-08-06

**Wanmin Dai** (01:08:24):
> @Wanmin Dai has joined the channel

**Dario Strbenac** (02:00:26):
> Have I been thinking about S4 method dispatch wrongly all these years?
> ```
> setGeneric("aClass", function(x, y, ...) {standardGeneric("aClass")})
> setMethod("aClass", numeric(0), function() {message("Empty")})
> setMethod("aClass", c("numeric", "character"), function(x, y = "G'day") {message("Both")})
> > aClass(9)
> Empty
> ```
> How do I create the empty constructor for an S4 class that's only dispatched if the constructor the user specifies is`aClass()`?

**Alan O'C** (03:23:22):
> `missing()`?

**Alan O'C** (03:23:34):
> or rather,`"missing"`?

**Dario Strbenac** (05:00:05):
> Ah, true.

**Hervé Pagès** (09:18:29) (in thread):
> ```
> setMethod("aClass", c("missing", "missing"), function(x, y) message("Empty"))
> ```
> 2 notes:
> 1. It's better to not put curly brackets around the call to`standardGeneric()`. This allows R to identify the function as a**standard generic**(with the curly brackets, R identifies the function as a non-standard generic).
> 2. All your methods must at least have the`x`and`y`arguments. Also, for clarity, the signature of all the methods should be a length-2 character vector.

**Jenny Drnevich** (10:32:46):
> Poll: how do you pronounce "tSNE"? Tiz-ny rhymes with Disney, or tee-sin-ee? I first learned the former but then saw 10X people saying the latter, but I've seen more of the former at Bioc2021. Anybody know if there is a definitive authority on the pronunciation?

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (10:35:13):
> i think it should really be tee-ess-en-eee even though I tend to say tee-snee

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (10:36:38):
> But I’ve heard every possible way I think, and I don’t know where to find the definitive authority:sweat_smile:

**Jenny Drnevich** (10:38:01):
> I could abuse my authority and put up a poll in the Single Cell Genomics Talk but I think we will be short on time.

**Alexander Toenges** (10:38:03):
> tee-snee, that is how the developer pronounces it, hear him at 5:08 of[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bCkXxaoeFZ8&t=308s](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bCkXxaoeFZ8&t=308s)
  - Attachment (YouTube): [BioTuring Webinar | Using t SNE for Data Analysis: A Quick Introduction | Laurens van der Maaten](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bCkXxaoeFZ8&amp;t=308s)

**Jenny Drnevich** (10:45:46):
> Although I admit I find tiz-ny more fun and easier to say.:face_with_hand_over_mouth:

**Kevin Blighe** (10:58:39):
> I usually say T S N E, but just now on the presentation I think that I said tiz-nee:expressionless:

**Luke Zappia** (11:07:56):
> Hidden advantage of UMAP, much easier to pronounce. I am yet to hear someone say um-ap but it’s probably only a matter of time.

**Jenny Drnevich** (11:11:46):
> Sadly, I think Bioconductor people will forever mispronounce BiocSwirl as "bio-cee" is hardwired into our brains. The developers pronounce it "bi-ock" swirl.

**Jared Andrews** (11:25:23):
> I had never heard the "bio-cee" pronunciation until yesterday, I always went with "bi-ock".

**Jenny Drnevich** (11:27:10):
> Maybe it's just me??? "bi-ock" is closer to "bio-konductor"

## 2021-08-10

**Woo** (08:12:24):
> @Woo has joined the channel

**Hervé Pagès** (13:54:42) (in thread):
> I'm curious what's the benefits of having this dual setup. Is R 4.1 for M1 really that faster than R 4.1 for intel? Only drawback I see is that you need to download and install many packages twice.

**Ambarish S. Ghatpande** (14:39:45) (in thread):
> It’s not such a big deal at all. I saw a few queries on SO and maybe the bioc support forum with people asking for workarounds.  People with m1 macs shouldn’t feel like they can’t use Bioconductor, that’s all. 
>  In terms of difference, the memory use seems to be quite different between the two versions The one needing Rosetta seems to hog much more of it. 
> Honestly I hadn’t thought of having to install many packages twice, although most R packages seem small in size compared to hard drive capacity.

**Hervé Pagès** (15:28:04) (in thread):
> > People with m1 macs shouldn’t feel like they can’t use Bioconductor, that’s all.
> Not sure why people with m1 macs would feel that way. Just to clarify Bioconductor is 100% compatible with m1 macs as long as you install R 4.1 for intel.
> 
> I'm really surprised by your claim that R 4.1 for intel seems to hog much more memory. I would expect maybe a slight difference in speed, even though not something really significant. But a big difference in memory consumption is unexpected and hard to explain.


## 2021-08-11

**Ambarish S. Ghatpande** (10:53:00) (in thread):

  - File (PNG): [Screen Shot 2021-08-11 at 10.42.35 AM.png](https://community-bioc.slack.com/files/U34P8RS3B/F02B2AF3U2Y/screen_shot_2021-08-11_at_10.42.35_am.png)

**Ambarish S. Ghatpande** (10:57:31) (in thread):
> the stats in the 2 screenshots above is what I meant. Note the memory used by the “session” under the x86 platform is close to 1 GB whereas the same under the aarch64 platform is ~ 226 MB.  Not really sure we can compare speed since the most relevant packages (Bioconductor based) dont work un the aarch64 platform. Like I said, could be acrcane stuff, not really a big deal for a user like me.

## 2021-08-12

**Hervé Pagès** (14:46:15) (in thread):
> Yes that's a big difference! Interesting. It's still hard for me to believe that this is only due to the different architectures or to a Rosetta inefficiency. I can see a lot of discussion and benchmarks on internet for Intel+Rosetta vs M1 but didn't see anything about difference in memory consumption. This tends to suggest that maybe what you see is an R/RStudio thing even though I didn't see anything like this reported on the R-SIG-Mac mailing list (didn't check the RStudio mailing lists/forums). Could it be that something changed between R 4.1.0 and R 4.1.0 Patched r80661 that explains this? It would certainly deserve a little bit more investigation.

**Levi Waldron** (17:32:13) (in thread):
> That’s all there is, but the pkgdown page ([https://waldronlab.io/HGNChelper/](https://waldronlab.io/HGNChelper/)) does also have the man pages for the other functions. I think the other functions besides checkGeneSymbols are mostly of historical curiosity, but they also seem harmless enough not to warrant deprecation. I could be convinced to add something to the vignette or to deprecate them if you want to make a case for it…
  - Attachment (waldronlab.io): [Identify and Correct Invalid HGNC Human Gene Symbols and MGI Mouse Gene Symbols](https://waldronlab.io/HGNChelper/)
> Contains functions for identifying and correcting HGNC human gene symbols and MGI mouse gene symbols which have been converted to date format by Excel, withdrawn, or aliased. Also contains functions for reversibly converting between HGNC symbols and valid R names.

Since [@natfriedman](https://twitter.com/natfriedman)'s tweet a few weeks ago, we’ve been making a number of improvements including detecting BibTeX files, R (inst/CITATION) files, and citing papers. Check out the docs to learn more: [docs.github.com/en/github/crea](http://docs.github.com/en/github/crea)… [twitter.com/github/status/](http://twitter.com/github/status/)…
  - Attachment (twitter): [Attachment](https://twitter.com/github/status/1428395360842665990)
> We've added enhanced citation support so academics and researchers can easily receive acknowledgments for their contributions to software with citation links in the common APA or BibTeX styles.
> 
> [github.blog/2021-08-19-enh](http://github.blog/2021-08-19-enh)…

**Jenny Drnevich** (11:28:23) (in thread):
> I knew that`->`existed but never really saw it in practice before.

**James MacDonald** (11:29:48) (in thread):
> It's a logical extension of mgrittr (sp?) pipes, where you do
> ```
> this %>% that %>% the_other
> ```
> And the result is the last thing piped thataway ->

**Jenny Drnevich** (11:35:47) (in thread):
> I agree that it's a logical extension and even teach it as a "fun fact" in my intro R when I teach pipes. But then I say the convention it to always put the assignment at the beginning of the line of code.

**James MacDonald** (11:35:55) (in thread):
> Although if you want to go full tidyverse, isn't it really
> ```
> huh %<>% this %>% that %>% the_other
> ```

**James MacDonald** (11:36:34) (in thread):
> Which I believe pipes everything back into the`huh`object? Obviously?

**Jenny Drnevich** (11:36:42) (in thread):
> Never go full tidyverse!

**James MacDonald** (11:37:01) (in thread):
> No, I've seen Tropic Thunder. I know better than that.

**Alan O'C** (11:57:29) (in thread):
> ```
> apply(
>   mat,
>   2,
>   function(x) stuff(x)
> ) -> variable
> ```

**Alan O'C** (11:57:48) (in thread):
> That is so cursed

**Martin Morgan** (12:04:18) (in thread):
> `x <- 2 -> y`is also fun for this — kind of splitting a number between two variables, like the atom…

**Alan O'C** (12:05:06) (in thread):
> I prefer to do`2 -> x <- 3`:laughing:

**Simina Boca** (13:36:07) (in thread):
> I wonder if folks who write right-to-left have a preference? (eg Hebrew, Arabic, Farsi speakers/writers)

## 2021-09-08

**Mikhail Dozmorov** (12:47:00):
> I may be missing something obvious, but why the following regexp returns TRUE for the second string?
> ```
> strings <- c('abd', 'dabc', 'abcabc')
> grepl("a*d", strings)
> #> [1]  TRUE  TRUE FALSE
> ```
> And why the following gives all TRUEs?
> ```
> grepl("d*a", strings)
> #> [1] TRUE TRUE TRUE
> ```
> Happened during the live demo in class, and I have no explanation why.

**Aaron Lun** (12:48:37):
> well, the last string doesn't have a`d`, but all strings have an`a`.

**Mikhail Dozmorov** (13:01:03) (in thread):
> I considered “d*a” regexp as a substring starting with “d” and ending with “a”, with any characters in between. Is it not correct?

**Alan O'C** (13:01:44) (in thread):
> `d*a`means "zero or more instances of d, followed by one a"

**Alan O'C** (13:02:40) (in thread):
> `d.*a`is "d, then zero or more of any character, then a"

**Mikhail Dozmorov** (13:03:33) (in thread):
> Ah, the “zero” part is where I blanked:slightly_smiling_face:Lessons learned. All correct, thanks,@Alan O'C!

**Alan O'C** (13:04:18) (in thread):
> No worries, behaviour of`*`is a bit different in regex vs globs:slightly_smiling_face:

**Sean Davis** (13:13:06) (in thread):
> Great thread. Thanks!

**Marcel Ramos Pérez** (13:31:56) (in thread):
> "starting with d and ending with a with any characters in between" corresponds to`"^d.*a$"`

**Alan O'C** (14:39:45) (in thread):
> In case anybody is bored, I find this fun (I'm stuck at abba, though)[https://alf.nu/RegexGolf](https://alf.nu/RegexGolf)

**Mikhail Dozmorov** (14:59:35) (in thread):
> Added to my references:
> * [Curated Regular Expression Resources]([https://paulvanderlaken.com/2020/04/07/curated-regular-expression-resources/](https://paulvanderlaken.com/2020/04/07/curated-regular-expression-resources/))
> * [Learn regex the easy way]([https://github.com/ziishaned/learn-regex](https://github.com/ziishaned/learn-regex))
> * [All about Regular Expressions]([https://www.regular-expressions.info](https://www.regular-expressions.info))
> * [Preview your regex]([https://regexr.com/](https://regexr.com/))
> * [Play Regex Golf]([https://alf.nu/RegexGolf](https://alf.nu/RegexGolf))

## 2021-09-28

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (10:14:54):
> Writing a side point about bioconductor in a lesson. Does anyone remember what happens upon acceptance of a new package that was submitted with a major version different from 0 ? Does it get incremented by 1 or is the version forced to 1.0.0 ?

**Laurent Gatto** (10:22:13):
> As far as I know, new submissions should always be 0.99.z and any updates increment z.

**Kasper D. Hansen** (10:25:41):
> If you’re teaching this, I think the lesson is this: if you want to use Bioc just accept and use the package version numbering scheme we use. There are many philosophies around version numbers, but forget about it and just embrace the Bioconductor way.  Specifically, you should NOT follow the recommendations in “R Packages” by Wickham.

**Martin Morgan** (10:41:29):
> I think`x.99.z`gets added to devel as-is, and bumped to`x+1.0.0`at the release. Package versions can never go backward, so if say your package had been on CRAN or GitHub or otherwise distributed at version 3.1-2 your only choice is`3.99.0`… If you use`0.99.0`then`install.packages()`(which is what`BiocManager::install()`uses) would say ’oh, there’s a newer version installed, so I won’t update.

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (10:45:39):
> thanks both

**Laurent Gatto** (11:34:30) (in thread):
> Given the name requirement, would it even be possible to submit a CRAN package to Bioconductor?

**Martin Morgan** (12:35:56) (in thread):
> Usually it’s moving a CRAN package to Bioconductor, which involves submitting / review / accepting under Bioconductor standards, then notifying CRAN to remove the package. So it’s available in CRAN but not Bioconductor before acceptance by Bioconductor, and Bioconductor but not CRAN after acceptance.
> 
> This is how it works in practice, but I wouldn’t say that I endorse changing repositories as a good thing to do.

**Chang Yin** (15:35:48):
> @Chang Yin has joined the channel

**Mahmoud Ahmed** (23:09:49):

## 2021-11-18

**Sara Yones** (09:57:49):
> @Sara Yones has joined the channel

**Vince Carey** (11:07:49):
> pretty quiet here.  back to regular expressions?  given
> ```
> > names(cs)
>  [1] "Case ID"                    "Secondary \nor Recurrent"  
>  [3] "Age at \nProcedure"         "Gender"                    
>  [5] "Path Dx"                    "MGMT Status"               
>  [7] "2012 \nMethylation\n Class" "G-CIMP\n methylation"      
>  [9] "IDH1\n status"              "Expression\nSubclass"      
> [11] "Therapy Class"              "Vital Status"              
> [13] "OS (days)"                  "Progression Status"        
> [15] "PFS (days)"                 "V16"
> ```
> produce
> ```
> [1] "Case_ID"                "Secondary_or_Recurrent" "Age_at_Procedure"      
>  [4] "Gender"                 "Path_Dx"                "MGMT_Status"           
>  [7] "2012_Methylation_Class" "G_CIMP_methylation"     "IDH1_status"           
> [10] "Expression_Subclass"    "Therapy_Class"          "Vital_Status"          
> [13] "OS_days"                "Progression_Status"     "PFS_days"              
> [16] "V16"
> ```
> My solution involves 2 gsubs.

**Alan O'C** (11:11:36) (in thread):
> I would probably just`gsub`on`make.names`, although that's probably not in the spirit of the question

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (11:20:41) (in thread):
> @Vince Carey: Can you give the output of`cat(deparse(names(cs)))`so that we can recreate the vector easily in an R session please?

**Alan O'C** (11:21:25) (in thread):
> Aye, I was gonna suggest`dput`

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (11:21:54) (in thread):
> ohh... didn't know that one!

**Vince Carey** (11:22:00):
> ```
> c("Case ID", "Secondary \nor Recurrent", "Age at \nProcedure", 
> "Gender", "Path Dx", "MGMT Status", "2012 \nMethylation\n Class", 
> "G-CIMP\n methylation", "IDH1\n status", "Expression\nSubclass", 
> "Therapy Class", "Vital Status", "OS (days)", "Progression Status", 
> "PFS (days)", "V16")
> ```

**Vince Carey** (11:25:34) (in thread):
> @Alan O'CI hadn't thought of make.names ... it is certainly useful but produces lots of dots.

**Alan O'C** (11:28:45) (in thread):
> Yeah, the lazy way of sanitising is to do`gsub("\\.+", "_", make.names(x))`, although that leaves some trailing`_`and some leading`X`s

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (11:29:44) (in thread):
> with magrittr I got super close, with the issue of the prefix`X`introduced by`make.names()`:
> ```
> make.names(x) %>% gsub("^.", "", .) %>% gsub(".$", "", .) %>% gsub("[.]+", "_", .)
> ```
> There is only one failure for this approach: the field "2012_Methylation_Class" because it gets an "X" prefix

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (11:30:31) (in thread):
> my issue with that is that I'm not so keen to trim X's from the left, since some variables might have a genuine X there

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (11:32:26) (in thread):
> hold that though, I got an idea

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (11:34:39) (in thread):
> arf - not yet, maybe with a bit more time:sweat_smile:

**Marcel Ramos Pérez** (11:36:27):
> `gsub("\\s\\((\\w+)\\)|(\\s\\(**|\\s**\\\n\\s*)|(-)", "_\\1", names(cs))`

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (11:39:43) (in thread):
> OK, so... I wish I could say "tadaaaa":tada:but I'll admit that it is fairly convoluted:
> ```
> x %>%
>   paste0("_", .) %>%
>   make.names() %>%
>   gsub("^[.]", "", .) %>%
>   gsub("[.]$", "", .) %>%
>   gsub("[.]+", "_", .) %>%
>   gsub("^X_", "", .)
> ```

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (11:41:19) (in thread):
> Happy to explain the reason for individual bits if needed. I can't say that I'd be as happy to go down the "single regex" road for that one:laughing:

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (11:44:06) (in thread):
> why do I get`character(0)`when I run that?

**Marcel Ramos Pérez** (11:45:15) (in thread):
> You can try it on
> ```
> cs <- c("Case ID", "Secondary \nor Recurrent", "Age at \nProcedure", 
> "Gender", "Path Dx", "MGMT Status", "2012 \nMethylation\n Class", 
> "G-CIMP\n methylation", "IDH1\n status", "Expression\nSubclass", 
> "Therapy Class", "Vital Status", "OS (days)", "Progression Status", 
> "PFS (days)", "V16")
> ```

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (11:45:47) (in thread):
> ```
> > cs <- c("Case ID", "Secondary \nor Recurrent", "Age at \nProcedure", 
> + "Gender", "Path Dx", "MGMT Status", "2012 \nMethylation\n Class", 
> + "G-CIMP\n methylation", "IDH1\n status", "Expression\nSubclass", 
> + "Therapy Class", "Vital Status", "OS (days)", "Progression Status", 
> + "PFS (days)", "V16")
> > gsub("\\s\\((\\w+)\\)|(\\s\\(**|\\s**\\\n\\s*)|(-)", "_\\1", names(cs))
> character(0)
> ```

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (11:45:55) (in thread):
> weird, right?

**Marcel Ramos Pérez** (11:46:34) (in thread):
> No, there are no names. I was trying to emulate the original example. But you can also do
> ```
> cs <- setNames(nm = cs)
> ```

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (11:47:15) (in thread):
> omg, i'm blind:sweat_smile:

**Kevin Rue-Albrecht** (11:47:32) (in thread):
> working now ^^

**Vince Carey** (11:54:02):
> Wow.  I need to study that@Marcel Ramos Pérez.
> ```
> > all.equal( gsub("\\s\\((\\w+)\\)|(\\s\\(**|\\s**\\\n\\s*)|(-)", "*\\1", names(cs)), gsub("\\(|\\)", "", gsub("( )**(\\n| |-)( )**", "*", names(cs))))
> [1] TRUE
> ```

**Martin Morgan** (13:31:09):
> `sub("*$", "", gsub("[[:space:][:punct:]]+", "*", cs))`; these ‘character classes’ are described in`?regex`and can improve readability — match one or more characters from the set of characters including`[:space:]`or`[:punct:]`; the outer`sub()`replaces trailing`_`in entries like`OS (days)`there’s only one end of each entry, so`sub()`instead of`gsub()`. A variant is`gsub("[[:space:]]+", "_", gsub("[[:punct:]]+", "", cs))`Hmm, and taking@Michael Lawrence’s suggestion to heart, might as well make the intentions explicit, at least in english
> ```
> remove_punctuation <- function(x)
>     gsub("[[:punct:]]+", "", x)
> 
> replace_whitespace <- function(x, with)
>     gsub("[[:space:]]+", with, x)
> 
> cs |>
>     remove_punctuation() |>
>     replace_whitespace(with = "_")
> ```

**Michael Lawrence** (13:31:44) (in thread):
> I think it would be better code to be more explicit about the intent, i.e., using`strsplit()`and`unstrsplit()`.

**Peter Hickey** (16:16:23) (in thread):
> cheating by using a package, but`janitor::clean_names()`is great for this sort of task ([https://www.rdocumentation.org/packages/janitor/versions/1.2.0/topics/clean_names](https://www.rdocumentation.org/packages/janitor/versions/1.2.0/topics/clean_names))


## 2021-11-19

**Christian Arnold** (05:06:22):
> @Christian Arnold has joined the channel

**Vince Carey** (08:51:18) (in thread):
> A defect here is that "-" is mapped to "", so we get "GCIMP" instead of "G_CIMP"

**takashi watanabe** (09:07:22):
> @takashi watanabe has joined the channel

**Martin Morgan** (10:17:28) (in thread):
> bummer, I guess one loses the use of`[:punct:]`and gains the use of escaped parentheses`\\(`
> ```
> remove_parentheses <- function(x)
>     gsub("[\\(\\)]+", "", x)
> 
> replace_whitespace_and_dash <- function(x, with)
>     gsub("[-[:space:]]+", with, x)
>     ## alternative pattern: "[- \n]+"
> 
> cs |>
>     remove_parentheses() |>
>     replace_whitespace_and_dash(with  = "_")
> ```
> Also using the stringr package
> ```
> cs |>
>     str_remove_all("[\\(\\)]") |>
>     str_replace_all("[- \n]+", "_")
> ```
> I guess this is just Vince’s two-gsub solution with maybe a little cleaner(?) regular expressions…

**Martin Morgan** (10:38:53) (in thread):
> I wonder if using R string literals`r"{...}"`would make this any more readable?
> ```
> > re = r"{\s\((\w+)\)|(\s\(**|\s**\n\s*)|(-)}"
> > gsub(re, "_\\1", names(cs))
> ```
> Maybe not!

Also, are there any example posts where the`yeastNagalakshmi`package is used? Much thanks in advance.

**Pablo Rodriguez** (06:29:45):
> Question for the people behind`BiocParallel`: the progressbar is gone, instead there is a message that says "iteration:" plus a number (that i assume is related to some step inside the function one parallelizes. Is this intended?
> 
> Example:
> ```
> > fun <- function(v) {
> +     message("working") ## 10 tasks
> +     sqrt(v)
> + }
> > bplapply(1:10, fun, BPPARAM=MulticoreParam(workers=2, progressbar=TRUE))
> iteration: 1working
> 
> 2working
> 
> 3working
> 
> 4working
> 
> 5working
> 
> 6working
> 
> 7working
> 
> 8working
> 
> 9working
> 
> 10working
> 
> ## here comes the result
> ```
> Same happens for`BPPARAM = SerialParam(progressbar=TRUE)`

**Martin Morgan** (07:25:49):
> This is unintentional and I opened an issue at[https://github.com/Bioconductor/BiocParallel/issues/172](https://github.com/Bioconductor/BiocParallel/issues/172)thanks for the report!

## 2021-11-26

**Francesc Català** (06:43:56):
> @Francesc Català has left the channel

**Chris Vanderaa** (10:36:21):
> Do you have an explanation for this?
> ```
> > any(logical(0))
> [1] FALSE
> > all(logical(0))
> [1] TRUE
> ```
> I'm completely confused:exploding_head::
> 1. How can an empty vector return TRUE/FALSE? I would have expected`NULL`or at least`NA`
> 2. The above also holds for`numeric(0)`(ok, R can interpret/coerce numeric to logical) and`character(0)`(:dizzy_face:)
> 3. How can all be TRUE if any is FALSE ?

**Alan O'C** (10:43:40) (in thread):
> From the documentation, it's a greedy function.
> > The value returned is ‘TRUE’ if all of the values in ‘x’ are ‘TRUE’ (including if there are no values), and ‘FALSE’ if at least one of the values in ‘x’ is ‘FALSE’.

**Alan O'C** (10:45:33) (in thread):
> ie it's doing something close to`!any(!x)`

**Chris Vanderaa** (10:48:30) (in thread):
> Ok thanks for the pointer, I hadn't caught that in the docs! In practice, I find this still very confusing. Luckily, I rarely deal with empty vectors:smile:

**Alan O'C** (10:49:29) (in thread):
> Yeah it's a nasty one for sure! Length zero (or zero row/column) objects are an endless source of errors

**Martin Morgan** (15:48:38) (in thread):
> I think it’s deeper than a help page or greedy function definition.`any()`and`all()`are union and intersection operations on sets of (e.g.,) logical values, respectively. A set containing the single value`TRUE`(hence`all(TRUE) == TRUE`) can be written as the union of the empty set and the set`TRUE`, so`all(union of empty set and TRUE) == TRUE`. But for that to be the case, it must be that`all(empty set) == TRUE`. It is  analogous to role played by 0 in arithmetic operations`+`and`*`on, e.g., non-negative integers,`0 + 1 == 1`and`0 * 1 = 0``

**Chris Vanderaa** (17:39:44) (in thread):
> OK that makes sense! Haha, this is yet another example where logic and (my) intuition disagree:laughing:. Thanks for the demonstration, I will sleep a little less ignorant tonight:wink:

## 2021-12-07

**Castro Mbithi** (02:28:51):
> @Castro Mbithi has joined the channel

**Castro Mbithi** (02:39:01):
> I wanted to share a little piece we wrote as part of this whole fixed price vs hourly struggle that affects remote work.
> 
> We used to hire developers on Upwork a lot in the past. We always had trouble deciding whether to pick “fixed price” or “hourly”. Fixed price is alluring since you only pay for quality work that you are satisfied with. But, it’s unfair to developers since estimating time precisely is a notoriously difficult thing to do. Moreover, we often found ourselves in the position where we kept having to add little bonuses to keep developers motivated to stick to a project rather than abandon it. So, we switched to hiring people by the hour. But we found developers were annoyed that the time tracking software was capturing their screen. And, some developers tracked exaggerated amounts of time to game the system.
> 
> Inspired by the Pomodoro technique, we came up with a new technique of tracking time, that works well with our existing tools, namely Slack. I wanted to share the idea and get the community’s feedback on it:[https://blog.learningdollars.com/2020/03/31/developer-time-tracking-using-slack-beyond-fixed-price-vs-hourly/](https://blog.learningdollars.com/2020/03/31/developer-time-tracking-using-slack-beyond-fixed-price-vs-hourly/)
  - Attachment (LD Talent Blog): [Developer Time Tracking using Slack - beyond Fixed Price vs Hourly](https://blog.learningdollars.com/2020/03/31/developer-time-tracking-using-slack-beyond-fixed-price-vs-hourly/)
> Slack Work Sessions are a novel method for Developer Time Tracking. It resolves 2 issues: Developers prefer hourly pay, but clients want to pay for results.

**Castro Mbithi** (02:39:35) (in thread):
> The second half of this video talks about how to execute projects on tight budgets - I hope it helps:[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iuEKqJbm9O8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iuEKqJbm9O8)(9 mins @ 2X speed) - in particular pay attention to the slack based work sessions for transparency part. The “transparent work sessions” part of the GIF is the TL;DR.
  - Attachment (YouTube): [LD Talent - Find and Manage Vetted Remote Developers - Stanford Team Disrupts Upwork and Recruiters](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iuEKqJbm9O8)
  - File (GIF): [aldgif-2FPS.gif](https://community-bioc.slack.com/files/U34P8RS3B/F02PS2TPF4J/aldgif-2fps.gif)

> [https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/publication/the-influence-of-organizational-structure-on-software-quality-an-empirical-case-study/](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/publication/the-influence-of-organizational-structure-on-software-quality-an-empirical-case-study/)
  - Attachment (Microsoft Research): [The Influence of Organizational Structure On Software Quality: An Empirical Case Study - Microsoft Research](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/publication/the-influence-of-organizational-structure-on-software-quality-an-empirical-case-study/)
> Often software systems are developed by organizations consisting of many teams of individuals working together. Brooks states in the Mythical Man Month book that product quality is strongly affected by organization structure. Unfortunately there has been little empirical evidence to date to substantiate this assertion. In this paper we present a metric scheme to quantify […]

**Krithika Bhuvanesh** (12:11:16):
> Hello everyone ! If you have an interest in***HEALTH DATA SCIENCE ,***this one-year career-ready Masters program may be of interest to you. We offer part time and full time options. More details and our brochure below:*The *******Health Informatics and Data Science (HIDS)******* Masters program at *******Georgetown University******* is designed to help students develop core competencies in data science, health-related data, predictive analytics, machine learning (ML), Artificial Intelligence (AI), and other advanced technologies. Through the program, students will be prepared to successfully take on a wide range of opportunities in health care organizations and related industries including, health technology developers, device manufacturers, pharma/biotech, academic medical centers, and management consulting firms. We help our students build critical skills for this growing sector to lead the evolution of healthcare.*Join one of our virtual information sessions in the coming weeks and talk to us about how the Georgetown-HIDS program fits into your academic and professional goals.
> February 25, 10:00 am EST[https://buff.ly/3KXLrsj](https://buff.ly/3KXLrsj)
  - Attachment (gradapply.georgetown.edu): [Georgetown University Health Informatics & Data Science Virtual Info Session](https://buff.ly/3KXLrsj)

> Do you intend to search below?
> This did not return any search results.
  - File (PNG): [image.png](https://community-bioc.slack.com/files/U34P8RS3B/F032Q4WFEQL/image.png)

**Kozo Nishida** (19:52:05):
> If we want to do a full-text search in natural language, we may need some kind of intelligent system.
> (And Bioconductor vignettes contain a lot of useful information, so it may be worth considering it.)

**Kozo Nishida** (19:56:01):
> I thought it from[this](https://community-bioc.slack.com/archives/CUKAPEE1Y/p1644534363975979).
  - Attachment: [Attachment](https://community-bioc.slack.com/archives/CUKAPEE1Y/p1644534363975979)
> @mirna [pathview](https://www.bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/html/pathview.html) is not in metagenomics biocViews, but it may be suitable for you. I found the following in the vignette of pathview.
> `Notebaly, it can be directly used for metagenomic, microbiome or unknown species data when the data are mapped to KEGG ortholog pathways (Subsection 8.5).`
> I think there are other cases like this, so it would be nice to have some advice from other people.

**Alan O'C** (20:10:11):
> Odd, maybe can be indexed

**Alan O'C** (20:10:35):
> If you're looking for a string like that, you should quote it, otherwise you're searching the individual words

**Alan O'C** (20:11:01):
> ie`file:vignettes/*  file:Rmd "the sentence I'm looking for"`

**Alan O'C** (20:14:20):
> ah, interesting. Even with quotes it doesn't like linebreaks either.


## 2022-02-11

**Kozo Nishida** (02:34:24):
> Thank you for information.
> But beginners may find it difficult to do a pro-like search.
> (There are many things to consider, such as that Rmd is not the only text source for vignette. etc.)

**Mike Smith** (05:25:34):
> You can combined search terms using regular expression syntax e.g. to find files that have extension *.Rmd or *.Rnw you can use`file:(Rmd|Rnw)$`

**Kozo Nishida** (05:59:48):
> Thank you. However, the problem I feel is not only that, but there is no system for natural language search that users can use without explicitly specifying it.
> (It's like we don't use Google that way.)

**Kozo Nishida** (06:14:43):
> @Mike SmithWhat is used for full-text search on[code.bioconductor.org](http://code.bioconductor.org)?

> If such a full-text search engine is already in use, I feel that I have said something impossible.
> If not, I would like to create a full-text search system for vignette pdf and html. (not for code.)
> (This is hope, and I doubt if I can make time for it ...)

**Mike Smith** (07:05:52) (in thread):

## 2022-02-21

**rohitsatyam102** (16:18:50):
> Hi everyone. I was trying to make a table with`formattable`in R but couldn't get rid of`NA`as converting NA to zeros will make`x>0`condition true thereby adding:arrow_up:arrow to these cells. Any thoughts??
> ```
> formattable(tt, list(
>   area(col = clus_6:clus_2) ~ formatter(
>     "span",
>     style = x ~ style(color = ifelse(x < 0 , "red", "green")),
>     x ~ icontext(ifelse(x < 0, "arrow-down", "arrow-up"), x))
> )
> ```
  - File (PNG): [image.png](https://community-bioc.slack.com/files/U34P8RS3B/F033YKFHKFF/image.png)

**Gene Cutler** (16:27:31) (in thread):
> Instead of`ifelse`, try using a 3-case test:
> ```
> case_when(
> x < 0 ~ "arrow-down",
> x > 0 ~ "arrow-up",
> is.na(x) | x == 0 ~ ""
> )
> ```

**Gene Cutler** (16:28:29) (in thread):
> I don’t know if the empty string is what you want for the blank, so you can play with that.


## 2022-02-22

**rohitsatyam102** (02:27:43) (in thread):
> Wow, it worked like magic. I almost forgot about`case_when`coz I use`ifelse`/`if`/`for `statements in routine and using multiple if else here was only evaluating first two conditions here. Many many thanks for saving the day. Here is the code that worked
> and here is how the output looks like.
> ```
> formattable(tt, list(
>   area(col = clus_6:clus_2) ~ formatter(
>     "span",
>     style = x ~ style(color = case_when(
>       x < 0 ~ "red",
>       x > 0 ~ "green",
>       x == 0 ~ "white"
>     )),
>     x ~ icontext(case_when(
> x < 0 ~ "arrow-down",
> x > 0 ~ "arrow-up",
> is.na(x) | x == 0 ~ ""
> ), x))
> )
> ```
  - File (PNG): [image.png](https://community-bioc.slack.com/files/U34P8RS3B/F0345D17SSG/image.png)


## 2022-02-23

**Susanna** (12:06:30):
> Hi! Kind of a long shot, but does anyone know of a method to predict whether a regulatory variant impact gene expression?

**Jared Andrews** (12:09:56):
> Tough question, but funseq2 may be useful to you:[http://funseq2.gersteinlab.org/](http://funseq2.gersteinlab.org/)

**Susanna** (12:13:39) (in thread):
> Thanks for the suggestion, but unfortunately funseq2 is for cancer data only and that's not what I'm working on

**Jared Andrews** (12:15:32) (in thread):
> Well, kind of a few steps removed, but you could consider using something like motifbreakR to see if a variant impacts TF motifs, which may help prioritize those that disrupt regulatory potential.[https://bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/html/motifbreakR.html](https://bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/html/motifbreakR.html)

**Susanna** (12:20:44) (in thread):
> Yes, that's a good one. I've used it already but wondered if there was something else I could try

## 2022-03-28

**Jovan Isma** (08:17:58):
> @Jovan Isma has joined the channel

**Susanna** (08:25:55):
> Hi there! I'm working on gene expression data and wonder if anyone know how to convert RPKM to TPM? Thank you!

**Alexandru-Ioan Voda** (09:01:55) (in thread):
> It's probably easier to convert raw read counts to TPM? Do you only have access to RPKM?

**Rob Patro** (09:16:41):
> TPM is just normalized RPKM * 1000000, so TPM_i = ( RPKM_i / sum_j RPKM_j ) * 1000000.

**Rob Patro** (09:17:26):
> Of course, there is the standard question of why you specifically want to convert RPKM to TPM here.

**Susanna** (09:52:43) (in thread):
> Yes, I'm afraid I only have RPKM

**Alexandru-Ioan Voda** (10:02:49) (in thread):
> sorry I was mistaken when I said "easier", Rob Patro's answer is correct and is just as easy as anything else

**Alexandru-Ioan Voda** (10:03:15) (in thread):
> Good luck with work:four_leaf_clover:^_^

**Artem Sokolov** (10:31:38):
> Keep in mind that if your gene expression data was collected by a 3' sequencing method [[e](https://bmcgenomics.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12864-018-5393-3).g.,[https://www.nature.com/articles/nmeth.f.376](https://www.nature.com/articles/nmeth.f.376)], you do NOT want to normalize by gene length. 3'-seq methods produce a single read per transcript, and normalization will skew gene expression values.

**Alexandru-Ioan Voda** (10:36:05) (in thread):
> Although I assume that if Susanna had access to some RPKM matrix from public sources (since there was no access to raw counts, maybe just BAMs), that the sources didn’t publish an RPKM-norm of 3’-seq.

**Susanna** (13:00:19) (in thread):
> Thank you, Artem.
> Correct Alexandru:+1:

**Alexandru-Ioan Voda** (15:37:37):
> Hi all! Curious, is there a package or suggested-R-function that can simulate having lower sequencing depth (lower library size, by subsampling), given a count matrix?

**Marc Elosua** (15:46:47) (in thread):
> Maybe you can try[DropletUtils](https://bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/vignettes/DropletUtils/inst/doc/DropletUtils.html)`downsampleReads()`!

**Peter Hickey** (17:38:46) (in thread):
> `scuttle::downsampleMatrix()`will do what you describe

## 2022-04-05

**Alan Murphy** (07:41:31):
> Hey! Apologies but not sure where to ask this question regarding reference genome SNP databases on Bioconductor:
> I'm the creator of the[MungeSumstats](https://github.com/neurogenomics/MungeSumstats)package on Bioconductor for the standardisation and processing of GWAS sumstats. I currently use Bioconductor packages derived from dbSNP to infer missing RS IDs. I use SNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP144.GRCh38 and SNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP144.GRCh37 dependent on the genome build. Does anyone know if there are later versions of dbSNP available than 144 on Bioconductor? I know there is SNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP151.GRCh38 for GRCh38 but I can't seem to find similar for GRCh37? Also how will these different versions compare to other reference genome datasets in Bioconductor (I also use BSgenome.Hsapiens.1000genomes.hs37d5, BSgenome.Hsapiens.NCBI.GRCh38 for different checks)? For example, is BSgenome.Hsapiens.NCBI.GRCh38 up-to-date with SNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP151.GRCh38?

**Vince Carey** (08:49:15):
> @Hervé Pagès^^


## 2022-04-06

**Martin Rodriguez Nuñez** (16:24:36):
> @Martin Rodriguez Nuñez has joined the channel


## 2022-04-07

**Hervé Pagès** (10:26:15) (in thread):
> Hi Alan,
> I started to look into building new SNPlocs packages for dbSNP155. Unfortunately I can no longer use my scripts for this because they are relying on the ASN.1 files that dbSNP no longer provides. Now they provide JSON and VCF files instead:[https://ftp.ncbi.nih.gov/snp/latest_release/](https://ftp.ncbi.nih.gov/snp/latest_release/)(Note that they made that switch about 4 years ago, around dbSNP152, see[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/mailman/pipermail/dbsnp-announce/2018q4/000193.html](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/mailman/pipermail/dbsnp-announce/2018q4/000193.html))
> The JSON files are huge and hard to parse. I came up with some naive code that I've run on the JSON files for chromosomes MT, Y, and 22. It's very slow:disappointed:I estimate it would take about 2 weeks to run it on all chromosomes! I should probably try to parse the VCF files instead.
> > For example, is BSgenome.Hsapiens.NCBI.GRCh38 up-to-date with SNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP151.GRCh38?
> The***SNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP**.GRCh38**packages only contain SNPs located on the chromosomes (SNPs located on scaffolds or alt/patch/fix sequences are omitted) so are compatible with***BSgenome.Hsapiens.NCBI.GRCh38***(contains the original GRCh38 assembly) and***BSgenome.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg38***(based on the GRCh38.p13 assembly).

**Alan Murphy** (10:34:12) (in thread):
> HeyHervé! Ah okay thanks for the explanation, that's good to know! Yeah I would guess the VCFs are easier to work with, there are quite a few packages in R read them in, including MungeSumstats. Are you working in R for this? Do you have a sample of a format are you trying to get them to?

**Sarath Kumar Murugan** (10:41:09):
> @Sarath Kumar Murugan has joined the channel

**Hervé Pagès** (10:46:18) (in thread):
> I have a pipeline. The first step and most CPU-intensive  is to convert the JSON files into simple tab-delimited files with 6 columns and one RefSNP id per line. See[https://github.com/hpages/SNPlocsForge/blob/273c0ecc0bb01984783747e4744d6ad1aa8b538c/man/extract_raw_snps_from_json.Rd#L46-L51](https://github.com/hpages/SNPlocsForge/blob/273c0ecc0bb01984783747e4744d6ad1aa8b538c/man/extract_raw_snps_from_json.Rd#L46-L51)The code is written in R. Then I feed these tab-delimited files to the rest of the pipeline.
> I'll try to generate the tab-delimited files from dbSNP's VCF files instead.

**Alan Murphy** (10:50:02) (in thread):
> That makes sense, thanks! If I get some time I'll do the same to see if I can come up with something to speed it up. Creating the tab-delimited file is the time intensive step too yeah?

**Hervé Pagès** (11:18:09) (in thread):
> yep, that's the most time intensive step

## 2022-04-18

**Alan Murphy** (14:16:05) (in thread):
> @Hervé Pagèsthis is amazing, thank you very much! Did you end up using the VCF? Will a version of dbSNP 155 also be made available for GRCh37? Both would be massively useful for MungeSumstats

**Hervé Pagès** (16:55:37) (in thread):
> The JSON files. Using brute force to process the 25 JSON files in parallel on a powerful server took about 2 days. The source tarball of the GRCh38 package is about 6 GB. The package for GRCh37 could be made lighter by reusing the rs id, chrom, and alleles columns from the GRCh38 package, so it would actually only need to contain the positions. This should make it between 3 and 4 times smaller. Or maybe it's easy enough to use something like`rtracklayer::liftOver()`to translate between GRCh38 and GRCh37 positions, in which case the  package for GRCh37 wouldn't even be needed. I'll look into this.

## 2022-04-22

**Anna Quaglieri (she/her)** (00:17:19):
> hey there! What's the suggested way to, given a list of human proteins/genes map them to the latest protein/Gene Ids? Sometimes names changes or synonims are used.

**Lluís Revilla** (03:19:25) (in thread):
> I think the easiest would be:
> ```
> library("Org.Hs.eg.db")
> select(Org.Hs.eg.db, keys = my_genes, keytype = "SYMBOL", columns = "GENEID")
> ```
> If you want the NCBI gene ID. There are several solutions, using other annotation packages if you want the ENSEMBL ids. I would recommend to look at the select help page, as there are arguments to decide what to do with synonyms (not sure about name changes). You could also use biomaRt to retrieve this kind of information.

**Anna Quaglieri (she/her)** (05:27:51) (in thread):
> Thanks a lot@Lluís Revilla! I will try this option:+1:

> “ACC_CNASNP-20160128”

**Vince Carey** (10:45:14) (in thread):
> Have you considered using the metadata slot of SummarizedExperiment?

**Vince Carey** (10:46:09) (in thread):
> @Laurent Gatto... perhaps you have worked on this?

**James Ward** (10:55:59) (in thread):
> Thanks for the response! That's a potential workaround I thought about and may try in the meantime. I could store an assays DataFrame there with info. The step that isn't ideal is subsetting assays, I was hoping for something that would also subset the assays DataFrame.The metadata slot is useful but is sort of like using S3 object attributes, it doesn't stay in sync with the main object. At least it isn't dropped during subsetting as with S3 object attributes!The rowData and colData are useful to keep data in sync with those dimensions. I was thinking of assays as another dimension.As I think it out, a driver of the use cases appears to be the subsetting step. (Also the addition of new assay matrix. Ahhh.) Even if the DataFrame is not subset, the rownames could be used to sync with what assayNames are present.

**Vince Carey** (10:58:46) (in thread):

**Anusha Nagari** (12:35:24):

**Anusha Nagari** (14:59:00):
> Hi everyone! Are the commits made to the release branches (ex: RELEASE_3_15) are reflected real time or will it take the usual 24-48 hours to propagate the changes like in dev version

**Marcel Ramos Pérez** (15:00:07):
> They should be reflected close to real time at[https://code.bioconductor.org/browse/](https://code.bioconductor.org/browse/)and expect the changes to propagate via the BBS on M, W, and F[https://bioconductor.org/checkResults/](https://bioconductor.org/checkResults/)

**Anusha Nagari** (15:03:00) (in thread):
> @Marcel Ramos PérezThank you, I am trying to push the changes to RELEASE_3_15 and following the steps from[Merge changes to the current release branch](https://contributions.bioconductor.org/git-version-control.html#optional-merge-changes-to-the-current-release-branch), however having issues with the changes not being pushed to. Could you please let me know if I am missing something?

**Marcel Ramos Pérez** (15:07:03) (in thread):
> I'd recommend[https://contributions.bioconductor.org/git-version-control.html#bug-fix-in-release-and-devel](https://contributions.bioconductor.org/git-version-control.html#bug-fix-in-release-and-devel)

**Anusha Nagari** (15:08:56) (in thread):
> I did not realize that I have to change the release version as well and already made the changes to devel branch. In that case can I follow the above thread?

**Marcel Ramos Pérez** (15:10:39) (in thread):
> If it is a bug fix, you should cherry-pick the commits over to the release branch

**Marcel Ramos Pérez** (15:10:42) (in thread):
> and yes

**Anusha Nagari** (16:05:12) (in thread):
> I followed the steps and merged updates resolving conflicts. But getting the error: ! [remote rejected] master -> master (pre-receive hook declined)
> 
> Attached is the screenshot. git status shows the working tree is clean

**Anusha Nagari** (16:05:16) (in thread):
  - File (PNG): [image.png](https://community-bioc.slack.com/files/U34P8RS3B/F03EZKA351B/image.png)

**Marcel Ramos Pérez** (16:08:26) (in thread):
> It looks like the conflicts weren't fully resolved, committed, and were caught by the git hook

**Anusha Nagari** (16:19:37) (in thread):
> Got it. I am unable to find the conflicts how do i go about this?

**Marcel Ramos Pérez** (16:33:08) (in thread):
> Did you try using grep? e.g., grep -rn '===\+'

**Marcel Ramos Pérez** (16:34:30) (in thread):
> Also see[https://contributions.bioconductor.org/git-version-control.html?q=merge%20conflig#resolve-merge-conflicts](https://contributions.bioconductor.org/git-version-control.html?q=merge%20conflig#resolve-merge-conflicts)
  - Attachment (contributions.bioconductor.org): [Chapter 21 Git Version Control | Bioconductor Packages: Development, Maintenance, and Peer Review](https://contributions.bioconductor.org/git-version-control.html?q=merge%20conflig#resolve-merge-conflicts)
> The Bioconductor project is maintained in a Git source control system. Package maintainers update their packages by pushing changes to their git repositories. This chapter contains several...

## 2022-05-21

**Alexandru-Ioan Voda** (07:56:29):
> Say I want to download a dependency, a baseline-model for a package that needs it to run specific analyses on new datasets.
> 
> How would I download the data to`<package_install_path>/extdata/`using****`BiocFileCache`****and****`usethis`****? I was told to use these but the docs are a bit convoluted for me
> 
> I understand that****`usethis`****is useful because it can help format Windows-like paths (with`\`instead of`/`like we do in Linux or Macs) for system calls, but the docs of****`usethis`******** and ********`BiocFileCache`******** ****seem very long and address many use-cases that I don't need?

**Vince Carey** (09:13:02):
> You should not operate on`<package_install_path>`... use BiocFileCache.  Maybe there are enough hints in
> ```
> > get_cached_model
> function(path = "/tmp/beta.csv", cache=BiocFileCache::BiocFileCache()) {
>   chk = BiocFileCache::bfcquery(cache, "beta.csv")
>   if (nrow(chk)==0) {
>        tag = names(BiocFileCache::bfcadd(cache, path, action="copy"))
>   }
>   else tag = chk$rid
>   cache[[tag]]
> }
> ```
> to get you going.  This handles caching a file on disk.  The man page for bfcadd has indications on ways of handling something on the web.  If the value to be cached is small, download it to the cache.  Only R itself should be writing into`<package_install_path>`

**Alexandru-Ioan Voda** (09:19:30) (in thread):
> Thank you for your reply!
> 
> The data is quite large, which is why I don't put it in`extdata`directly with documentation, and I'd prefer to have it in a non`/tmp/`-like path, since it should be perma-stored as long as the package is installed/used
> 
> Thanks for the code snippet, I'll try this out:relaxed:

**Alan O'C** (10:05:05) (in thread):
> The file cache is kept in the users home directory rather than`/tmp`eg I have`~/.cache/R/ExperimentHub`etc

**Kristian Ullrich** (04:42:27) (in thread):
> Yes, it is, see here:

**Kristian Ullrich** (04:42:28) (in thread):
> [https://gitlab.gwdg.de/mpievolbio-it/crbhits/-/blob/master/R/cds2codonaln.R](https://gitlab.gwdg.de/mpievolbio-it/crbhits/-/blob/master/R/cds2codonaln.R)
  - Attachment (GitLab): [R/cds2codonaln.R · master · mpievolbio-it / CRBHits](https://gitlab.gwdg.de/mpievolbio-it/crbhits/-/blob/master/R/cds2codonaln.R)
> CRBHits is a reimplementation of the Conditional Reciprocal Best Hit algorithm crb-blast in R.

**Kristian Ullrich** (04:53:04) (in thread):
> However, I need to say, that my implementation starts directly from CDS. This is because in a lot of times even on “good” resources the CDS and protein sequences do not match perfectly and PAL2NAL will also through an error for this situations. Of course there are cases of post-transcriptional AA changes which will be hard to be back-translated into codon alignments anyway.

**Kristian Ullrich** (04:55:03) (in thread):
> The[https://github.com/drostlab/orthologr](https://github.com/drostlab/orthologr)package has a wrapper to PAL2NAL already implemented.

## 2022-06-07

**Hervé Pagès** (15:49:44) (in thread):
> Major surprise is that some rs ids have been placed on a different chromosome in GRCh37 vs GRCh38 (e.g. rs183986666 was on chr10 in GRCh37 and is now on chr9 in GRCh38, see[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/snp/rs183986666#variant_details](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/snp/rs183986666#variant_details)). This breaks the naive assumptions I made when I wrote the scripts to extract SNP placements from the RefSNP json files e.g. snps in`refsnp-chr9.json.bz2`are not necessarily on chr9 in GRCh37. Had to refactor things substantially but now extraction is even slower. Will take days before completion...:disappointed:

**Alan Murphy** (16:20:37) (in thread):
> Wow that's mental! Ah no that's annoying, sorry to hear that and thank you very much for working on this!

## 2022-06-16

**Ariel Hippen** (15:35:47):
> In a SingleCellExperiment object, is there a way to subset based on gene expression values?

**Ariel Hippen** (15:35:52):
> Say I wanted to filter down to cells that express EPCAM. I could make a subset with just EPCAM and pass it to scater's`addPerCellQC`function, then subset on`sce$subsets_epcam_sum`but it seems like there must be a more direct route

**kent riemondy** (16:35:52) (in thread):
> You can subset more directly by examining the count matrix, e.g.:
> ```
> #BiocManager::install("scRNAseq")
> library(scRNAseq)
> sce <- FletcherOlfactoryData()
> 
> # if you want to select cells positive for 1 gene, e.g. Epcam
> epcam_positive <- which(assay(sce, "counts")["Epcam", ] > 0)
> epcam_pos_sce <- sce[, epcam_positive]
> 
> assay(epcam_pos_sce, "counts")["Epcam", 1:5]
> #> OEP01_N706_S501 OEP01_N701_S501 OEP01_N707_S507 OEP01_N705_S501 OEP01_N702_S505
> #>              80             203              40             736               3
> 
> # if you want to select cells positive for more than 1 gene
> genes_to_check <- c("Msi2", "Epcam", "Trp53")
> positive_cells <- which(colSums(assay(sce, "counts")[genes_to_check, ] > 0) == length(genes_to_check))
> triple_pos_sce <- sce[, positive_cells]
> 
> assay(triple_pos_sce, "counts")[genes_to_check, 1:5]
> #>       OEP01_N706_S501 OEP01_N703_S501 OEP01_N704_S503 OEP01_N704_S506
> #> Msi2               18              22             115              65
> #> Epcam              80             228             317             111
> #> Trp53             741              42               1               1
> #>       OEP01_N708_S506
> #> Msi2                3
> #> Epcam              12
> #> Trp53            5584
> ```

**Ariel Hippen** (16:42:52) (in thread):
> This works, thank you!

**Jenny Drnevich** (10:10:36):
> Any one else noticed/irritated by RStudio's recent change to their Help tab? The Usage section for any function's help page is now in a separate text box that has it's own scroll bar at the bottom of the Usage  section, which often isn't visible when there are lots of functions in the Usage. Makes it almost impossible to scroll and see a function at the same time, without make the Help pane extreme large!:rage:

**Marcel Ramos Pérez** (10:24:39) (in thread):
> That does sound frustrating. Can you provide an example function that has that? I haven't encountered one with that feature (I'm running RStudio 2022.02.3 Build 492). If you create an issue at[https://community.rstudio.com/](https://community.rstudio.com/)and link back here, we can upvote:slightly_smiling_face:

**Jenny Drnevich** (11:09:47) (in thread):
> I am also running 2022.02.3 Build 492; for Windows, so maybe it's different on Mac.`?limma::goana`is one and`?gplots::heatmap.2`has an egregiously long Usage section, but at least most of that only has one argument per line so scrolling isn't needed. I'll try to find some others later to create the RStudio issue.

**stefano mangiola** (04:08:15) (in thread):

> 
> a tidy way is using tidySingleCellExeriment

**SFS Worms** (08:52:15):
> @SFS Worms has joined the channel

**SFS Worms** (08:56:00):
> Having some issue trying to extract specific sequences from NGS set (I have one randomised region that I want to extract from amplicon reads). My code run, but it takes about 1hour for 60*10^6 reads. I'm thinking trying to run things with parallel processing might help, but I can't figure it out, does anyone got good ressources on using parallel processing with multiple alignments?

**Kasper D. Hansen** (11:04:13):
> Taking advantage of multiple processors efficiently is pretty hard. However, is your problem inherently embarrasingly parallel? Then you should get tons of milage out of additional processors. I am pretty unclear on what you're trying to do.

**SFS Worms** (11:41:03):
> Sorry, I was pretty unclear, so I have a bunch of sequences from a drift experiment in a fastq file. All the sequences have a conserved sequence followed by a randomized sequence that I am interested in, let's say ATGGTAGTNNNGTGA or whatever. I want to extract all the NNN sequences. Right now I'm doing it by aligning TAGT to sequence to see where the NNN is and then subsetting it with subseq, but the alignment provedure is extremely slow and I was hoping to parallellize it.

**Rob Patro** (11:45:32):
> So you want to extract all (say 3) nucleotide sequences that occur downstream of a`TAGT`?

**Rob Patro** (11:45:44):
> and only in the forward or also in the reverse complement?

**SFS Worms** (11:46:33):
> Only in the foward seq would do

**SFS Worms** (11:47:00):
> And I'm reasonably certian that`TAGT`only occurs once in the sequence in my case

**Rob Patro** (11:55:04):
> Have you tried something straightforward like the following?
> ```
> gunzip -c <fastq> | grep -oP "TAGT\K([ACGT]{3})"
> ```

**Rob Patro** (11:55:12):
> grep can be super fast

**SFS Worms** (11:56:04):
> Mmmmmmh, no, I'll try now

**SFS Worms** (11:56:06):
> Thanks!

**Rob Patro** (12:24:04):
> you’re welcome!

**Rob Patro** (12:24:41):
> I’ll note the`gunzip -c <fastq> |`part is only if your fastq is gzipped, if it’s already uncompressed it’s just`grep -oP "TAGT\K([ACGT]{3})" <fastq>`

**SFS Worms** (13:19:11):
> i'm not familiar with regex, but what's the role of \K? Not take the things before?

**Martin Morgan** (13:19:36):
> In R I***think***this could be implemented as
> ```
> library(Biostrings); library(stringi)
> reads = readDNAStringSet("<fastq>")
> stri_extract(reads, regex = "(?<=TAGT)[ACGT]{3}")
> ```
> This uses 'lookbehind'`(<=TAGT)`(`?<=`says start the match after the string in parentheses). If the fastq file where large one could iterate through in chunks (e.g., of 10's of millions of reads at a time) as illustrated on the help page`?readDNAStringSet`.

**Rob Patro** (13:21:01):
> Ingrep,\Kisshorthandforexactlywhat@Martin Morgansuggests,I.e.printoutonlythecapturegroup.

**SFS Worms** (13:21:19):
> Thanks!

**SFS Worms** (13:32:59) (in thread):
> This works fine, but somehow stri extract is even slower than my previous method, Ill look into it tomorrow

**Martin Morgan** (14:41:38) (in thread):
> It will be slow if you run out of memory; in that case you will need to iterate through your files in chunks.


## 2022-06-28

**Lea Seep** (03:01:56):
> @Lea Seep has joined the channel

**Arvid Hutzfeldt** (04:54:55):
> @Arvid Hutzfeldt has joined the channel

**Moni** (07:06:04):
> @Moni has joined the channel

**SFS Worms** (09:10:58) (in thread):
> So I'm not sure I understand why, but on 100k sequence
> ```
> extract.peptide2 = function(dnaSeq, regexPattern = "(?<=TGGCTTCATTGCGAGCAAT)[ACGT]{24}"){
>   seq = stri_extract(dnaSeq, regex = regexPattern) %>% DNAStringSet()
>   return(seq)
> }
> ```
> runs in 5.7 sec.
> ```
> extract.peptide3 = function(dnaSeq, regexPattern = "(?<=TGGCTTCATTGCGAGCAAT)[ACGT]{24}"){
>   x <- str_locate(dnaSeq, "(?<=TGGCTTCATTGCGAGCAAT)[ACGT]{24}")
>   peptideList <- subseq(x = dnaSeq, start = x[,1], end = x[,2])
>   return(peptideList)
> }
> ```

**SFS Worms** (09:11:05) (in thread):
> Second on in 0.47

**SFS Worms** (09:11:22) (in thread):
> And
> ```
> extract.peptide4 = function(dnaSeq, regexPattern = "TGGCTTCATTGCGAGCAAT", pepSize = 24){
>   x <- str_locate(dnaSeq, regexPattern)
>   peptideList <- subseq(x = dnaSeq, start = x[,2]+1, end = x[,2]+pepSize)
>   return(peptideList)
> }
> ```

**SFS Worms** (09:11:52) (in thread):
> In 0.17, so it seems stri_extract is less efficient that subseq() by a lot

**SFS Worms** (09:12:03) (in thread):
> Thanks a ton for the help, really pushed me in the right direction

**Martin Morgan** (10:36:05) (in thread):
> Building on your last example, because the`regexPattern`is actually fixed, I think you can use`vmatchPattern()`followed by a little massaging and then`subseq()`, maybe something like
> ```
> match <- vmatchPattern(regexPattern, dnaSeq, fixed=TRUE)
> found <- lengths(match) == 1L
> dnaSeq1 <- dnaSeq[found]
> subseq(
>     dnaSeq1,
>     min(width(dnaSeq1), unlist(end(found)) + 1L),
>     min(width(dnaSeq1), unlist(end(found)) + pepSize)
> )
> ```
> this should be fast because there is no coercion from`DNAStringSet`to`character()`, and because`subseq()`actually creates 'views' on to the DNA sequence, rather than creating a new DNAStringSet.

**SFS Worms** (10:44:43) (in thread):
> Oh nice, will give it a try!

**Hervé Pagès** (02:36:44) (in thread):
> I've just pushed***SNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP155.GRCh37_0.99.22.tar.gz***(929,496,192 SNPs) and***SNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP155.GRCh38_0.99.22.tar.gz***(949,021,448 SNPs) to BioC 3.16. The 2 packages should become available via`BiocManager::install()`in the next 3 hours or so.
> Sorry for the delay, had to completely refactor the pipeline I use to forge these packages. My previous pipeline missed a few SNPs that are included in this new version of***SNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP155.GRCh38***.

**Alan Murphy** (02:37:52) (in thread):
> Amazing news, thank you so much for this I really appreciate it!!

**Hervé Pagès** (02:40:14) (in thread):
> You're welcome. Also FYI I removed the following packages from BioC 3.16 to make room for these 2 new packages:
> - SNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP.20101109
> - SNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP.20120608
> - SNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP141.GRCh38
> - XtraSNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP141.GRCh38
> - SNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP142.GRCh37
> - SNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP151.GRCh38
> All of them are currently deprecated in BioC 3.15.

**Alan Murphy** (02:43:50) (in thread):
> That's all good with me, the only other dbSNP versions I use are:
> 
> SNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP144.GRCh37,
> SNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP144.GRCh38
> 
> which I'd like to keep as users of the package have expressed a need for the older version of dbSNP too

## 2022-07-31

**Amos Schorr** (05:51:50):
> Not related to bio, but hopefully y’all might know some ppl you can share this with. A super awesome org that teaches ppl data science and software engineering in places that don’t offer many opportunities is hiring a couple ppl (IT Dev, Director of Technology) and they really need help getting the word out. They’re fully remote, high impact jobs with explicit salary ranges in the JD.
> 
> 1. IT dev. You need to be able to work with Docker, Redis, PostgreSQL, AWS, jQuery, bootstrap, select2, Linux, Docker, AWS.
> 
> 2. Dir. of Technology. You need some leadership experience, experience developing tech and data products, managing infrastructure on AWS, open source experience, etc.[https://carpentries.org/jobs/](https://carpentries.org/jobs/)
  - Attachment (The Carpentries): [Job Opportunities with The Carpentries](https://carpentries.org/jobs/)
> The Carpentries is a fiscally sponsored project of Community Initiatives, a registered 501(c)3 non-profit organisation based in California, USA. We are a global community teaching foundational computational and data science skills to researchers in academia, industry and government.

## 2022-09-07

**Laura Marcos-Zambrano** (04:55:35):
> Hi all:grinning:I will need your advice
> I’m preparing a teaching course about R and Bioconductor basics for undergrad students. Everyone is going to bring their own laptop. What will be the best way to prepare everything for solving installation, and dependencies issues in each different computer?

**Luke Zappia** (05:55:51) (in thread):
> From experience teaching Software Carpentry workshops the biggest issue was institutional laptop where things were locked down (stuff like the default home directory being on a shared drive you can’t access). Hopefully that should be ok if people are using personal laptops.
> 
> I would send out a super detailed set of instructions for what to download and install including Windows/MacOS/Linux differences (preferably with pictures). It can seem obvious but just saying “install from this website” is often hard for people to follow. People also often struggle with the difference between R and RStudio and that they need to install both. Check if any of the packages you want to use have extra system dependencies but if you are sticking to common packages the normal install system should be able to handle things. It can also be helpful to get people to install packages in advance to avoid waiting for things to compile and to find any major issues.
> 
> I’m sure people with have other suggestions I have forgotten.
> 
> Good luck with the course!

**Laura Marcos-Zambrano** (07:15:43) (in thread):
> Thank you Luke:slightly_smiling_face:I will go with the "super detailed" set of instructions:stuck_out_tongue:and hope everyone can follow it.

**Laurent Gatto** (08:04:54) (in thread):
> Here's what we have for the workshop we are going to run next week:[https://carpentries-incubator.github.io/bioc-intro/setup.html](https://carpentries-incubator.github.io/bioc-intro/setup.html)

**Laura Marcos-Zambrano** (09:38:58) (in thread):
> Thanks!!:slightly_smiling_face:

**Kasper D. Hansen** (10:22:52) (in thread):
> In my experience, a very useful thing to do  is to make a script that***checks***whether the dependencies are installed and tell them to screenshot the output and send it. That way you know it “works” and you know how many people you will need to help.

**Federico Marini** (12:05:55) (in thread):
> On a similar line to@Kasper D. Hansen- for a summer school we recently ran, I specified the requirements in a page, telling them what to do to check if all runs, and then if problems arise, we offered some kind of troubleshooting on day 0. Can be (if not too much going on) on starting time -1 hour:wink:

**Ana Beatriz Villaseñor Altamirano** (17:50:08) (in thread):
> If you have time (and or TAs), you can schedule 1 hr for solving setup issues before starting classes.
> Also you could use Rstudio Cloud[https://www.rstudio.com/resources/webinars/rstudio-cloud-in-the-classroom/](https://www.rstudio.com/resources/webinars/rstudio-cloud-in-the-classroom/)as far as I remember it was free for teaching, maybe I’m wrong
  - Attachment (rstudio.com): [RStudio Cloud in the Classroom](https://www.rstudio.com/resources/webinars/rstudio-cloud-in-the-classroom/)
> Much has been written in the statistics and data science education literature about pedagogical tools and approaches to provide a practical computational foundation for students.

**Sean Davis** (21:15:57) (in thread):
> @Laura Marcos-ZambranoI'm happy to host on Orchestra ([http://orchestra.cancerdatasci.org](http://orchestra.cancerdatasci.org)) if you'd like (it's free). The one caveat is that there is no persistent storage between 8-hour sessions, so students need to download their work.
> 
> If you are teaching a semester course, go through the challenges of getting software installed on their personal laptops. If just a few limited sessions, then Orchestra might be a good fit. I will often  do both under the assumption that someone will have problems during coding sessions; it is good to have a backup like Orchestra just in case that you can launch in a couple of minutes for a student.

**Sean Davis** (21:17:35) (in thread):
> Orchestra has the advantage of being able to preinstall software and to include course materials inside the environment.

**Abdul Saboor Khan** (17:52:17):
> Hello everyone, can anyone tell me how to resolve this issue " Error in eigen(input) : infinite or missing values in 'x'" , yes I have NAs in my matrix file, I am making the phenotype network using this code "cormat2 <- cor(network_nem[1:16], method = "spearman", use = "na.or.complete") " , and then this qgraph(cormat2,graph = "cor", layout = "circle", sampleSize = nrow(network_nem), alpha = 0.3, cut = 5, bonf = T, title = "Phenotype Network of A. nemorensis", title.cex = 2, vsize = 7, usePCH = T, details = T, threshold="sig", height = 30, width = 40, label.scale = T, theme ='TeamFortress') . I have one column as a character and I have to keep it in the network but it doesn't accept other than numeric. If you have the solution please tell me.

**Hervé Pagès** (18:48:28) (in thread):
> Please ask your question again on our support site:[https://support.bioconductor.org/](https://support.bioconductor.org/)Also please make sure to read the posting guide before you post. In particular the code you show should illustrate the problem and others should be able to run it. Format your post by putting code between triple backticks. This helps with readability and it makes it easier for others to copy/paste it. Also please provide your`sessionInfo()`. Thanks!


## 2022-09-11

**Janani Ravi** (18:15:46):
> ***R@IISA******R Workshops by Ayush Patel and ***@Heather Turner**** in Bengaluru, India, Dec 30, 2022**
> > This is the second in the series of R-related conferences and workshops offered as part of the biannual[International Indian Statistical Association meeting](https://www.intindstat.org/conference2022/index). The first was held in[Mumbai in 2019](http://r-iisa2019.rbind.io/). The 2022 day-long workshop will take place at the[Indian Institute of Science, Bangalore](https://iisc.ac.in)on December 30, 2022. This workshop has been endorsed by the[R Foundation](https://www.r-project.org/foundation/).  This is a call for participants throughout India. Apply to participant using the form on the website:
> More info:[https://r-iisa2022.rbind.io/](https://r-iisa2022.rbind.io/)| Register here:[https://bit.ly/Riisa2022](https://bit.ly/Riisa2022)More on Twitter:[https://twitter.com/AsiaR_comm/status/1569085556734435328](https://twitter.com/AsiaR_comm/status/1569085556734435328)

> Do you want to use [@Bioconductor](https://twitter.com/Bioconductor) 3.15? That means that you’ll need to install R 4.2 on your computer from CRAN [https://cran.r-project.org/](https://cran.r-project.org/)
> 
> For macOS users, you might want to use to install :package: deps for compiling
> 
> sudo R
> source("[https://mac.R-project.org/bin/install.R](https://mac.R-project.org/bin/install.R)")
> install.libs("all")
> 
> #rstats [https://twitter.com/Bioconductor/status/1519428806150443014](https://twitter.com/Bioconductor/status/1519428806150443014)

## 2022-10-26

**Nicholas Cooley** (09:24:29):
> Hi everyone, I have a sort of under the hood question about`mclapply`, which to my understanding, works by forking. How do the forks in`mclapply`get assigned their memory limits? i.e. do they inherit`R_MAX_VSIZE`from the parent session? Do they initialize their own limits from what’s available? Thanks!

**Martin Morgan** (11:12:33) (in thread):
> I'd guess yes, they inherit from the parent session (because the environment variable is assigned to in C to global variable, and the global variable is inherited by the forked process. But you'd get a more definitive answer on the[R-devel mailing list](https://stat.ethz.ch/mailman/listinfo/r-devel).@Henrik Bengtssonmight also have something to say...

**Henrik Bengtsson** (13:14:56) (in thread):
> Like Martin, I'd also guess forked child processes inherit this from the parent process, but that's just my best guess.
> 
> BTW, if you're running into memory issues with`mclapply()`, please know that forked processing is tricky in R (and sometimes unstable). Although it might spare some memory because of how forking works, eventually child processes might start updating the memory so that the OS eventually end up with make copies. I haven't formally investigated it, but I can see how this could happen "at random" as R's garbage collection starts running in the different forked processes.

**Nicholas Cooley** (14:25:46) (in thread):
> Thanks both of you! It’s mostly a curiosity question, because i was running into some instability when the forks were consuming large amounts of memory. I just ended up asking for less cores and things turned out ok.

**David S Burton** (19:55:36):
> @David S Burton has joined the channel
