## 2022-12-06

**Alan O'C** (10:30:47):
> Any clues as to why I see ggplot2 deprecation warnings (sigh,`aes_string`...) when running tests locally, but not on the build machines? Both running ggplot2 3.4.0 apparently

**Marcel Ramos Pérez** (11:00:04) (in thread):
> Have you tried to reproduce inside the Bioc container? My guess is that the deprecation warning shows up once per session and gets triggered in another package first?:thinking_face:

**Alan O'C** (11:57:12) (in thread):
> Ah!`This warning is displayed once every 8 hours.`

**Hervé Pagès** (13:34:45) (in thread):
> Is this a case of over engineering? I understand that they don't want to make too much noise but OTOH this is not great for reproducibility.

**Alan O'C** (13:37:19) (in thread):
> It's kind of antisocial imo. two of my packages had a tonne of warnings that don't show up on the BBS, and these functions are deprecated since 3.0 apparently. Not sure if CRAN has the same issue, but it does probably mean when they make them defunct then half of Bioc will be in the red

**Hervé Pagès** (13:43:08) (in thread):
> Maybe they could mitigate the problem by turning this into a hard warning if`IS_BIOC_BUILD_MACHINE=true`?

**Hervé Pagès** (13:43:52) (in thread):
> and something similar when`R CMD check`runs on a CRAN machine

**Mike Smith** (14:09:10) (in thread):
> Switching around the effort, I think they provide the option for setting the frequency as a global option i.e.[https://rlang.r-lib.org/reference/abort.html#muffling-and-silencing-conditions](https://rlang.r-lib.org/reference/abort.html#muffling-and-silencing-conditions)Perhaps that could be set on the build machines?
  - Attachment (rlang.r-lib.org): [Signal an error, warning, or message — abort](https://rlang.r-lib.org/reference/abort.html#muffling-and-silencing-conditions)
> These functions are equivalent to base functions base::stop(),
> base::warning(), and base::message(). They signal a condition
> (an error, warning, or message respectively) and make it easy to
> supply condition metadata:
> Supply class to create a classed condition that can be caught
> or handled selectively, allowing for finer-grained error
> handling.
> Supply metadata with named ... arguments. This data is stored
> in the condition object and can be examined by handlers.
> Supply call to inform users about which function the error
> occurred in.
> Supply another condition as parent to create a chained condition.
> 
> Certain components of condition messages are formatted with unicode
> symbols and terminal colours by default. These aspects can be
> customised, see Customising condition messages.

**Hervé Pagès** (14:47:28) (in thread):
> Maybe. But if the goal is reproducibility, an environment variable that we can add to[https://master.bioconductor.org/checkResults/3.17/bioc-LATEST/Renviron.bioc](https://master.bioconductor.org/checkResults/3.17/bioc-LATEST/Renviron.bioc)would be nice.

**Hervé Pagès** (15:50:39) (in thread):
> Issue opened for***lifecycle***:[https://github.com/r-lib/lifecycle/issues/150](https://github.com/r-lib/lifecycle/issues/150)

## 2022-12-12

**Zainab Ashimiyu-Abdusalam** (04:41:00):
> @Zainab Ashimiyu-Abdusalam has joined the channel

**Amos Schorr** (10:52:08):
> Hey folks! I work for Norn Group, a nonprofit in the bio of aging research field (we’re best known for our Impetus Grants program). Because this is such a new field, it’s fragmented and hard for everyone to navigate. So we’re building out an opt-in database for ppl who have or want careers in aging. It will NOT be a community you need to participate in. We’ll just be using this to make connections and help people build out labs, companies, and careers.
> 
> We’re launching today and as of now have ~70 signups of people at all levels and in all kinds of roles. If this intrigues you, get more info and sign up if you like[here](http://norn.group/talent-bench)! It’s free and could be useful for you.
> 
> Additionally, if you’d be so kind as to[retweet](https://twitter.com/amos_schorr/status/1602258925809971202)/share our posts[on LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7008023356436303872/)announcing it, that would be just darling of you:heart:My linkedin profile picture is of me moderating a panel with a dog on my lap. Or, more accurately, an adorable dog that’s sitting on my lap not giving a damn about the awesome conversation we were having. So it’s worth sharing my post if only for that.

## 2022-12-23

**Nicholas Cooley** (09:38:31):
> Hi folks, I’ve got a question that I’ve been unable to find a satisfying answer to:
> Given a fasta file, and a gff, can I generate a gbk file for region subsets? I haven’t been able to find any tools that will do this.

**Spencer Nystrom** (11:05:11) (in thread):
> I think something in the`emboss`toolkit can do this.[https://emboss.sourceforge.net/docs/](https://emboss.sourceforge.net/docs/)

**Spencer Nystrom** (11:05:57) (in thread):
> `seqret`I think can do what you want.

**Spencer Nystrom** (11:06:36) (in thread):
> I'm not familiar with Bioc support for gbk files though.

**Nicholas Cooley** (11:15:51) (in thread):
> Thanks! I found a github repo for a package called`biofiles`that seems to almost work, but it doesn’t appear to be currently supported.

**Spencer Nystrom** (11:30:43) (in thread):
> Yeah, it doesn't help that genbank is notorious for being the worst to parse.

## 2023-01-24

**Jennifer Gerbracht** (08:30:21):
> @Jennifer Gerbracht has joined the channel

**Levi Waldron** (11:06:35):
> I asked the[openai.com](http://openai.com)API playground some questions about Bioconductor using their Q&A endpoint:
> ```
> Human: What do you know about the Bioconductor project for open-source bioinformatics?
> AI: I understand that the Bioconductor project is an open-source software platform for analyzing and visualizing biological data. It provides a comprehensive suite of software for basic and advanced analyses of high-throughput data in genomics and proteomics. It also provides tools for common data-mining tasks.
> Human: What is the Bioconductor project good for?
> AI: The Bioconductor project is best known for its ability to facilitate a wide range of genomic data analysis tasks. It offers tools for gene expression, proteomics, high-throughput screening, sequence analysis, and many more. It is also used to integrate data from diverse sources and visualize it in meaningful ways.
> Human: What are the weaknesses of the Bioconductor project?
> AI: The main weakness of the Bioconductor project is that it can take a lot of time and effort for users to learn how to use all the tools available. Additionally, the project does not provide a unified user interface, so users must be familiar with different programming languages and learn the commands associated with each tool.
> Human: How do you get involved in the Bioconductor project?
> AI: To get involved in the Bioconductor project, you can join their community and take part in discussions. You can contribute to the open-source software by developing new features or fixing bugs. Additionally, you can also participate in workshops and educational events related to the project.
> Human: How has Bioconductor changed over the years?
> AI: Bioconductor has evolved significantly over the years, incorporating more user-friendly features, offering an updated suite of tools, and providing better support for different platforms. It has become an indispensable resource for bioinformatics researchers and is now used in many different fields.
> Human: Why is the Bioconductor project important to science?
> AI: The Bioconductor project is important to science because it provides a comprehensive suite of tools for analyzing and visualizing biological data. It allows researchers to make sense of large amounts of genomic or proteomic data, enabling them to uncover novel insights. It also helps to bridge the gap between wet lab studies and computational research.
> Human: How does the Bioconductor build system work?
> AI: The Bioconductor build system is based on a dependency graph of the various packages. It builds each package by downloading source code, compiling it, and running tests. If the build is successful, it will then generate binaries which can be used to install the package. The system also allows developers to manage the dependencies of their packages.
> Human: What are the most important Bioconductor libraries?
> AI: The most important Bioconductor libraries are the Biobase, BiocGenerics, and BiocParallel packages. These libraries provide essential functions for data analysis, such as importing and manipulating the data, selecting the appropriate statistical tests to use, and running multiple analyses in parallel. Additionally, they also provide essential support for creating visualizations, developing new packages, and managing dependencies.
> ```

**Krithika Bhuvanesh** (11:09:57) (in thread):
> Interesting . I didn’t think it would be so  verbose. Thanks for sharing !

**Sean Davis** (11:29:29) (in thread):
> There is no question the world changed with the introduction of ChatGPT.

**Nitesh Turaga** (11:37:31) (in thread):
> I wonder how it generated this ??
> ```
> Human: What are the weaknesses of the Bioconductor project?
> AI: The main weakness of the Bioconductor project is that it can take a lot of time and effort for users to learn how to use all the tools available. Additionally, the project does not provide a unified user interface, so users must be familiar with different programming languages and learn the commands associated with each tool.
> ```

**Janani Ravi** (13:15:05) (in thread):
> Very cool — and mind blown**again**!:shocked_face_with_exploding_head:

**Hervé Pagès** (17:58:21) (in thread):
> Big fail on the last question though

## 2023-02-23

**Jędrzej Świeżewski** (05:26:37):
> @Jędrzej Świeżewski has joined the channel

**Zarmena Khan** (13:06:20):
> @Zarmena Khan has joined the channel

**Anson** (13:14:36):
> @Anson has joined the channel

**Claire Seibold** (15:48:19):
> @Claire Seibold has joined the channel

**Lambda Moses** (17:55:19):
> Just wondering, I know that Bioconductor counts the number of downloads and number of distinct IP addresses downloading each package each month. Does that include downloads by CIs like GitHub Actions, say used by the package authors themselves or by authors of other packages that depend on those packages?

**Sean Davis** (19:58:21):
> It includes all downloads but not git checkouts and does include instances where the package is a dependency and not a direct install.

**Hervé Pagès** (20:19:45):
> Yes, to be precise, we only count downloads from[https://bioconductor.org/packages/](https://bioconductor.org/packages/), which is where the standard installation method (`BiocManager::install()`) picks Bioconductor packages from. Whether the package was explicitely requested or got downloaded to satisfy a dependency is counted the same way, as 1 download (or 1 hit).

**Lambda Moses** (20:21:03):
> Thanks!

**Lambda Moses** (20:22:36):
> I don’t think anyone is actually doing it. I’m just wondering what if someone artificially increases the package download ranking by automatically downloading the packages by CIs run several times a day.:zany_face:

**Hervé Pagès** (20:28:23):
> That will only increase the "Nb of downloads" but not the "Nb of distinct IPs" (unless they manage to automatically download each time from a distinct IP). We've seen something like this in the past where some package would suddendly get repeatedly downloaded thousands of times per month by some script running somewhere on a cluster (some sys admin probably left a rogue script running unintentionally). This is why we've switched to only consider the "Nb of distinct IPs" for the package ranking. This is a more meaningful/more reliable metrics.

**Lambda Moses** (20:35:29):
> Right, almost forgot. I heard about that happening from a blog post analyzing CRAN package download data. GitHub Actions runners don’t have a fixed IP address and there’re ranges of IP addresses. Hence it’s possible to artificially increase the number of distinct IP addresses downloading a package by repeatedly running a GitHub Action, but that’s limited by the number of IP addresses Microsoft’s data centers have. OK, thanks! Won’t waste more of your time.

## 2023-03-18

**Y-h. Taguchi** (12:02:43):
> @Y-h. Taguchi has joined the channel

**Y-h. Taguchi** (12:16:04):
> There seems to be some problem in Bioconductor Single Package builder,[http://bioconductor.org/spb_reports/TDbasedUFEadv_buildreport_20230318114618.html](http://bioconductor.org/spb_reports/TDbasedUFEadv_buildreport_20230318114618.html)
> ```
> Error in library(testthat) : there is no package called 'testthat'
> there is no package called Biobase
> ```
> I believe that these should not happen,  Actually, Mac in Bioconductor Single Package builder does not report these errors. Is it possible for you to fix? Or have I done something wrong?

**Suhana** (12:24:22):
> @Suhana has joined the channel

**Andres Wokaty** (23:40:55) (in thread):
> We updated R on the devel builders Thursday and Friday, so it usually takes a two runs to get back to normal, but this might be due to a change on our linux builder on Friday to help identify missing dependencies:[https://github.com/Bioconductor/BBS/issues/248](https://github.com/Bioconductor/BBS/issues/248).
  - Attachment: [#248 WISH: Check with `*R_CHECK_SUGGESTS_ONLY*=true` to identify missing package dependencies](https://github.com/Bioconductor/BBS/issues/248)
> (This replaces invalid feature requests [#242](https://github.com/Bioconductor/BBS/issues/242), [#243](https://github.com/Bioconductor/BBS/issues/243), and [#244](https://github.com/Bioconductor/BBS/issues/244).)
> 
> **Background**
> 
> Using `*R_CHECK_SUGGESTS_ONLY*=true` will run `R CMD check` with sandboxed an R library, where on declared package dependencies in `DESCRIPTION` + recursive hard dependencies are included. If an example, package test, or a vignette needs a package that is not in this set of dependencies, it will not be available to be loaded and result in an error.
> 
> **Wish**
> 
> Add `*R_CHECK_SUGGESTS_ONLY*=true` to `Renviron.bioc` to detect missing package dependencies.
> 
> `R CMD check` sets up a sandboxed R library for it's life span. It <https://github.com/wch/r-source/blob/8bf881e5da4da60f3aaad1b1812924817906bb29/src/library/tools/R/check.R#L138-L277|uses symbolic file links to do this>, so the performance overhead for doing this is really small. I could *not* detect a performance penalty (see below for details).
> 
> **Examples**
> 
> I ran the following examples using `Renviron.bioc` for Bioconductor 3.17:
> 
> ```
> wget [https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Bioconductor/BBS/master/3.17/Renviron.bioc](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Bioconductor/BBS/master/3.17/Renviron.bioc)
> ```
> 
> I also ran with:
> 
> ```
> export *R_CHECK_TESTS_NLINES*=17
> ```
> 
> to make it clear what one of the ERRORs is about.
> 
> **Example 1: Package bnem (missing RUnit for tests and BiocStyle for vignettes)**
> 
> Downloading [**bnem**](https://bioconductor.org/packages/devel/bioc/html/bnem.html) package source tarball:
> 
> ```
> $ wget [https://bioconductor.org/packages/devel/bioc/src/contrib/bnem_1.7.0.tar.gz](https://bioconductor.org/packages/devel/bioc/src/contrib/bnem_1.7.0.tar.gz)
> ```
> 
> **Current Bioconductor 3.17 checks**
> 
> When checking with:
> 
> ```
> $ R_CHECK_ENVIRON=Renviron.bioc R --vanilla CMD check bnem_1.7.0.tar.gz
> ```
> 
> package passes with a single NOTE.
> 
> * using log directory ‘/tmp/bioc/bnem.Rcheck’
> * using R Under development (unstable) (2022-11-30 r83391)
> * using platform: x86_64-pc-linux-gnu (64-bit)
> * using session charset: UTF-8
> * checking for file ‘bnem/DESCRIPTION’ ... OK
> * checking extension type ... Package
> * this is package ‘bnem’ version ‘1.7.0’
> * package encoding: UTF-8
> * checking package namespace information ... OK
> * checking package dependencies ... OK
> * checking if this is a source package ... OK
> * checking if there is a namespace ... OK
> * checking for hidden files and directories ... OK
> * checking for portable file names ... OK
> * checking for sufficient/correct file permissions ... OK
> * checking whether package ‘bnem’ can be installed ... OK
> * checking installed package size ... OK
> * checking package directory ... OK
> * checking ‘build’ directory ... OK
> * checking DESCRIPTION meta-information ... OK
> * checking top-level files ... OK
> * checking for left-over files ... OK
> * checking index information ... OK
> * checking package subdirectories ... OK
> * checking R files for non-ASCII characters ... OK
> * checking R files for syntax errors ... OK
> * checking whether the package can be loaded ... OK
> * checking whether the package can be loaded with stated dependencies ... OK
> * checking whether the package can be unloaded cleanly ... OK
> * checking whether the namespace can be loaded with stated dependencies ... OK
> * checking whether the namespace can be unloaded cleanly ... OK
> * checking loading without being on the library search path ... OK
> * checking dependencies in R code ... NOTE
> Namespace in Imports field not imported from: ‘rmarkdown’
>   All declared Imports should be used.
> * checking S3 generic/method consistency ... OK
> * checking replacement functions ... OK
> * checking foreign function calls ... OK
> * checking R code for possible problems ... OK
> * checking Rd files ... OK
> * checking Rd metadata ... OK
> * checking Rd cross-references ... OK
> * checking for missing documentation entries ... OK
> * checking for code/documentation mismatches ... OK
> * checking Rd \usage sections ... OK
> * checking Rd contents ... OK
> * checking for unstated dependencies in examples ... OK
> * checking contents of ‘data’ directory ... OK
> * checking data for non-ASCII characters ... OK
> * checking data for ASCII and uncompressed saves ... OK
> * checking installed files from ‘inst/doc’ ... OK
> * checking files in ‘vignettes’ ... OK
> * checking examples ... OK
> * checking for unstated dependencies in ‘tests’ ... OK
> * checking tests ...
>   Running ‘runTests.R’
>  OK
> * checking for unstated dependencies in vignettes ... OK
> * checking package vignettes in ‘inst/doc’ ... OK
> * checking running R code from vignettes ...
>   ‘bnem.rmd’ using ‘UTF-8’... OK
>  NONE
> * checking re-building of vignette outputs ... OK
> * checking PDF version of manual ... OK
> * DONE
> 
> Status: 1 NOTE  
> See  
> ‘/tmp/bioc/bnem.Rcheck/00check.log’  
> for details.
> **With also `*R_CHECK_SUGGESTS_ONLY*=true`**
> 
> When checking with:
> 
> ```
> $ *R_CHECK_SUGGESTS_ONLY*=true R_CHECK_ENVIRON=Renviron.bioc R --vanilla CMD check bnem_1.7.0.tar.gz
> ```
> 
> the package fails with two ERRORs:
> 
> ```
> * checking tests ...
>   Running ‘runTests.R’
>  ERROR
> Running the tests in ‘tests/runTests.R’ failed.
> Complete output:
>   > BiocGenerics:::testPackage("bnem")
>   Error in library("RUnit", quietly = TRUE) : 
>     there is no package called 'RUnit'
> ```
> 
> and
> 
> ```
> * checking re-building of vignette outputs ... ERROR
> Error(s) in re-building vignettes:
>   ...
> --- re-building ‘bnem.rmd’ using rmarkdown
> Error: processing vignette 'bnem.rmd' failed with diagnostics:
> there is no package called ‘BiocStyle’
> ```
> 
> * using log directory ‘/tmp/bioc2/bnem.Rcheck’
> * using R Under development (unstable) (2022-11-30 r83391)
> * using platform: x86_64-pc-linux-gnu (64-bit)

**Hervé Pagès** (17:05:54) (in thread):
> @Y-h. TaguchiThis should be fixed now. See Lori's announcement on the bioc-devel list:[https://stat.ethz.ch/pipermail/bioc-devel/2023-March/019536.html](https://stat.ethz.ch/pipermail/bioc-devel/2023-March/019536.html)You should be able to trigger a new build for your package by bumping its version.

**Y-h. Taguchi** (19:21:07):
> I know that Core team work hard and it is unrealistic to ask them something more. But I wonder if we can access single package builder during development before we start issue at github of bioconductor. I have found[here](http://bioconductor.org/spb_reports/TDbasedUFEadv_buildreport_20230321182334.html)that***CheckTime***is doubled in Mac compared with other platforms. Since I do not have mac box, only opportunity for me to check how long CheckTime in Mac is only after the submission.   Then I was forced to struggle with shortening CheckTime with including rewriting application completely. It might not be very efficient.

**Vince Carey** (19:38:19):
> @Y-h. Taguchithis is a good question.  I think you would be able to get a handle on performance in the mac domain using github actions.  It is not straightforward to perfectly emulate the bioconductor build system but it would be possible to get a reasonable approximation.  We have a group working on github actions recommendations for bioconductor packages and the slack channel#github-actions.  In the long run we would not want to have individual developers using SPB but would prefer to have valid SPB emulators available.  Have a look at the actions channel and consider using biocthis or rworkflows to build a .github/workflows entry for you.  Let us know how it works out.

## 2023-03-25

**Anne-Maud Ferreira** (00:24:32):
> Hello, is there a place that describes the process to change the email address of a package author or who to contact? The current email address associated with my package will be deleted so I would like to change it to be able to continue maintaining my package. Thank you so much for your help:slightly_smiling_face:Best.

**Lori Shepherd** (09:12:07):
> The important one will be to update your email in the package description and push that up. You should deleting the old and add the new to the bioc-devel mailing list. I think you should be able to update the bioccredentials account itself but let me know if you have issue and the same with the support site.

## 2023-04-01

**Chengyang Ji** (03:50:04) (in thread):
> My guess is the jupyter repl is actually not running on the lowest R environment(since jupyter is a python web app). > I could be totally wrong here.

**Alex Mahmoud** (13:32:52):
> Repost from CZI Open Sci slack in case it is interesting to people::loud_sound:Maybe of interest to folks here, and anyone from the R community!
> At *******10am UTC+1 this Thursday ***,****The Turing Way will be hosting Julia Silge from Posit (formerly RStudio) and Ella Kaye (Warwick University) to hear about their experience developing open source tools and communities with the R project.
> Please sign up via the[Eventbrite page](https://www.eventbrite.co.uk/e/the-turing-way-hosts-julia-silge-and-ella-kaye-tickets-616033842807)if you are interested.
  - Attachment (Eventbrite): [The Turing Way hosts: Julia Silge  and Ella Kaye](https://www.eventbrite.co.uk/e/the-turing-way-hosts-julia-silge-and-ella-kaye-tickets-616033842807)
> The Turing Way welcomes Julia Silge and Ella Kaye to exchange insights from community building with R/Python and Open Source tools.

**Lori Shepherd** (14:14:23):
> Nominations for Bioconductor 2023 Awards are now open.  Bioconductor is excited to announce an opportunity to recognize those making significant outstanding contributions to the Bioconductor community. The Bioconductor project is announcing the call for Bioconductor Awards, honoring various forms of contributions to the project. Four awardees will be selected, each having contributed to the project in an outstanding way based on one or more of selection criteria. More information and details found[https://bioconductor.org/about/awards/](https://bioconductor.org/about/awards/)and the nomination form can be found at[https://forms.gle/gPzmH8ewHNHWckdv6](https://forms.gle/gPzmH8ewHNHWckdv6).  The deadline for nominations is May 31, 2023.  The awardee's will be recognized at the annual Bioc2023 conference August 2-4.
  - Attachment (Google Docs): [Bioconductor Awards 2023](https://forms.gle/gPzmH8ewHNHWckdv6)
> Nomination form for the Bioconductor Awards (see [https://bioconductor.org/about/awards/](https://bioconductor.org/about/awards/) for more details). 
> 
> Please fill out all the fields in the following sections.
> 
> In the Nominator section, please provide your own contact information in case we need to contact you with any question.
> 
> In the Nominee section, please indicate how the nominee provides outstanding contributions for each of the selected nomination criteria. Four awards will be given using the selected criteria as justification.

**Lori Shepherd** (14:18:27) (in thread):
> The webpage for the awards will be updated within the hour to reflect 2023 information.

**Ahmad Alkhan** (07:46:16):
> @Ahmad Alkhan has joined the channel

**Kozo Nishida** (11:58:09):
> Hi all,
> I am interested in [re]implementing some Bioconductor packages in Python, would that be considered a different idea than[https://github.com/BiocPy](https://github.com/BiocPy)?
> (I would like to contribute to BiocPy if the idea is not different.)

**Jayaram Kancherla** (12:12:21) (in thread):
> I would love to have more contributions from the community (need to work on advertising this effort more).
> 
> what packages are you planning on implementing?

**Kozo Nishida** (12:33:06) (in thread):
> There are two that have already been implemented, not planned.
> * [https://github.com/cytoscape/py4cytoscape](https://github.com/cytoscape/py4cytoscape)(that is Python equivalent of[https://www.bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/html/RCy3.html](https://www.bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/html/RCy3.html)
> * [https://github.com/kozo2/pywikipathways](https://github.com/kozo2/pywikipathways)(that is Python equivalent of[https://bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/html/rWikiPathways.html](https://bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/html/rWikiPathways.html))
> (However, py4cytoscape was implemented by a Cytoscape community member who is not me.)
  - Attachment (Bioconductor): [RCy3](https://www.bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/html/RCy3.html)
> Vizualize, analyze and explore networks using Cytoscape via R. Anything you can do using the graphical user interface of Cytoscape, you can now do with a single RCy3 function.
  - Attachment (Bioconductor): [rWikiPathways](https://bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/html/rWikiPathways.html)
> Use this package to interface with the WikiPathways API. It provides programmatic access to WikiPathways content in multiple data and image formats, including official monthly release files and convenient GMT read/write functions.

**Kozo Nishida** (12:34:57) (in thread):
> In addition to the above, I would also like to implement all Cytoscape-related Bioconductor packages in Python as well.

**Kozo Nishida** (12:36:33) (in thread):
> I am very interested in a Python implementation of AnnotationHub in case of non-Cytoscape related packages.

**Kozo Nishida** (12:40:09):
> I wonder if this is related to the above, but how should we understand the difference between rOpenSci and Bioconductor package review?
> I am asking this because I have recently started working with the pyOpenSci community (I am also active in the PyData community).
> I'm wondering if the Python implementation of Bioconductor would now go to pyOpenSci if the BiocPy management policy is not well known.

**Leopoldo Valiente** (12:48:19):
> @Leopoldo Valiente has joined the channel

**Martin Morgan** (12:58:14) (in thread):
> This might be interesting with respect to AnnotationHub --[https://github.com/scverse/genomic-features](https://github.com/scverse/genomic-features)-- a (very much in 'beta') Python package to make EnsemblDb objects from AnnotationHub available to Python users.

**Jayaram Kancherla** (12:58:47) (in thread):
> `AnnotationHub`would be an awesome contribution to the BiocPy org, taking advantage of the data representations already in biocpy.
> 
> In addition, I was considering how to make Bioconductor-[like, ish] packages more discoverable on PyPI. One way of improving the search is by adding`Bioconductor`as a custom classifiers ([https://pypi.org/classifiers/](https://pypi.org/classifiers/)) in PyPI. Haven’t gotten around to submitting this yet, but I will

**Martin Morgan** (13:20:46) (in thread):
> One maybe mistaken reservation I have about BiocPy is that it seems to capture the 'implementation' details, rather than the 'interface' to the object, e.g., for GenomicRanges one only needs two of three of start / end / width, with the specific choice an implementation detail. If all that is available is the implementation, then the Python community has to understand the internal representations and then re-implement the user interface (including all the edge cases accumulated over the years in Bioconductor!)

**Isaac Virshup** (16:39:04) (in thread):
> Hey Kozo, I haven't found the python ecosystem to be anywhere near as centralized to the R one.
> 
> For the cytoscape stuff, I would suggest asking the [biocypher]([https://biocypher.org](https://biocypher.org)) people? They're interested in bio knowledge graphs generally and could point you in the right direction.
> 
> I'd really like to see more reuse of annotation hub in the python ecosystem (I'm working on the`genomic-features`package Martin mentioned, and am involved in scverse), so could be interested in collaborating there.

**Franck RICHARD** (17:10:11):
> @Franck RICHARD has joined the channel


## 2023-05-04

**Hassan Kehinde Ajulo** (11:38:03):
> @Hassan Kehinde Ajulo has joined the channel

**sophy** (13:02:58):
> @sophy has left the channel

**Vince Carey** (15:28:16) (in thread):
> @Isaac Virshupthanks for chiming in.  I am very interested in helping to ensure that scientists can use the language of their choice to do the analyses they want to do.  How to synchronize efforts in Bioc and scverse is a basic concern.  I started trying to understand some of the issues involved by writing scviR which is in Bioc 3.17.  I am sure I am behind the development cycle of scvi-tools, and catching up will take nontrivial effort.  We are also discussing how to enhance AnnotationHub and we wouldn't want to break things that you might be working with in genomic-features.  (I think the risk is quite low but you never know).  I think Bioconductor has benefited greatly by a) having the clearly identified, ~6 month parallel release (no API changes) and devel (can break with deprecation process) cycles, and b) limiting dependencies to bioc and CRAN packages.  These approaches have their costs but I think they have been outweighed by benefits.  Do you see any tendencies towards this kind of centralization/periodicity in scverse?

**Vince Carey** (15:33:29) (in thread):
> @Kozo NishidaI don't know how we should proceed as BiocPy matures.  I will note that we can have vignettes for R packages that use python chunks; in BiocHail I get python/spark via basilisk.  One way to think of Bioc for python is that we think of PyPi as a kind of CRAN, and anyone wanting to provide python capabilities for Bioconductor users would just have to write a basilisk-based package that pins the desired versions of exported python functionality.  A thorough review might require python expertise -- but if there is a review/credentialing/regular testing process underlying the PyPi component, maybe we would only be concerned with the R code defining the interface.

**Vince Carey** (15:49:51) (in thread):
> And maybe we need to start a new channel?  "BiocPython"?

**Vince Carey** (15:52:51) (in thread):
> Just one more point.  Wanted to mention the alabaster.* packages as tools for converting Bioc data structures like SingleCellExperiment to JSON+HDF5 (e.g.) for ingestion in other languages/systems.  Getting experience with the metadata and construction of nicely usable instances in other languages will be worthwhile.  alabaster.ranges for GRanges, for example.

**Theary Chhim** (19:03:50):
> @Theary Chhim has joined the channel


## 2023-05-05

**Kozo Nishida** (00:34:05) (in thread):
> Thanks for the info.@Vince CareyI understand your thoughts on BiocPy.
> Thanks for the info on BiocHail. I am a hail user but did not know BiocHail.
> I will try to use basilisk.

**Kozo Nishida** (00:35:18) (in thread):
> I started a new channel#biocpython.

**Isaac Virshup** (07:47:57) (in thread):
> @Vince Carey
> > We are also discussing how to enhance AnnotationHub and we wouldn't want to break things that you might be working with in genomic-features.
> Would be happy to be a part of this conversation! I talked about this a little bit with@Martin Morganat the recent[scverse hackathon](https://twitter.com/scverse_team/status/1653418406450798595?s=20). It could be useful to include people from`open2c`in this as well depending on which area of annotationhub.
  - Attachment (Twitter): [scverse on Twitter](https://twitter.com/scverse_team/status/1653418406450798595?s=20)
> Many exciting things – and intense coding – at the scverse hackathon this week hosted by @OliverStegle and @saezlab in Heidelberg!
>  
> Our focus was on interoperability across languages and knowledgebases. Read on to see what we made! 1/n

**Isaac Virshup** (07:49:28) (in thread):
> > I think Bioconductor has benefited greatly by a) having the clearly identified, ~6 month parallel release (no API changes) and devel (can break with deprecation process) cycles
> This has been a thing we've discussed a bunch, which I'll try to summarize here. It's also something that we touched on in the scverse announcement publication ([rdcu.be/c9Bx6](https://rdcu.be/c9Bx6)) and during the review process with Wolfgang.
> 
> I don't think we'll go for big parallel releases. We definitely don't have the resources to arrange this for the whole community at the moment.
> 
> What we are doing/ going to do is:
> * Make release candidates for packages with many dependents (e.g. scverse core)
> * Make it easy for downstream packages to have a CI job that tests against these (e.g.[this CI job](https://github.com/scverse/cookiecutter-scverse/blob/6a4338030e0c83a9034d607ed66abeff83e18840/%7B%7Bcookiecutter.project_name%7D%7D/.github/workflows/test.yaml#L30)(which needs better documentation)). We would like to monitor these for failure after making a release candidate, but are still figuring that out.
> 
>   * We may add this as requirement to be listed as a "scverse ecosystem package" ([https://scverse.org/packages/#ecosystem](https://scverse.org/packages/#ecosystem))
> The core packages also follow semantic versioning w.r.t. API changes. I'd encourage downstream packages to do the same, but we're not taking steps to enforce that.
> > b) limiting dependencies to bioc and CRAN packages.
> Does this essentially mean: "no installing dependencies from github"? Or is it more than that?
> > Do you see any tendencies towards this kind of centralization/periodicity in scverse?
> Periodicity: Not on the same scale. We would like to have more regular releases of tools, but are still figuring out greater development resources first. Even then, it'd be a goal, not necessarily a requirement.
> 
> Centralization: Yes, but with a lighter touch. Essentially, we have restrictions on packages that we'll advertise/ give a badge to. But we don't want to go so far as having our own package distribution.

**Vince Carey** (08:07:25) (in thread):
> Right -- we do not permit dependence on github-only packages, and if a package is removed from CRAN the dependent package needs to adjust or motivate the CRAN contributor to fix to get it back into CRAN.  Since your ecosystem requirements include PyPi availability, it may not be such a stretch to enlarge the scope of Bioconductor software contributions to explicitly support interop with scverse via basilisk.  But further discussion will be needed.

**Vince Carey** (08:10:42) (in thread):
> @Alex Mahmoud@Brian Schilderthere is a link above to a[github action](https://github.com/scverse/cookiecutter-scverse/blob/6a4338030e0c83a9034d607ed66abeff83e18840/%7B%7Bcookiecutter.project_name%7D%7D/.github/workflows/test.yaml#L30)for CI in scverse.  Seems like it connects with the concerns we have in the cloud working group/rworkflows...

**Brian Schilder** (08:53:48) (in thread):
> will give some thoughts on the new#biocpythonchannel

Even though not explicit in the advertisement, application to tumour biology are possible (especially given the focus of many labs in the institute). He's welcome to contact me directly.
  - Attachment (~/): [Open position in the CBIO lab: spatial proteomics and PTMs](https://lgatto.github.io/spatprot-job-2023/)
> A position for a PhD student (4 years) or a research assistant (1 yearcontracts, renewable) is open in the CBIO lab focusing on proteinsub-cellular localisation (spatial) proteomics, post-translationalmodification and machine learning.
  - Attachment (~/): [Open position in the CBIO lab: single-cell proteomics](https://lgatto.github.io/scp-job-2023/)
> A fully-funded position for a PhD student (4 years) or a post-doctoralresearcher (2 - 3 years, depending on experience) is open in theComputational Biology and Bioinformatics lab (CBIO) at the de DuveInstitute, UCLouvain in Brussels, Belgium.

## 2023-06-27

**Louis Le Nézet** (04:52:55):
> Hi everyone,
> i don't really know where to post this question, so feel free to redirect me to the correct channel:slightly_smiling_face:I was wondering how is the S3 and S4 methods supported in bioconductor and how to nicely write them.
> In my case, I'm writing different functions that either take a dataframe and a list of columns names or a list of vector.
> The aim would be to do the same as with`boxplot`.
> Should I write something like the following:
> ```
> test <- function(x, ...){
>   UseMethod("test")
> }
> 
> test.default <- function(x, x1, x2, ...){
>   print("Is default with multiple vector")
> }
> 
> test.data.frame <- function(x, colnames, ...){
>   print("Is data.frame")
>   print(colnames)
> }
> ```

**Lluís Revilla** (05:23:20) (in thread):
> Bioconductor doesn't have a special support for S3 and S4 methods than R. You can use other books like[https://adv-r.hadley.nz/s4.html](https://adv-r.hadley.nz/s4.html)to learn how to write them. The special case for Bioconductor is that whenever possible you should reuse classes to benefit from their methods. But this is a nice question for the Bioconductor working group[https://github.com/Bioconductor/BiocClassesWorkingGroup](https://github.com/Bioconductor/BiocClassesWorkingGroup)(I'll log it so that we can answer them in future materials)

**Asier Ortega** (06:16:21):
> @Asier Ortega has joined the channel

**Yoon Ji Jung** (06:58:02):
> @Yoon Ji Jung has joined the channel

**Louis Le Nézet** (07:13:37) (in thread):
> Thanks for the answer:slightly_smiling_face:

**Marjani Hall** (11:09:28):
> @Marjani Hall has joined the channel

**Laurent Gatto** (11:52:20) (in thread):
> And following up from Lluís comment, most of the methods are S4, because the Bioconductor classes are exclusively S4. The important point is thus the data that you are handling and want to write a method for.

**Hervé Pagès** (13:59:17) (in thread):
> Yes, Bioconductor recommends defining an S4 generic and methods for this. No need for an S3 generic and methods. So:
> ```
> setGeneric("foo", function(x, ...) standardGeneric("foo"))
> 
> setMethod("foo", "ANY",
>     function(x, ...) {
>         all_vectors <- list(x, ...)
>         stopifnot(all(sapply(all_vectors, is.vector)))
>         ...
>         ...
>     }
> )
> 
> setMethod("foo", "data.frame",
>     function(x, colnames) {
>         ...
>         ...
>     }
> )
> ```

## 2023-07-10

**Lluís Revilla** (10:01:29):
> I'm interested in the[FEM package](https://bioconductor.org/packages/3.10/bioc/html/FEM.html)removed in 3.12. According to[git.bioconductor.org](http://git.bioconductor.org)I see it listed`R packages/FEM`but when I try to clone it or see it via the[code browser](https://code.bioconductor.org/browse/)it doesn't appear. I could download the source code, but while checking the package I was surprised to see that it still has[downloads](https://bioconductor.org/packages/stats/bioc/FEM/)as of this year.  Are the downloads numbers across all versions (Bioconductor & package) but without the mirrors?

**Helge Hecht** (22:41:18):
> There will be a mass spectrometry BoF at the Galaxy Community Conference - meeting details are in the following document:[https://docs.google.com/document/d/1G_k4xtvaNkXkVOFiN22vahx_iryNeRaBj5JaeZuclmI/edit](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1G_k4xtvaNkXkVOFiN22vahx_iryNeRaBj5JaeZuclmI/edit)If you'd like to learn about the state of MS tools in Galaxy or would like to learn more about the activities this meeting is open for everyone to join and express their interest!


## 2023-07-11

**Akewak Jeba** (02:19:37):
> @Akewak Jeba has joined the channel

**Harvinder Singh** (02:26:13):
> @Harvinder Singh has joined the channel

**Mike Smith** (10:56:11) (in thread):
> Don't take this as gospel, but I'll try to answer the bits I know.
> 
> AFAIK a deprecated package is never actually removed from the git server.  In fact, if you try this command you can still clone`FEM`:
> ```
> git clone[https://git.bioconductor.org/packages/FEM.git](https://git.bioconductor.org/packages/FEM.git)
> ```
> When a package is removed from Bioconductor, it's actually removed from the "Manifest", the master list(s) of all packages in the current devel branch.  (I think) these drive the build system, package propagation, landing package creation etc.  You can actually download and look at the manifest files yourself, they're also in a git repository:
> ```
> git clone[https://git.bioconductor.org/admin/manifest.git](https://git.bioconductor.org/admin/manifest.git)
> ```
> I use the software package list to determine what's available in the code browser.  If a package get's deprecated and removed from the manifest, it disappears from the code browser.  That's probably not ideal, but when I was developing the site the manifest seemed a good way to easily obtain a comprehensive list of packages and to know when a new package was added to Bioc that I needed to add to the browser.
> 
> I presume the list of packages in a particular release is just whatever is in the Manifest on the date of the release freeze - but I don't actually know that for sure.  There's not any`RELEASE_X_Y`branches in the manifest repo that pinpoint those moments, but there are commits where multiple packages are removed around past release times.
> 
> I don't know much about the download counts, but I suspect you're correct that the download counts are across all versions of the packages, and`FEM`still gets a small number of download for the old versions.  Maybe@Robert Shearknows more, I believe he's spent some time looking at the download statistics and might know how the counts are aggregated.

**Lluís Revilla** (12:13:19) (in thread):
> Thanks Mike, I am not sure what happened, because when I tried `git clone ... ` yesterday it didn't clone the repo. Many thanks for maintaining the[code.bioconductor.org](http://code.bioconductor.org)site and a glimpse behind the curtains on how it works.

**Robert Shear** (12:13:41) (in thread):
> It appears as though FEM was removed in the 3.12 release, so the last published release is 3.11. You can access it at[http://www.bioconductor.org/packages/3.11/bioc/html/FEM.html](http://www.bioconductor.org/packages/3.11/bioc/html/FEM.html).  If you want to see all the packages in 3.12, you can visit[http://bioconductor.org/packages/3.12/](http://bioconductor.org/packages/3.12/).
  - Attachment (Bioconductor): [FEM](http://www.bioconductor.org/packages/3.11/bioc/html/FEM.html)
> The FEM package performs a systems-level integrative analysis of DNA methylation and gene expression data. It seeks modules of functionally related genes which exhibit differential promoter DNA methylation and differential expression, where an inverse association between promoter DNA methylation and gene expression is assumed. For full details, see Jiao et al Bioinformatics 2014.

**Lluís Revilla** (12:14:18) (in thread):
> I was surprised because it is removed and it has almost the same amount of downloads as one of my packages (I was thinking this could be used to control for "background"/automatic  downloads of Bioconductor). Thanks!

**Lluís Revilla** (12:15:31) (in thread):
> That's what I did@Robert Shear! A publication used FEM and I wanted to avoid rewriting it from scratch. Thanks!

**Robert Shear** (12:16:26) (in thread):
> All of the packages accessed via[www.bioconductor.org](http://www.bioconductor.org)or[bioconductor.org](http://bioconductor.org)are in the download count. As you suspect, nothing from the mirrors is counted. Access via[git.bioconductor.org](http://git.bioconductor.org)is not counted.

**Caterina** (15:34:49):
> @Caterina has joined the channel

**Amos Schorr** (17:13:09):
> Hey folks! If you’re curious about what the near future of bio looks like in the wetlab, boy do I have a treat for you! We’ve got 4 leaders talking IN A REALLY GROUNDED WAY about what these changes might be and what you might want to think about to best equip yourself to do goddamn brilliant work on July 12th. Free for student & academics. Pay what you wish for everyone else.[https://www.eventbrite.com/e/near-future-skills-gaps-14-the-wetlab-tickets-639557713337](https://www.eventbrite.com/e/near-future-skills-gaps-14-the-wetlab-tickets-639557713337)
  - Attachment (Eventbrite): [Near Future Skills Gaps (1/4): The Wetlab](https://www.eventbrite.com/e/near-future-skills-gaps-14-the-wetlab-tickets-639557713337)
> A virtual panel of leaders in aging biology discussing neglected topics central to doing good work & building a career.

## 2023-07-21

**Amos Schorr** (05:04:41):
> Hi! We’ve got 2 virtual events coming up that I’d love you to join and share with good folks you know. They’re focused on near-future skill gaps in aging biology research. On July 26th, we’ll be discussing CompBio with Jacob Kimmel (co-founder @ NewLimit), Ravi Pandya (Sr. Director, ML & Computational Platform @ Altos Labs), and Jarod Rutledge (co-founder @ Teal Omics, a Wyss-Coray lab spinout). And on August 9th, we’ll be discussing software & ML with Joe Betts-LaCroix (CEO @ Retro Bio), Luca Naef (CTO, VantAI), and our very own @Andy Lee  (CTO, Vincere Biosciences).[https://www.eventbrite.com/e/near-future-skill-gaps-24-computational-biology-tickets-665910445067](https://www.eventbrite.com/e/near-future-skill-gaps-24-computational-biology-tickets-665910445067)[https://www.eventbrite.com/e/near-future-skill-gaps-34-software-ml-tickets-677946495217](https://www.eventbrite.com/e/near-future-skill-gaps-34-software-ml-tickets-677946495217)
  - Attachment (Eventbrite): [Near Future Skill Gaps (2/4): Computational Biology](https://www.eventbrite.com/e/near-future-skill-gaps-24-computational-biology-tickets-665910445067)
> A virtual panel of leaders in aging biology discussing neglected topics central to doing good work & building a career.
  - Attachment (Eventbrite): [Near Future Skill Gaps (3/4): Software & ML](https://www.eventbrite.com/e/near-future-skill-gaps-34-software-ml-tickets-677946495217)
> A virtual panel of leaders in aging biology discussing neglected topics central to doing good work & building a career.

## 2023-08-05

**Abdullah Al Nahid** (10:49:31):
> Hi everyone, I created a simple chrome extension to access PDF files directly, to avoid waiting for this screen (screenshot attached) to load.
> It will inject a floating PDF icon in the bottom right corner of your screen when you visit such a paper with epdf like in ASM, Willey.
> 
> Here's the downloadable zip file and the source code of the extension that you can unzip and load into your browser:[https://github.com/nahid18/journal-epdf-downloader](https://github.com/nahid18/journal-epdf-downloader)Feel free to star the repo if you like it
  - File (PNG): [image.png](https://community-bioc.slack.com/files/U34P8RS3B/F05LG8T21SP/image.png)

## 2023-08-10

**Dirk Eddelbuettel** (14:06:25):
> My google-fu is failing me. I had glanced at, and quite enjoyed, JJ's keynote at this year's BioC Conf about quarto, manuscripts and all that jazz and have now tried twice to share them but ... can no longer find it. Is there a public repo with his slides including the short embedded screen capture videos at the end?

**Hervé Pagès** (15:12:04) (in thread):
> Don't know about the slides but the video of his presentation is available for replay on Hopin at[https://app.hopin.com/events/bioc2023/replay](https://app.hopin.com/events/bioc2023/replay)


## 2023-08-11

**Sarah Parker** (12:36:23) (in thread):
> Here are the slides! He used a quarto presentation--[https://jjallaire.quarto.pub/reproducible-manuscripts-with-quarto/#/title-slide](https://jjallaire.quarto.pub/reproducible-manuscripts-with-quarto/#/title-slide)You can find all his quarto pubs at[https://jjallaire.quarto.pub/](https://jjallaire.quarto.pub/)

**Dirk Eddelbuettel** (12:37:55) (in thread):
> My google-fu and memory are clearly bad because I once knew that too, of course.  Silly me looked for his GH repos and found nuttin and was perplexed.  Quarto domain it is, of course.  Thanks a million!

**rohitsatyam102** (15:45:36):
> Hi Every. Can anyone here tell if there is an R package for calculating dN/dS or pN/pS based on SNPEff annotated VCF and reference FASTA and GTF file. I am aware that there is a package called dNdScv but it is highly biased toward using annotation from Ensembl and idk how to calculate CDS start and End per transcript from GTF file.

**Amanda Hiser** (16:41:24):
> @Amanda Hiser has left the channel

**Abdullah Al Nahid** (21:27:43):
> @Abdullah Al Nahid has left the channel

**Hervé Pagès** (22:53:09) (in thread):
> > idk how to calculate CDS start and End per transcript from GTF file.
> Import the file as a TxDb object with`GenomicFeatures::makeTxDbFromGFF()`, then do (assuming`txdb`is your TxDb object):
> ```
> tx <- transcripts(txdb)
> 
> ## Add 'cds_start' and 'cds_end' metadata columns:
> 
> tx_lens <- transcriptLengths(txdb, with.cds_len=TRUE,
>                                    with.utr5_len=TRUE,
>                                    with.utr3_len=TRUE)
> # sanity check
> stopifnot(identical(tx_lens$tx_id, mcols(tx)$tx_id))  
> 
> cds_start <- start(tx) +
>              ifelse(strand(tx) == "+", tx_lens$utr5_len, tx_lens$utr3_len)
> cds_end   <- end(tx)   -
>              ifelse(strand(tx) == "+", tx_lens$utr3_len, tx_lens$utr5_len)
> 
> no_cds_idx <- which(tx_lens$cds_len == 0L)
> cds_start[no_cds_idx] <- NA
> cds_end[no_cds_idx]   <- NA
> 
> mcols(tx)$cds_start <- cds_start
> mcols(tx)$cds_end   <- cds_end
> ```
> This is what`tx`looks like with the 2 additional metadata cols:
> ```
> > tx
> GRanges object with 29173 ranges and 4 metadata columns:
>            seqnames        ranges strand |     tx_id     tx_name cds_start
>               <Rle>     <IRanges>  <Rle> | <integer> <character> <integer>
>       [1]     chr2L     7529-9484      + |         1 FBtr0300689      7680
>       [2]     chr2L     7529-9484      + |         2 FBtr0300690      7680
>       [3]     chr2L     7529-9484      + |         3 FBtr0330654      7680
>       [4]     chr2L   21952-24237      + |         4 FBtr0309810      <NA>
>       [5]     chr2L   66584-71390      + |         5 FBtr0306539     67116
>       ...       ...           ...    ... .       ...         ...       ...
>   [29169]   chrYHet 319739-320997      - |     29169 FBtr0114244      <NA>
>   [29170]   chrYHet 327052-328489      - |     29170 FBtr0114245      <NA>
>   [29171] chrUextra 523024-523048      - |     29171 FBtr0330363      <NA>
>   [29172] chrUextra 523024-523086      - |     29172 FBtr0330361      <NA>
>   [29173] chrUextra 523060-523086      - |     29173 FBtr0330362      <NA>
>             cds_end
>           <integer>
>       [1]      8610
>       [2]      9276
>       [3]      8610
>       [4]      <NA>
>       [5]     70895
>       ...       ...
>   [29169]      <NA>
>   [29170]      <NA>
>   [29171]      <NA>
> ```
> I wish there was a simpler way though. I'll think about adding something to***GenomicFeatures***.
> Note that this is the kind of question you would typically ask on our support site.

**Daniel Niiaziiev** (07:07:42):

**Jenea Adams** (09:39:43):
> Hi there! We're ([BWCB](http://blackwomencompbio.org)) on a mission to capture >= 100 responses from computational biologists in our first CompBio Perspectives Survey. We'd like to understand perceptions of resources and opportunity from computational biologists globally. It takes just ~2 minutes to complete and you can find it[here](https://forms.gle/D8h9FyJ1eX6YYsq56). Feel free to also pass along to others in your network.
  - Attachment (Google Docs): [CompBio Perspectives Survey](https://forms.gle/D8h9FyJ1eX6YYsq56)
> WHAT IS THIS SURVEY?
> We're on a mission to learn from at least 100 computational biologists' professional experiences.
> 
> This is a brief survey The Black Women in Computational Biology Network (BWCB) is deploying to understand the distribution of resources and support across our diverse scientist community. 
> 
> This survey should take about 2 minutes. 
> 
> WHO SHOULD TAKE THIS SURVEY?
> All computational biologists are welcome to complete this survey! If you're not already registered as a BWCB supporter, you'll be directed to do so at the end of this survey if you wish. However, prior BWCB affiliation or knowledge is not a prerequisite for this survey. 
> 
> WHO SHOULD NOT TAKE THIS SURVEY?
> :exclamation:If you're an active BWCB member (Black women in compbio) do NOT complete this survey! Please check your email inbox for the link to your personalized survey or email <mailto:info@blackowmencompbio.org|info@blackowmencompbio.org> if you can't find it :exclamation:
> 
> HOW WILL THIS INFORMATION BE USED?
> Your answers to the experience and demographic sections will be anonymized and analyzed in aggregate. This information will provide valuable insights into the challenges and opportunities faced by individuals in the computational biology field. The data will be used to inform strategies for improving inclusivity, diversity, and support within the computational biology field.
> Rest assured, your responses are completely anonymous. No personally identifiable information will be linked to your survey data.
> As we strive to capture the richness of experiences, we may include individual quotes in the final report. However, these quotes will be anonymized, and any identifying details will be removed to ensure confidentiality.
> Your participation is instrumental in shaping the future of computational biology, and we appreciate your candid and thoughtful responses.
> Feel free to share with other computational biologists in your network. 
> 
> Questions? email us <mailto:info@blackwomencompbio.org|info@blackwomencompbio.org>

## 2024-01-24

**Devon Kohler** (08:52:16):
> Dear friends and colleagues:
> 
> The May Institute on Computation and Statistics for Mass Spectrometry and Proteomics, taking place on April 29 – May 10, 2024 on the campus of Northeastern University in Boston MA, is now accepting applications. The application deadline is February 15, 2024.
> 
> Instructors are leading experts, who contributed numerous experimental and computational methods and software. The target audiences are both beginners and experienced scientists, with both experimental and computational expertise.
> 
> Participants can select a subset of the following programs:
> 
> * April 29 - May 1, Quantitative proteomics with Skyline. Lead instructors: Tina Ludwig, Brendan MacLean and Lindsay Pino
> * May 1 – May 3, Beginners R and beginners statistics. Lead instructor: Ryan Benz
> * May 1 – May 3, Intermediate R, data visualization and statistics. Lead instructor: Kylie Bemis
> * May 6 – May 8, Statistics for quantitative mass spectrometry: methods and case studies with MSstats. Lead instructors: Olga Vitek and Devon Kohler
> * May 6, Analysis of mass spectrometry images with Cardinal. Lead instructor: Kylie Bemis
> * May 7, Modern software development practice with Python. Lead instructor: Charles Tapley Hoyt
> * May 8, Scientific writing. Lead instructor: Alicia Williams
> * May 8 – May 10, Interpretation of proteomic experiments in the context of biomolecular networks with INDRA. Lead instructor: Ben Gyori
> * May 10, Barnett Institute 50th Anniversary and Karger Medal Celebration. Featured speakers: Jenny Van Eyk and Nikolai Slavov
> 
> A limited number of registration fee wavers will be available for students and postdocs affiliated with academic or nonprofit institutions in the US.
> 
> Please share this widely. More information is at[https://computationalproteomics.khoury.northeastern.edu/](https://computationalproteomics.khoury.northeastern.edu/)

## 2024-02-02

**Alan O'C** (11:21:18):
> Has anybody here submitted + revised an F1000 paper? We submitted a latex document originally, and in our post-review revisions are now being asked to track changes their docx with the revised version, which would take... far too long... and be completely useless, in terms of references/figures/basically everything. Is it worthwhile trying to convince them to take the sensible path?

**Lena** (11:29:15) (in thread):
> they don't let you track changes using[latexdiff](https://ctan.org/pkg/latexdiff?lang=en)?

**Alan O'C** (11:30:08) (in thread):
> The direct quote (after I said we previously submitted a latex doc) was
> > Unfortunately, we do require all version submissions to be made on the word doc of the published article

**Alan O'C** (11:30:47) (in thread):
> But thanks! I'll suggest that as an alternative

**Lena** (11:31:03) (in thread):
> sorry, I have no experience with them:confused:it does sound unreasonable

**Alan O'C** (11:33:50) (in thread):
> Wondering if@Aaron Lunremembers if he went through this for v2 of[https://f1000research.com/articles/5-2122/v2](https://f1000research.com/articles/5-2122/v2), if so I am more impressed by this than any amount of research or software output since
  - Attachment (f1000research.com): [F1000Research Article: A step-by-step workflow for low-level analysis of single-cell RNA-seq data with Bioconductor.](https://f1000research.com/articles/5-2122/v2)
> Read the latest article version by Aaron T.L. Lun, Davis J. McCarthy, John C. Marioni, at F1000Research.

**Aaron Lun** (11:48:46) (in thread):
> My submission was pure latex IIRC, no docx involved.

**Kasper D. Hansen** (12:03:51) (in thread):
> In my understanding this has changed substantially because F1000Res internal workflow has changed

**Kasper D. Hansen** (12:04:36) (in thread):
> It used to be all latex. I have no recent experience, but you're not the first one mentioning word and that obviously makes it a somewhat less appealing publishing venue

**Alan O'C** (12:09:30) (in thread):
> Thanks all. Yes, there's no way I would've submitted knowing this in advance. For reproducible manuscripts it makes zero sense
> 
> Also should mention the conversion introduced syntax errors first time around so it's not even working as intended

## 2024-02-07

**Lluís Revilla** (06:46:10):
> I'm having trouble posting to[support.bioconductor.org](http://support.bioconductor.org). I think I saw a message about it somewhere ( a channel or the mailing list) but I cannot find it or post my question but I see other people posting. Is it experiencing some problems?

**Lluís Revilla** (06:47:12) (in thread):
> I either get a problem with the tags (sometimes it tells me that I need to place a comma to create a tag, other's just nothing) or a problem with the text of my question (without any information about what is wrong).

**Vince Carey** (07:19:46) (in thread):
> Sorry@Lluís Revillawe have made some changes to deal with spam and there are going to be some bumps.  Will be back in touch.

**Vince Carey** (07:25:24) (in thread):
> have you tried clearing browser cache and perhaps using incognito mode?  i had no problem with a new post with fresh auth via google.

**Lori Shepherd** (07:36:30) (in thread):
> its not a spam issue its a special character issue most likely if you are copying and pasting text from terminal.  we lightened the constraints except for a validity check on ascii characters; we haven't been able to narrow down what browswer or OS is the root cause but that is likely the cause. Also tags are required there are generic tags and all the packages show up as suggestions but we do require a tag.  The default can be Bioconductor

**Lluís Revilla** (07:40:56) (in thread):
> I tried different browsers but it might be a character issue. A warning or message of what is wrong would be helpful to know what to fix...
> The problem with tags was not that I didn't use them but that the tags I added aren't allowed somehow, even if they appear in the menu when I start writing it.

**Lluís Revilla** (07:42:04) (in thread):
> My OS is ubuntu 22.04 on Firefox 122.0.1 or on Chromium Version 121.0.6167.139 (Official Build) snap (64-bit)

**Lori Shepherd** (07:44:25) (in thread):
> You should still be able to use any free form tag you want but you still need to add at least one tag suggested/approved - if it was not having to do with a package,  "Bioconductor" is the catch all,  there is also "release" , "devel" , I think we  added things like  "conferences" , etc...

**Lluís Revilla** (07:54:19) (in thread):
> I desist to post the question here. I upgraded the computer, restarted, cleaned the cache and used several browsers the tags are now valid (at the third try) but no clue what/how to fix the text.
> For some more context I copied the output of reprex...

## 2024-02-13

**Maria Doyle** (08:25:05) (in thread):
> @Lluís Revillawere you able to post at support site? I’m having a similar issue trying to post the message in screenshot below on behalf of CoC committee. It won’t post and I can’t see what the issue is. I’m on Mac and have tried Chrome and Firefox (freshly installed). Can anyone tell what may be wrong?
  - File (PNG): [Screenshot 2024-02-13 at 13.22.36.png](https://community-bioc.slack.com/files/U34P8RS3B/F06JJNS6F6F/screenshot_2024-02-13_at_13.22.36.png)

**Lori Shepherd** (08:27:22) (in thread):
> can you send me your text so I can try to reproduce

**Maria Doyle** (08:28:50) (in thread):
> yes below, thanks for taking a look
> > Hello Bioconductor Community
> > 
> > It is important to reflect often on the importance of upholding [Bioconductor values][1], our commitment to an open approach to science, collaboration, diversity, inclusivity, and a welcoming environment is at the core of our community.
> > 
> > The Bioconductor Code of Conduct serves as a guiding light, ensuring that we maintain a supportive space for all. Let’s continue working together to nurture an environment that encourages the exchange of ideas and fosters collective growth. Everybody can reach the [Code of Conduct committee][2] if you consider that some situation doesn’t reflect these values.
> > 
> > Thank you for your dedication to the Bioconductor community.
> > 
> > 
> >   [1]:[https://bioconductor.github.io/bioc_coc_multilingual/en-US.html](https://bioconductor.github.io/bioc_coc_multilingual/en-US.html)[2]:[https://bioconductor.github.io/bioc_coc_multilingual/](https://bioconductor.github.io/bioc_coc_multilingual/)

**Lori Shepherd** (08:31:25) (in thread):
> Its the issue with special characters --- when I paste into a text editor,  the apostrophe for Let's and doesn't  ends up as non ascii characters.  And we have a validity check that checks for this.

**Lori Shepherd** (08:31:43) (in thread):
> Let�~~@~Ys    and doesn�~~@~Yt

**Lori Shepherd** (08:39:30) (in thread):
> so i can look into a solution -- were you copying and pasting that from somewhere else or typing directly into the support site block?

**Maria Doyle** (08:41:24) (in thread):
> thaks for find that! I copied and pasted from the message I was sent in Slack

**Lluís Revilla** (10:48:20) (in thread):
> I was not able to post to the support site (or Biostars), there is another question in the support site with the same problem. It might be an encoding issue but it would be nice if it would report that to the user so that we can fix and post

**Lori Shepherd** (11:20:12) (in thread):
> yes looking into if its possible to have an informative message when the encoding validation is triggered

**Jenea Adams** (13:51:17):
> Hello all, I have two announcements from[BWCB](http://blackwomencompbio.org):
> * On February 28th, 2024 we are hosting a public townhall centering community-building around exploring opportunities for allyship in the computational biology community. We will begin by sharing some insights from our survey results capturing the experiences of various communities within the computational biology field. Many of you here participated in that survey, and we appreciate your input! We welcome you to join us for a conversation that will create space to engage critically together on what we're learning.
> 
>   * RSVP here:[https://www.blackwomencompbio.org/event-details/townhall-on-opportunities-for-allyship-in-computational-biology](https://www.blackwomencompbio.org/event-details/townhall-on-opportunities-for-allyship-in-computational-biology)
> 
> * We have just released applications for our 2024[grant programs](https://www.blackwomencompbio.org/grants). If you work with colleagues or trainees who could benefit from becoming a member applying, I appreciate you sharing this with them. Applications close on March 1, 2024.
  - Attachment (BlackWomenCompBio): [Townhall on Opportunities for Allyship in Computational Biology | BlackWomenCompBio](https://www.blackwomencompbio.org/event-details/townhall-on-opportunities-for-allyship-in-computational-biology)
> All in the STEM community are welcome to join us for flash talks an open discussion on the the current state of opportunities for allyship in the field of computational biology. We will also introduce the BWCB Connect program, an initiative to spark connection and networking for all in the field.
  - File (PNG): [2.png](https://community-bioc.slack.com/files/U34P8RS3B/F06JZQHHS65/2.png)

**Hervé Pagès** (19:27:44) (in thread):
> This apostrophe is UTF-8. UTF-8 characters are very common, especially in R output (e.g. fancy quotes). Any chance the support site can accept them? The pre-rendering accepts them so it's kind of unexpected that when I press "Save" my answer (which looked fine in the preview) just disappears and seems to be lost forever (I might have spent a lot of time writing it:sweat:).

## 2024-02-15

**David Rach** (09:55:37):
> What is your favorite/most-useful R:books:book? I am on a reading binge this year,  currently on the last  couple chapters of Advanced R, and looking for additional book suggestions:book::worm:

**Jacques SERIZAY** (13:07:05) (in thread):
> Not as well organized as Hadley's books, but[Computational Genomics with R](https://compgenomr.github.io/book/)from Altuna Akalin covers most aspects of bioinfo analysis for bulk assays. It has not been updated for quite some time though, and it doesn't cover at all the tidyomics principles:scream:.
  - Attachment (compgenomr.github.io): [Computational Genomics with R](https://compgenomr.github.io/book/)
> A guide to computationa genomics using R. The book covers fundemental topics with practical examples for an interdisciplinery audience

**Jacques SERIZAY** (13:16:16) (in thread):
> While this thread is open, any suggestion for***python***package development? Package dev approaches in python are a lot more diverse than in R. Any suggestion for a good, clear guide for package dev (not coding)?

**Martin Morgan** (15:51:03) (in thread):
> Since you're at the end of r4ds, I'd suggest 'The Art of R Programming' from Norman Matloff. Most of the book is kind of an anti-r4ds, emphasizing an understanding of the language foundations. Chapters 9  (object-oriented programming) and 13+ (debugging, performance, interfacing to other languages, parallel) are pretty unique ('dated' if looked at now, 'visionary' if at the time of writing). Maybe especially interesting (and challenging 'exercises for the reader') are when Matloff says that something is one way (`for`loops scale quadratically, so are slow for big data,`1:100000`allocates a lot of memory) but now`for`loops are not particularly slow, and`1:100000`actually occupies very little space. What changed in**R**? What lessons can we as (advanced)**R**programmers learn?

**Lluís Revilla** (18:11:50) (in thread):
> For python package development I recommend to create an R package and wrap it with rpy2:wink:. For R you can read everything on the Big Book of R:[https://www.bigbookofr.com/](https://www.bigbookofr.com/)I think there are more than 200 books.
  - Attachment (bigbookofr.com): [Big Book of R](https://www.bigbookofr.com/)
> 400+ Free R programming books

**Jayaram Kancherla** (18:32:44) (in thread):
> @Jacques SERIZAYwe put together a guide on how we develop packages in biocpy -[https://github.com/BiocPy/developer_guide](https://github.com/BiocPy/developer_guide)

**Charlie George** (05:22:01):
> Hi everyone, I've been involved in a project[www.x-net.bio](http://www.x-net.bio)to research interdisciplinary career research barriers in the UK and propose recommendations to address them, much of our research focused on those at the intersection of biomedical science and computation/statistics such as yourselves. We've just launched our series of 13 recommendations to UK funders which hope to support and insentise a more collaborative and supportive research culture for all. You can see the tweetorial summarising the efforts here[https://x.com/CGATist/status/1764926690126401926?s=20](https://x.com/CGATist/status/1764926690126401926?s=20)please retweet so it reaches as many colleagues as possible. Many thanks:slightly_smiling_face:
  - Attachment (X (formerly Twitter)): [Chris Ponting :blue_heart: (@CGATist) on X](https://x.com/CGATist/status/1764926690126401926?s=20)
> NEWS:tada: Today X-Net launched recommendations to @UKRI_News to tackle barriers for #InterdisciplinaryResearch careers
> :point_down:we explain how these were developed with researchers, public & industry
> ​[https://t.co/Plrs5pyTIZ](https://t.co/Plrs5pyTIZ)
> Collaboration among @mrc_hgu @MRC_WIMM @mrcppu @KavliOxford 1/12

**Aishat Ijiyode** (05:23:24):
> @Aishat Ijiyode has joined the channel

**Precious Orakwe** (13:09:32):
> please can someone guide me on how to start contributing

**Aleru Divine** (13:11:45) (in thread):
> Hi@Precious Orakweplease join the#bugsigdbchannel

**Marcel Ramos Pérez** (13:12:20) (in thread):
> Hi Precious, you can join the#outreachychannel for more guidance. Also of relevance:[https://community-bioc.slack.com/archives/C35G93GJH/p1709746183127649](https://community-bioc.slack.com/archives/C35G93GJH/p1709746183127649)
  - Attachment: [Attachment](https://community-bioc.slack.com/archives/C35G93GJH/p1709746183127649)
> Welcome Outreachy applicants! Please join #bugsigdb for Microbiome Study Curation (BugSigDB) discussions. We also have an Outreachy-specific channel #outreachy.  Project mentors are on those channels and we can better support you there. This channel #general alerts the entire community so it's better to do introductions on #outreachy. Thank you and good luck!

> rOpenSci March news! #RStats:handshake:Leadership changes! Read Karthik farewell post, and Noam post about his new role:globe_with_meridians:Dev Guide 0.9.0: Multilingual Now! And Better:microphone:Yani Interview with Code for Thought podcast:date:Upcoming coworking sessions: Quarto in April with Pao Corrales, "Meet our new Executive Director" in May.:notes:New packages {nuts}, {quadkeyr}, {weatherOz}:package:Package news:spiral_note_pad:Many blog posts:wave:Calls for contributions:hammer_and_wrench:Package Development corner[https://ropensci.org/blog/2024/03/29/ropensci-news-march-2024/](https://ropensci.org/blog/2024/03/29/ropensci-news-march-2024/)
  - Attachment (ropensci.org): [rOpenSci News Digest, March 2024](https://ropensci.org/blog/2024/03/29/ropensci-news-march-2024/)

## 2024-04-12

**Felipe ten Caten** (13:22:59):
> @Felipe ten Caten has joined the channel

**Yanina Bellini Saibene** (14:50:10):
> From a Researcher (Ashley Juavinett):
> > We are conducting a study to understand professional experiences in computing within the context of biology.:computer::herb::microscope:If you are in a biology-related field AND use some amount of computing (e.g. writing code) in your current role, indicate interest in a 1-hour interview with $25 compensation here:[https://forms.gle/YXozM6Wx2ZwmruVp8](https://forms.gle/YXozM6Wx2ZwmruVp8)The study is open undergrad/grad students, postdocs, faculty, & folks in industry.
> > Please share!:pray:
  - Attachment (Google Docs): [Sign-Up Form: Computing Paths in Biology Interview](https://forms.gle/YXozM6Wx2ZwmruVp8)
> Thank you for your interest in participating in an interview to help us learn more about computing paths in biology! Please fill out the following information to indicate your interest. If selected to participate, Austin Zuckerman (Ph.D. student working with Ashley Juavinett) will reach out to you to officially schedule a time and date! The interview will last approximately one hour and you will receive a $25 gift card for participating.


## 2024-04-13

**Yanina Bellini Saibene** (14:49:06):

## 2024-04-16

**Siraj Farhan** (15:19:03):
> Hey all!
> 
> I'm the founder of***Synthia Labs***, excited to share our AI agent (like ChatGPT but for Bioinformatics) we've been developing to***accelerate bioinformatics tool development***. It features capabilities like***direct editing & executing of scripts***(Python, R, bash),***installing packages***,***handling large datasets***and***integrating custom Docker images***Check out our***3-min demo***here:[https://www.loom.com/share/e94083ed1c354dc1abd32e46c68c3624](https://www.loom.com/share/e94083ed1c354dc1abd32e46c68c3624)We're offering***early access***! Please fill out this***quick form***to register your interest:[https://forms.gle/b2rV4AnPyGRACMVB6](https://forms.gle/b2rV4AnPyGRACMVB6)Thanks for helping us advance bioinformatics!
  - Attachment (Loom): [Synthia | An AI Scientist](https://www.loom.com/share/e94083ed1c354dc1abd32e46c68c3624)
  - Attachment (Google Docs): [AI in Bioinformatics](https://forms.gle/b2rV4AnPyGRACMVB6)
> Your input will help us understand the current use of language models in bioinformatics and identify the key challenges faced by the community. 
> Thank you for your valuable insights and support in advancing AI in bioinformatics!
> The Synthia Labs Team

## 2024-04-24

**Izabela Mamede** (08:26:36):
> Hello Bioconductor community,
> I'm interested in exploring RNA modifications from nanopore data. Could anyone recommend tools and packages specifically designed for this purpose? Are any inside BioConductor? Additionally, I'm curious if there are any streamlined workflows available for this analysis, or if any research teams are actively working on developing them. Any insights or recommendations would be greatly appreciated. Thank you!

**Lance Zhang** (09:35:09):
> @Lance Zhang has joined the channel

**Changqing** (19:52:12) (in thread):
> NanoMethViz?


## 2024-04-25

**Nan Deng** (01:20:57):
> @Nan Deng has joined the channel

**Izabela Mamede** (07:08:49) (in thread):
> When I used this for the last time it worked perfect for DNA data, not so much for RNA (good for m6a not m5c etc), and only for methylation. But thanks for the suggestion, I will check if they made any new releases.

**AbuZar Khan** (07:52:13):
> @AbuZar Khan has joined the channel

**Peter Hickey** (21:07:37) (in thread):
> @Shian Su^^^

**Shian Su** (21:24:33) (in thread):
> This is something I'd like to support, but it's a bit difficult without test data. In theory NanoMethViz works for any kind of modification mapped to a reference. In practice the plots are calibrated for m5c in DNA. I think Nanopore's ModKit is probably your best bet for some exploratory analysis.
> 
> If you could describe any specific issues you're encountering I am happy to try and help. I'm personally working on some m6a C.elegans DNA data right now and the data is simultaneously too dense (too many A sites compared to CG motifs I've calibrated to) and too sparse (the prevalence of methylation is generally <5%).


## 2024-04-26

**Joel Nitta** (01:19:35):
> @Joel Nitta has joined the channel

**Martin Emons** (06:41:51):
> @Martin Emons has joined the channel

**Izabela Mamede** (08:30:17) (in thread):
> I am trying ModKit right now! For now I have only one test data of RNA of chikungunya infection in human cells, the ideia would  be to see what i can get from it and if it is worth it to sequence more samples/other infections. I really appreciate your suggestions, I am having a similar problem, since NanoMethViz is not fully calibrated for other mods/ or RNA.

**Stevie Pederson** (10:12:26) (in thread):
> OMG!!! Their system literally removes all line breaks from your code!!! Their solution is to ask you to re-submit each code chunk as an individual, standalone Word document so they can see the formatting, despite having all of the code correctly formatted (& tested) in the original submission. Thanks for warning me@Alan O'Cand I very much wish I'd listened. Although I must admit, no-one in their right mind would've imagined a system that does what they're doing
