## 2024-05-28

**Jenny Drnevich** (11:28:01):
> For anyone with experience with NIH's biosketch format - the instructions explicitly say we can cite research products like educational curricula or software, but "Use of hyperlinks and URLs to cite these items is not allowed.". How in the world are we supposed to cite things like this that solely exist on line and are not published?! Even more confusing is the link they give to the FAQ about how to cite interim research projects in the proper format says to use a DOI and gives a URL in their example! I thought people here would likely have software that fits in this category and could share how they cite it in NIH's biosketch.[https://grants.nih.gov/grants/how-to-apply-application-guide/forms-h/general/g.240-r&r-seniorkey-person-profile-(expanded)-form.htm#Instructions](https://grants.nih.gov/grants/how-to-apply-application-guide/forms-h/general/g.240-r&r-seniorkey-person-profile-(expanded)-form.htm#Instructions)

**Marouen Ben Guebila** (11:37:06) (in thread):
> Hey Jenny, I think it is ok to use URLs in the new Biosketch format. Hyperlinks however are not allowed because they may be used to trace back reviewers. ([https://netzoo.github.io](https://netzoo.github.io)vs https://netzoo.gitub.io)

**Jenny Drnevich** (11:51:48) (in thread):
> I could see why they don't want embedded hyperlinks but the instructions also say no URLs. This is the first time my biosketch has gotten flagged and I've had them in for years, so I wondered if this was a new requirement?

**Marouen Ben Guebila** (12:03:42) (in thread):
> Oh this seems new then, I’ve gotten away with URLs without hyperlinks in the past, so they may be enforcing it now.

**Frederick Tan** (12:04:42) (in thread):
> Comments in this post suggest there is some enforcement variability between institutes
> * [https://www.nia.nih.gov/research/blog/2021/04/missing-links-why-nih-doesnt-allow-hyperlinks-grant-applications](https://www.nia.nih.gov/research/blog/2021/04/missing-links-why-nih-doesnt-allow-hyperlinks-grant-applications)

**Jenny Drnevich** (12:07:08) (in thread):
> Thanks, Marouen and Fred!

**Jenny Drnevich** (12:14:24) (in thread):
> And of course there is conflicting information from NIH about this! "***Do***use hyperlinks in relevant citations and publications included in biosketches and publication list attachments"[https://nexus.od.nih.gov/all/2019/05/13/the-dos-donts-of-hyperlinks-in-grant-applications/](https://nexus.od.nih.gov/all/2019/05/13/the-dos-donts-of-hyperlinks-in-grant-applications/)
  - Attachment (nexus.od.nih.gov): [The Do’s & Don’ts of Hyperlinks in Grant Applications](https://nexus.od.nih.gov/all/2019/05/13/the-dos-donts-of-hyperlinks-in-grant-applications/)
> It would be hard to read more than a couple paragraphs on the internet these days without encountering a hyperlink to a definition or additional clarifying information. Hyperlinks are everywhere. So, why does NIH limit the use of hyperlinks in grant applications?

**Jenny Drnevich** (08:22:13) (in thread):
> Update: turns out my Grants & Contracts just required me to take out embedded links but plain text to the URL was fine. > :star2:***Join Us at the Scverse Conference 2024!***:star2::spiral_calendar_pad:***Dates***: September 10-12, 2024:round_pushpin:***Location:***Munich, Germany:memo:***Details & Registration***:[Scverse Conference 2024](https://scverse.org/conference2024)Scverse is hosting its first conference in Munich. It will hosts talks, poster sessions and workshop on open source software for single cell and spatial genomics.***Speakers:***
> * ***Fabian Theis***- Helmholtz Munich
> * ***Alex Wolf***- Lamin
> * ***Angela Pisco***- Insitro
> * ***Rob Patro***- University of Maryland
> * ***Christina Leslie***- MSKCC
> * ***Maria Brbic***- EPFL
> :earth_africa:***Travel Grants Available***: Need support to attend? We're offering travel grants. Don’t let anything hold you back from this opportunity!:rocket:***Call for Participation***: We’re still accepting abstract submissions and workshop proposals! Contribute your ideas and engage with peers and leaders in the field.:link:***Submit Your Ideas***:[Abstracts & Workshops](https://scverse.org/conference2024/call-for-proposals)Mark your calendars and get ready for an enlightening experience. We’re excited to see you there!:loudspeaker:***Spread the Word***: Help us make Scverse Conference 2024 a milestone event by sharing this announcement with your colleagues and across your networks!
> 
> #scverse #CompBio #OpenSource

**Jared Andrews** (17:19:52):
> The***KIDS24 BioHackathon***signup is now OPEN!:date:***Event Date:***September 4-6, 2024:round_pushpin:***Location:***St. Jude Children's Research Hospital - Memphis, TN:computer:***Participation:***In-person and Virtual
> Join us for three days of intensive coding full of innovation, collaboration, and fun! Whether you're attending in person or joining us virtually, this is your chance to make a difference and showcase your skills. Don't miss out on this incredible opportunity to be part of something impactful.***Register now and secure your spot!***[Register Here](https://cvent.me/E4mybl)If you have any questions please email[biohackathon@stjude.org](mailto:biohackathon@stjude.org)We can't wait to see you there!:rocket:I am involved in planning this event at St. Jude, and we're looking for enthusiastic folks to take a look. Come help build something cool and useful, see what St. Jude is about, and learn some new skills! Feel free to post here or DM me with any questions.
> 
> Students and trainees are very welcome, so if anyone in your group/institution may be interested, please pass it along! Limited travel awards will be available.

**Danielle Callan** (11:48:47):
> I'd like to share something I'm excited about:
> 
> The[MicrobiomeDB](https://microbiomedb.org/mbio/app)team recently developed a suite of R packages to accompany the website (if you aren't familiar, check it out!). It supports the same types of analyses (alpha diversity, beta diversity, differential abundance, correlations, etc) as the site, via an API thats designed to be user-friendly and question-centric. Its built on a custom data container that is flexible enough to support a wide variety of data, while performant enough to also serve as the backbone of the site. You can import your own data from TreeSummarizedExperiments (and thanks to the great folks building mia, various other formats as well) or work with our curated data.
> 
> Find more details here:[https://microbiomedb.github.io/MicrobiomeDB/](https://microbiomedb.github.io/MicrobiomeDB/)We'd love feedback! So don't hesitate to reach out. There is a[Discord Server](https://discord.gg/rHty9PRED7), as well, if anyone were interested.

## 2024-07-01

**Steffen Neumann** (07:05:25):
> Hi, we have a not-really BioC question, more R basics: We develop a package which depends on another one. Duh.
> The dependency is[https://github.com/PecanProject/pecan/tree/develop/modules/rtm](https://github.com/PecanProject/pecan/tree/develop/modules/rtm)but also on[https://pecanproject.r-universe.dev/PEcAnRTM](https://pecanproject.r-universe.dev/PEcAnRTM)Our github actions fail because it can't resolve this package.
> We tried `Remotes: PEcAnRTM=github::PecanProject/pecan/modules/rtm/.` (without the trailing dot you get a 404, now we get`deps::.: ! pkgdepends resolution error for deps::..`Note that the directory name != package name).
> While you can `install.packages('PEcAnRTM', repos = '[https://pecanproject.r-universe.dev](https://pecanproject.r-universe.dev)')`, the`DESCRIPTION/Additional_repositories`is only supported in R CMD check, not when actually installing.

**Kerry** (10:06:23) (in thread):
> this is github package issue 404 issue with the credentials.
> usethis::create_github_token()
> 
> I tried to install the PEcAnRTM and failed to compile, so I think there may be an issue with the package

**Kerry** (10:13:59) (in thread):
> devtools::install_github("PecanProject/pecan", subdir="modules/rtm", ref='develop',auth_token = "addintokenhere")
> 
> however, I'm getting the zero-exit status as I'm missing two dependencies ERROR: dependencies ‘PEcAn.logger’, ‘PEcAn.assim.batch’ are not available for package ‘PEcAnRTM’

**Joyce Qiu** (10:55:43):
> @Joyce Qiu has joined the channel

**Steffen Neumann** (11:03:30) (in thread):

## 2024-08-16

**Camila Pereira Perico** (19:13:20):
> Hello bioconductor community.
> I have a question regarding the versions submitted to bioconductor.
> I submitted a version of my package via git. Shortly afterwards, I added some changes and submitted a new version.
> The old version had a 'check' error and I received an e-mail informing me of the error. Unfortunately, I can't upload the fixes for the old version and I keep getting emails informing me of the error.
> 
> Would it be worth contacting bioconductor to remove the old version? Could you help me?

**Lori Shepherd** (19:52:46) (in thread):
> You should be able to update with the newer version of the code to Bioconductor. If you have trouble pushing to git we can assist.   Keep in mind for changes to take place you need a valid version bump.  Also keep in mind there are two active branches at all times, release and devel.  Is it possible you only updated one branch and are still getting emails for the other branch?

**Camila Pereira Perico** (20:01:44) (in thread):
> Hi Lori, thank you very much for your reply.
> I don't know if this is the problem. The package is rSWeeP. The release version is 1.16.0. The faulty version is 1.16.1 and the latest version (devel) that is working correctly is 1.17.2.
> I'm just worried that I'm still getting notifications about this old version, because version 1.17.2 has been checked and is OK.
> Do I need to take any action?

**Lori Shepherd** (20:35:56) (in thread):
> You should push changes to fix release to the RELEASE_3_19 branch and bump to 1.16.2.

**Camila Pereira Perico** (20:49:38) (in thread):
> Got it. Thank you so much for your help Lori.

**Lori Shepherd** (20:51:15) (in thread):
> Let us know if you have any other questions or concerns.:grin:

## 2024-09-02

**Laurent Gatto** (03:20:21):
> The package landing pages have a 'In Bioconductor since' field that gives the years since a package is in Bioc and it's fifst Bioc and R versions. I can get all sorts of information about a package with biocViews package, but apparently not this one.   Does anyone know of the  'In Bioconductor since' is available programmatically, or is it somehow derived from the version (and if so, how).

**Laurent Gatto** (03:32:37) (in thread):
> BiocPkgTools doesn't have this information either, as far as I can see.

**Laurent Gatto** (03:35:22) (in thread):
> Progress: I can extract the first download stats (which should reflect month/year of first release) from`BiocPkgTools::biocDownloadStats()`

**Laurent Gatto** (04:45:42) (in thread):
> Although these only go until 2009.

**Lori Shepherd** (06:24:36) (in thread):
> I can look tomorrow since today's the holiday but yes it's programmatically.  I think it's a cross from when it's in the manifest and the release dates. Which is why I think at some point we can only do a year+ from when we started using manifests.  I'll look officially tomorrow tho

**Laurent Gatto** (06:49:05) (in thread):
> Thank you

**Lluís Revilla** (10:57:24) (in thread):
> The download include the devel branch. If a package is accepted after a new Bioconductor version it might have more months than being published. Maybe you could check the commit date of version changes in the major.minor


## 2024-09-03

**Lori Shepherd** (09:00:39) (in thread):
> confirmed it does a cross check against the release dates and manifests

**Lori Shepherd** (16:31:13):
> Bioconductor 3.20 is released! Thanks to all developers and community members for contributing to the project! Please see full release announcement:[https://bioconductor.org/news/bioc_3_20_release/](https://bioconductor.org/news/bioc_3_20_release/)
  - Attachment (bioconductor.org): [Bioconductor - Bioconductor 3.20 Released](https://bioconductor.org/news/bioc_3_20_release/)
> The Bioconductor project aims to develop and share open source software for precise and repeatable analysis of biological data. We foster an inclusive and collaborative community of developers and data scientists.

**Sean Davis** (16:26:23):
> A little off-topic, but here is a set of medical-student-curated AI in medicine resources that my students chose to engage with (self-directed learning) during our AI in medicine course. It includes ethics, legal, clinical, research, technical topics in the form of videos, papers, podcasts, blog posts, etc. Particularly for clinical colleagues asking you about AI, this list (though long) has been through at least one medical student's hands.[https://bit.ly/ai-med-resources](https://bit.ly/ai-med-resources)
  - Attachment (Google Docs): [Contributed AI Learning Resources](https://bit.ly/ai-med-resources)

**Yanina Bellini Saibene** (07:06:21):
> Nice to close the year accomplishing objectives :-):sunglasses::grin:Very proud of this guide that we generated in rOpenSci: everything we developed and learned during the translations and localizations of our content is detailed in this book: the processes, the roles, and the technical tools.
> 
> We hope it will be helpful to other groups and communities!:writing_hand:Blog post By[Yanina Bellini Saibene](https://ropensci.org/author/yanina-bellini-saibene/),[Maëlle Salmon](https://ropensci.org/author/ma%C3%ABlle-salmon/),[Pao Corrales](https://ropensci.org/author/pao-corrales/),[Elio Campitelli](https://ropensci.org/author/elio-campitelli/)– Edited by[Steffi LaZerte](https://ropensci.org/author/steffi-lazerte/):[https://ropensci.org/blog/2024/12/17/localization-guide/](https://ropensci.org/blog/2024/12/17/localization-guide/):toolbox:rOpenSci Localization and Translation Guidelines. Why and how we localize and translate our resources:[https://translationguide.ropensci.org](https://translationguide.ropensci.org)Both content are in English and Spanish.  Feedback is appreciated.:slightly_smiling_face:

**Maria Doyle** (07:17:48) (in thread):
> Congrats on the guide, Yani! Thanks for sharing – it looks like a great resource. It might also be worth posting in#documentation-translation!

**Yanina Bellini Saibene** (07:20:52) (in thread):
> Thanks@Maria Doyle!  Shared there too:slightly_smiling_face:

## 2025-02-06

**Ouma Ronald** (01:58:14) (in thread):
> hi@Yanina Bellini Saibene. Is this also another opensource community under microbiome?  Just wanted to know.

**Yanina Bellini Saibene** (03:35:14) (in thread):
> Hi Ouma, Idon’tthink so, what microbiome is?

**Yanina Bellini Saibene** (03:37:28) (in thread):
> We are fiscal sponsored by NumFOCUS if that helps with your question

**Ouma Ronald** (03:39:38) (in thread):
> I read the message but didn't get it right and that's why I asked if its another community but under community-bioc

**Maria Doyle** (05:42:59) (in thread):
> Hi Ouma,
> Just to clarify, rOpenSci is a separate open-source community focused on general R packages. You can learn more about them at[https://ropensci.org/](https://ropensci.org/). Bioconductor focuses on bioinformatics and computational biology, including microbiome research. Both communities share a commitment to open science and open source.
> Hope this helps clear things up!
  - Attachment (ropensci.org): [rOpenSci](https://ropensci.org/)
> Open Tools and R Packages for Open Science

## 2025-03-02

**Sean Davis** (07:10:16):
> Over the last 48 hours, critical biomedical data infrastructure such as pubmed, NCBI, and some NIH institutional sites have been either down or only intermittently functional. I put together a status page here:[https://stats.uptimerobot.com/Zrqh8AhvKn](https://stats.uptimerobot.com/Zrqh8AhvKn)to track a few of these resources. I can add up to 40 more sites. Let me know (in a thread, please) if you have additional US government sites that are critical to your work that you want to track. And, of course, feel free to share with colleagues and politicians for visibility and awareness.

**Izabela Mamede** (09:24:03) (in thread):
> Hey! Thanks for the resource, it is very useful. Could you add blast[https://blast.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov](https://blast.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov)?

**Vince Carey** (10:10:15) (in thread):
> the uptimerobot system may be inaccurate concerning[reporter.nih.gov](http://reporter.nih.gov)... which is slow but seems to be up at this time

**Sean Davis** (10:12:06) (in thread):
> Thx,@Vince Carey. I adjusted the timeout.

**Martin Morgan** (12:29:17) (in thread):
> I noticed that[https://ccr.cancer.gov](https://ccr.cancer.gov)is tagged as 'forbidden', but is accessible via my browser. Also, I wonder if the time zone could be changed to EST (or dynamic based on origin of request) to reflect the US basis of the results. And perhaps there are pages to be linked to where planned / known service interruptions are provided for some of these sites?

**Godfrey Wagutu** (13:10:21):
> @Godfrey Wagutu has joined the channel

**isoah gracious** (13:29:09):
> @isoah gracious has joined the channel

**Sean Davis** (13:34:59) (in thread):
> I've gotten a TON of interest from folks about this coffee-time project. The infrastructure is actually a commercial service, so I don't have much control over it. Given the interest, urgency, and importance, I'll probably put in a little time to work on a more configurable, open version.

**Nithya kruthi** (18:56:52):
> @Nithya kruthi has joined the channel

**Sean Davis** (12:55:44):
> The Cold Spring Harbor Lab course,[https://meetings.cshl.edu/courses.aspx?course=C-DATA](https://meetings.cshl.edu/courses.aspx?course=C-DATA), has extended the deadline for application to March 15. There is a cost associated with the course, but there are funds for financial hardship or need available.
  - Attachment (meetings.cshl.edu): [Statistical Analysis of Genome Scale Data](https://meetings.cshl.edu/courses.aspx?course=C-DATA)
> Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory Meetings & Courses -- a private, non-profit institution with research programs in cancer, neuroscience, plant biology, genomics, bioinformatics.

**Julien Roux** (01:28:38):
> The abstract call for the BC2 conference (Basel, Switzerland, 8-10 Sept) is closing tomorrow, March 6th. There are quite some sessions of this bioinformatics-oriented conference that could be a good fit for people here![https://www.bc2.ch/scientific-programme](https://www.bc2.ch/scientific-programme)
  - Attachment (bc2.ch): [Scientific programme](https://www.bc2.ch/scientific-programme)
> The [BC]2 Basel Computational Biology Conference is Switzerland's main event in the domain of Computational Biology and one of the major events of its kind in Europe. The conference unites scientists from academia, industry and healthcare.

**Sean Davis** (06:22:36):

**Ludwig Geistlinger** (08:40:20) (in thread):
> re: Variant Effect Prediction tools and databases. Would we want to add[AlphaMissense](https://zenodo.org/records/8208688)+[AlphaMissenseR](https://www.bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/html/AlphaMissenseR.html)? Can open a PR.
  - Attachment (Zenodo): [Predictions for AlphaMissense](https://zenodo.org/records/8208688)
> This repository provide AlphaMissense predictions. Please see the README for more details. For questions about AlphaMissense or the prediction Database please email <mailto:alphamissense@google.com|alphamissense@google.com>.
  - Attachment (Bioconductor): [AlphaMissenseR](https://www.bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/html/AlphaMissenseR.html)

**Kate Penrose** (09:29:59):
> @Kate Penrose has joined the channel

**Sean Davis** (10:45:21) (in thread):
> Awesome!

**Sean Davis** (10:48:42) (in thread):
> Thanks, all, for the feedback in realtime!

**Sean Davis** (10:49:58) (in thread):
> The NIH folks reached out to let me know that this was not a planned event. I didn't get more details nor did I ask for them.

**Sean Davis** (10:52:02) (in thread):
> A positive result was the increased visibility and news coverage of some of these critical resources. Hopefully, it was a singular blip.

**Robert Castelo** (13:11:25) (in thread):
> You may also want to add the Bioconductor package[GenomicScores](https://bioconductor.org/packages/GenomicScores)that provides access to different types of genomic scores including conservation, minor allele frequencies or pathogeniticy, such as CADD or AlphaMissense scores (see section 7 of its[vignette](https://bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/vignettes/GenomicScores/inst/doc/GenomicScores.html#7_Retrieval_of_multiple_scores_per_genomic_position)).
  - Attachment (Bioconductor): [GenomicScores](https://bioconductor.org/packages/GenomicScores)
> Provide infrastructure to store and access genomewide position-specific scores within R and Bioconductor.

> :mega:The call for the[rOpenSci Champions Program 2025](https://ropenscilabs.github.io/ChampionsProgram/)in Spanish edition is open. In this blogspot, you have all the details to participate::writing_hand:ESP:[https://ropensci.org/es/blog/2025/03/10/campeones-america-latina/](https://ropensci.org/es/blog/2025/03/10/campeones-america-latina/):memo:ENG[https://ropensci.org/blog/2025/03/10/champeons-latin-america/](https://ropensci.org/blog/2025/03/10/champeons-latin-america/)We have also scheduled an event with champions and mentors who will share their experiences in previous cohorts for Wednesday, 12/03.:video_camera:[https://ropensci.org/es/commcalls/champions-latino-2025/](https://ropensci.org/es/commcalls/champions-latino-2025/)Share this info with your community!!

> * [Español, LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/ropensci_rse-datosabiertos-softwarelibre-activity-7304839748714479616-FT-y?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAAAH31g8B4liq53DhzTsRVsft6OZXuLCUeOw).

> * [Español, BlueSky](https://bsky.app/profile/ropensci.org/post/3ljzku6oaajy2)
  - Attachment (es.linkedin.com): [#rse #datosabiertos #softwarelibre #cienciaabierta #cienciadedatos | rOpenSci](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/ropensci_rse-datosabiertos-softwarelibre-activity-7304839748714479616-FT-y?utm_source=share&amp;utm_medium=member_desktop&amp;rcm=ACoAAAH31g8B4liq53DhzTsRVsft6OZXuLCUeOw)

**Yanina Bellini Saibene** (09:41:04):
> rOpenSci March news!:envelope_with_arrow:#RStats:mega:rOpenSci Champions Program 2025 In Spanish: Apply before April 30th!:tada:New rOpenSci Champion(a|e)s Program cohort:rocket:Better documentation for R-universe!:handshake:rOpenSci Participation at NumFOCUS’ DISC Unconf 2025:video_camera:Community Call “Fomentando la Ciencia Abierta en América Latina - Programa de rOpenSci” Resources:spiral_calendar_pad:Coworking:world_map:New packages {pangoling}, {mbquartR}, {mapmetadata}, {geotargets}, {saperlipopette}:sparkles:3 use cases, of {osmextract} and {babelquarto}:memo:Blog posts:raised_back_of_hand:Calls for contributions:toolbox:Package development corner[https://ropensci.org/blog/2025/03/27/news-march-2025/](https://ropensci.org/blog/2025/03/27/news-march-2025/)
  - Attachment (ropensci.org): [rOpenSci News Digest, March 2025](https://ropensci.org/blog/2025/03/27/news-march-2025/)
> Call for

**Claire Rioualen** (09:56:10) (in thread):
> Hi! Not sure about internal organisation, but if you're interested in data management and deposition:
> * In France there is a national repository for data deposition, and "[thematic reference centres](https://recherche.data.gouv.fr/en/category/3/guide/how-to-choose-a-data-repository)" that can help or orientate people depending on their research Focus
> * In Europe there are also some useful resources provided or recomended by the ELIXIR network, such as the[Research Data Management community](https://elixir-europe.org/communities/research-data-management), a list of[databases for data deposition](https://elixir-europe.org/platforms/data/elixir-deposition-databases), or maybe the[Data Stewardship Wizard](https://ds-wizard.org/)resources
  - Attachment (recherche.data.gouv.fr): [Recherche Data Gouv](https://recherche.data.gouv.fr/en/category/3/guide/how-to-choose-a-data-repository)
> To share or open up research data, these need to be deposited in a trustworthy data repository that promotes the FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable,...
  - Attachment (elixir-europe.org): [ELIXIR Deposition Databases for Biomolecular Data | ELIXIR](https://elixir-europe.org/platforms/data/elixir-deposition-databases)
> ELIXIR has compiled a list of resources that it recommends for the deposition of experimental data. The scientific community has a shared responsibility to ensure long-term data preservation and accessibility. The purpose of this Deposition Databases list is to provide guidance to those who formulate policy and working practices about the appropriate repositories for publishing open data in the life sciences.
  - Attachment (ds-wizard.org): [Data Stewardship Wizard](https://ds-wizard.org/)
> Leading open-source platform for collaborative and living data management plans trusted worldwide from data management pioneers to international research institutes.

**Lori Shepherd** (15:21:24):
> Bioconductor 3.21 is released!
> Thanks to all developers and community members for contributing to the project!
> Please see the full release announcement:[https://bioconductor.org/news/bioc_3_21_release/](https://bioconductor.org/news/bioc_3_21_release/)
  - Attachment (bioconductor.org): [Bioconductor - Bioconductor 3.21 Released](https://bioconductor.org/news/bioc_3_21_release/)
> The Bioconductor project aims to develop and share open source software for precise and repeatable analysis of biological data. We foster an inclusive and collaborative community of developers and data scientists.
